actions.title: Ending Radical and Wasteful Government DEI Programs and Preferencing
actions.status: Active - Partial Injunction
actions.description:
Executive Order 14151 directs all agencies to terminate DEI/DEIA offices, positions, programs, grants, and contracts within 60 days. Revokes Biden equity orders (EO 13985, 14031, 14045, 14049, 14091, 14096).

NONPROFIT IMPACT: Over 1,000 nonprofits rewrote mission statements to comply. DEI-focused grants terminated. Organizations with diversity missions face federal funding exclusion. Grantee certification requirements chill nonprofit advocacy.

IMPLEMENTATION: NEA requires certification against "gender ideology" and DEI in grants. Smithsonian closed diversity office. Kennedy Center eliminated Social Impact team. NMAAHC, NMAI, Latino Museum programming affected.

SMITHSONIAN GOVERNANCE CONFLICT: The Smithsonian is a trust instrumentality of the United States, organizationally separate from all three branches of government (CRS IF12718). The President is not a member of the Board of Regents and has no appointment or removal authority over the Secretary or museum directors. EO 14151's directive to close Smithsonian DEI offices operates in contested legal territory — the President lacks clear statutory authority to direct Smithsonian internal operations. The Smithsonian employs both federal employees (appropriation-funded, subject to DOGE reduction authority) and 'trust employees' (trust-funded, NOT federal employees under employment law). The reported 47 Smithsonian diversity officers terminated may include trust-funded positions not lawfully subject to executive termination authority. Congressional appropriations comprise ~66% of Smithsonian expenditures — the remaining ~34% from trust funds is not subject to executive impoundment, providing a meaningful but limited buffer against compliance pressure.

ECONOMIC IMPACT: This order disrupts the arts and culture sector that represents 4.4% of U.S. GDP — approximately $1.02 trillion in annual economic activity (BEA). The sector grew 13.7% in 2021 vs. 5.9% overall GDP, demonstrating its role as an economic resilience engine. By targeting NEA, NEH, Smithsonian, and cultural institutions through DEI certification requirements, EO 14151 constricts a sector that Congress explicitly sustained through emergency appropriations: $75M (CARES Act to NEA, 855 organizations), $130M (ARPA to NEA, ~700 organizations), and $16.25B (SVOG, 13,000+ venues). The Shuttered Venue Operators Grant alone disbursed ~$14.6 billion — demonstrating how seriously Congress took this sector's economic role. Arts and culture production is economically independent of complex supply chains and generates downstream activity in publishing, printing, broadcasting, and manufacturing, meaning DEI-based funding restrictions produce compounding economic harm beyond the arts sector itself.

TRIBAL DATA INFRASTRUCTURE THREAT: EO 14151's DEI elimination mandate extends to OMB — the same agency that sets the standardized definitions for racial and ethnic categories used by the Census Bureau to identify and count AI/AN populations. OMB currently defines American Indian or Alaska Native as 'individuals with origins in any of the original peoples of North, Central, and South America.' Any revision to this definition through DEI-driven rulemaking would directly affect how 9,666,058 AI/AN-identifying people (2020 Census) are counted for federal funding and service allocation. This threat compounds a pre-existing crisis: no comprehensive federal biennial tribal population report has been published since 2013, despite congressional mandate (P.L. 115-93). The elimination of tribal liaison officers and DEI-labeled consultation staff removes the personnel who facilitate confidential data sharing between tribes and federal agencies — degrading the data infrastructure through which treaty funding obligations are calculated.

FOREIGN POLICY DEI ELIMINATION: EO 14151's domestic DEI elimination has a direct foreign policy counterpart: the State Department eliminated the 'National/Racial/Ethnic Minorities' section from the annual Country Reports on Human Rights Practices — a 31-year reporting mechanism (1993-2024) that documented Afro-descendant, Indigenous, and ethnic minority human rights conditions in 190+ countries. Congress mandated these reports under the Foreign Assistance Act; no congressional directive authorized removing this section. The United States ratified ICERD in 1994, creating binding treaty obligations to report on measures ensuring racial non-discrimination. Eliminating domestic racial equity programs AND international racial minority monitoring simultaneously represents a pattern the UN Committee on the Elimination of Racial Discrimination — the ICERD monitoring body — may assess. A UN Working Group of Experts on People of African Descent is currently drafting a new Declaration on People of African Descent (CRS IN11790, September 2025) — U.S. actions are being documented at the international level precisely as they are most harmful. The Durban Declaration (2002) established that poverty, marginalization, and economic disparities 'are closely associated with racism' — EO 14151 eliminates the domestic and international monitoring frameworks the U.S. participated in establishing.

AFRO-DESCENDANT HEMISPHERIC SCALE: The UN estimates approximately 200 million people of African descent live in the Americas. EO 14151's impacts extend beyond the U.S. domestic Black population (~44 million) to affect the hemispheric framework protecting Afro-descendant communities in Latin America and the Caribbean — including Afro-Brazilian (~120M), Afro-Colombian (~4.7M), Afro-Cuban, Afro-Puerto Rican, Afro-Dominican, Garifuna, Afro-Ecuadorian, and Afro-Peruvian communities. World Bank data (June 2025) shows higher poverty rates among Afro-descendants vs. total population in Brazil, Colombia, Ecuador, Peru, and Uruguay. Congress expressed institutional concern for Afro-descendant rights through H.Con.Res. 175 (109th Congress) and related 2005 hearings — the current elimination contradicts this recorded interest. (CRS IN11790)

FOOD INSECURITY MONITORING ELIMINATION: EO 14151's DEI framework extends to USDA, where the Economic Research Service terminated its 30-year food insecurity survey (September 20, 2025), calling it 'redundant.' The survey was the only federal mechanism tracking racial food insecurity disparities — the 2024 data (last release, December 2025) showed Black-headed households at 24.4% food insecurity vs. White at 10.1%. H.R. 1/P.L. 119-21 cuts SNAP by $187 billion over 10 years (CBO: 2.4M lose benefits); work requirements take effect December 31, 2026. There will be no federal data to measure whether these cuts worsen racial food insecurity disparities. This is the third 30+ year monitoring termination connected to DEI elimination: State Department Country Reports minorities section (1993-2024), ERS food insecurity survey (1995-2025), and tribal population reporting (mandated but unpublished since 2013). The pattern is consistent: eliminate the monitoring mechanism before the harm it would measure takes effect. Total FY2026 federal food assistance: approximately $151.3 billion (P.L. 119-37), dominated by SNAP ($106.766B serving 42.4M/month). (CRS R42353)

SOURCES: CRS Report IF12446 (June 29, 2023), https://crsreports.congress.gov/product/pdf/IF/IF12446; CRS Report IF12612 Version 3 (April 2, 2024), https://crsreports.congress.gov/product/pdf/IF/IF12612; CRS Report IF12718 Version 4 (July 26, 2024), https://crsreports.congress.gov/product/pdf/IF/IF12718; CRS Insight IN11790 Version 10 (September 18, 2025), https://crsreports.congress.gov/product/pdf/IN/IN11790; CRS Report R42353 Version 31 (February 19, 2026), https://crsreports.congress.gov/product/pdf/R/R42353

actions.status: Partially Enjoined (NEA provision struck Sept 19, 2025)

Directs agencies to "end federal funding of gender ideology" and recognize only "biological sex."

NONPROFIT IMPACT: NEA requires grant certification against promoting "gender ideology" - affects LGBTQ+ arts organizations, theaters, and cultural groups. Rhode Island Latino Arts led successful lawsuit. Ballroom culture (Black/Latino origin) and Two-Spirit traditions excluded from recognition.

TRIBAL COMMUNITY DIMENSION: Two-Spirit traditions — gender identities recognized across hundreds of tribal nations since time immemorial — are excluded from federal recognition under this order's binary biological sex framework. This erasure affects the 9,666,058 AI/AN-identifying people (2020 Census), particularly tribal members whose cultural practices include Two-Spirit roles in ceremony, governance, and community life. The order's impact compounds the broader tribal data infrastructure crisis: OMB's racial/ethnic category definitions — now subject to DEI elimination pressure — determine how AI/AN populations are counted for federal services. Excluding Two-Spirit identity from federal recognition is both a cultural erasure and a data infrastructure harm, as tribal members may be less likely to engage with federal data systems that deny their identity.

LITIGATION: NEA provision struck down Sept 19, 2025 as First Amendment violation.

LGBTQ+ LEGISLATIVE REVERSAL: The 118th Congress introduced three bills for LGBTQ+ institutional representation at the Smithsonian: H.R. 6101 (National Museum of American LGBTQI+ History and Culture Act), H.R. 6102 (commission to study feasibility), and H.R. 4273 (LGBTQI+ and Women's History Education Act directing NMAH to develop educational resources). None became law. No LGBTQI+ museum or educational mandate existed entering the 119th Congress. EO 14168's directive that agencies recognize only 'biological sex' and EO 14253's direction that the Women's History Museum 'not recognize men as women in any respect' then moved to restrict existing LGBTQ+ content across the Smithsonian. The LGBTQ+ community moved from zero institutional representation toward active erasure with no congressional floor in between. NMAH holds the AIDS Memorial Quilt panels, Barbara Gittings papers, Harvey Milk memorabilia, and women's history collections — all now without statutory protection under H.R. 4273's failed mandate.

SOURCES: CRS Report IF12612 Version 3 (April 2, 2024), https://crsreports.congress.gov/product/pdf/IF/IF12612; CRS Report IF12719 Version 7 (December 17, 2024), https://crsreports.congress.gov/product/pdf/IF/IF12719

actions.title: IMLS Staff on Administrative Leave, 1,200 Grants Terminated
actions.status: Implemented
actions.description:
Institute of Museum and Library Services terminated 1,200+ grants affecting museums and libraries nationwide.

NONPROFIT IMPACT: Terminated Native American Library Services grants - only federal program supporting tribal libraries. 28+ tribal libraries affected. Native American/Native Hawaiian Museum Services grants ended. NAGPRA compliance support eliminated. African American museum and library grants terminated. Latino cultural institution grants ended. Asian American cultural organization grants terminated.

TRIBAL DATA INFRASTRUCTURE IMPACT: The 28+ tribal libraries losing IMLS support serve communities where BIA service population data covers approximately 2 million individuals across ~326 reservations — but this metric systematically undercounts Alaska Natives and many Oklahoma tribes who lack reservation-based service areas. IMLS-funded tribal libraries are often the only community institutions with the capacity to help tribal members navigate federal data and service systems. Their defunding compounds the pre-existing tribal population data crisis documented by CRS IF12612: no biennial tribal population report published since 2013; tribal service population data unavailable below the national level; and tribal enrollment data subject to FOIA vulnerability that creates disincentives for accurate data sharing with federal agencies.

ECONOMIC IMPACT: IMLS grants to museums and libraries are part of the federal infrastructure supporting a sector that generates 4.4% of U.S. GDP — approximately $1.02 trillion in annual economic activity (BEA Arts and Cultural Production Satellite Account). Museums function as anchor institutions in the arts and culture economic ecosystem — their destabilization has downstream effects on tourism, local tax revenue, employment, and the informal economy surrounding cultural destinations. The arts sector grew 13.7% in 2021 vs. 5.9% overall GDP, demonstrating counter-cyclical resilience that makes its defunding strategically damaging — communities lose their most resilient economic stabilizer precisely when they need it most. Ann Markusen's research documents that arts and culture participants do not rely heavily on other industries but do generate downstream economic activity in publishing, printing, broadcasting, and manufacturing — meaning IMLS cuts ripple outward into adjacent economic sectors. Congress explicitly recognized the arts sector as critical economic infrastructure through emergency appropriations under P.L. 116-136 (CARES Act, $75M to NEA, 2020), P.L. 116-260 (SVOG, $16.25B to 13,000+ venues, 2021), and P.L. 117-2 (ARPA, $135M to NEA, 2021). The current elimination of IMLS reverses this congressional trajectory and operates against the explicit mandate in P.L. 89-209 (1965 National Foundation on the Arts and the Humanities Act). State and local governments are the primary actors in arts-based economic development, but federal program eliminations shift uncompensated burdens to sub-national governments already operating under fiscal constraint.

COMMUNITIES AFFECTED: The CRS confirms that federal arts programming explicitly targets nonprofit organizations, local governments, and tribal communities. Tribal museum collections, Indigenous cultural centers, and reservation heritage sites depended on IMLS as a complementary federal preservation funding stream alongside NEA Our Town and HPF grants — all now disrupted simultaneously.

HERITAGE TECHNOLOGY DIMENSION: Museums and libraries supported by IMLS use LiDAR — the dominant technology for documenting archaeological sites, sacred landscapes, and historic properties. The reduction of IMLS institutional capacity also reduces the ability to evaluate technology supply chain risks in cultural resource management. PRC firms hold 58% of the global automotive LiDAR market and PRC firm Hesai co-led development of U.S. and international LiDAR standards. Heritage professionals using PRC-manufactured LiDAR equipment face unacknowledged data sovereignty risks for the spatial data they generate about tribal lands and cultural sites. (See CRS IF12473, 'U.S.-China Competition in Emerging Technologies: LiDAR,' Updated Feb. 26, 2026.)

FY2024 FINANCIAL BASELINE — WHAT WAS BEING ELIMINATED: IMLS received $294.8 million in FY2024 — peak funding following a four-year bipartisan increase from $257M (FY2021), a 14.7% increase. IMLS is funded through the Labor, HHS, Education, and Related Agencies Appropriations Act — the same bill funding NIH, CDC, SNAP education provisions, Head Start, and community health centers. Simultaneous attacks on IMLS and these co-funded programs reflect coordinated cuts through a single appropriations subcommittee. The FY2026 budget proposes reducing IMLS to $0. Combined with NEA ($207M) and NEH ($207M), the proposed elimination targets $708.8 million in FY2024-level arts, humanities, and library appropriations.

PROGRAM-LEVEL BASELINE (FY2024): Library Grants to States: $180.0M — the largest federal library program in the U.S., distributed by population-based formula to every state, DC, and U.S. territory (including Puerto Rico, Guam, American Samoa, USVI, Northern Marianas — for territories, IMLS is often the ONLY federal library funding source). Native American/Native Hawaiian Library Services: $5.763M — the ONLY dedicated federal library funding for tribal libraries, serving 574 federally recognized tribes. No other federal agency provides library-specific grants to tribal communities. Native American/Native Hawaiian Museum Services: $3.772M (a 66% increase from $2.272M in FY2021-2022, now threatened with $0). Combined tribal library/museum funding: $9.535M — the entire federal investment in tribal libraries and Native American museums. Museum Grants for African American History & Culture: $6.0M — authorized by P.L. 108-184, the same National Museum of African American History and Culture Act that created NMAAHC, supporting more than 250 African American museums and HBCUs. Museum Grants for American Latino History & Culture: $6.0M — authorized by P.L. 116-260, the same law authorizing the Smithsonian's National Museum of the American Latino, supporting five HSI-Latino museum partnerships. Museums for America: $30.33M. National Leadership (Libraries): $15.287M. National Leadership (Museums): $9.348M. Laura Bush 21st Century Librarian: $10.0M. Research, Analysis, and Data Collection: $5.65M (eliminating IMLS eliminates the federal measurement infrastructure that documents whether libraries and museums are serving underserved communities).

COMMUNITY-SPECIFIC FUNDING ELIMINATED: $9.535M dedicated to tribal libraries and Native museums + $6.0M for African American museums/HBCUs + $6.0M for Latino museums/HSIs = $21.535M in FY2024 specifically targeted to communities of color — all proposed for elimination at $0.

STATUTORY AUTHORITY: Federal library support dates to 1956 (Library Services Act). IMLS consolidates library and museum programs under the Library Services and Technology Act (LSTA) and Museum and Library Services Act of 2018 (P.L. 115-410). The President cannot eliminate congressionally authorized programs by executive order — courts found EO 14238's elimination attempt 'violated the Take Care Clause and separation of powers.' The underlying LSTA would still exist even if IMLS were dismantled, requiring some federal library funding mechanism.

P.L. 108-184 DUAL ATTACK: The NMAAHC Act (P.L. 108-184) authorized both NMAAHC itself (the Smithsonian museum, now under EO 14253 political content review) and this IMLS grant program supporting 250+ African American museums and HBCUs. A single law is being attacked from two directions simultaneously: the museum it created faces content censorship while the community grant network it authorized faces elimination.

GAO FACILITIES STUDY: P.L. 118-47 (enacted March 22, 2024) directed GAO to study the physical condition of library and museum facilities nationwide — an 18-month deadline placing delivery approximately September 2025, the same period IMLS was being eliminated by executive order. The congressional mandate for a national assessment of library and museum physical infrastructure was threatened by the elimination of the agency whose programs maintain that infrastructure.

AMERICA250: IMLS received $1.0M in FY2024 Program Administration for America250 commemoration preparation — library-based programming for the 250th anniversary of U.S. independence in 2026, now terminated as the commemoration begins.

MILITARY HERITAGE DIMENSION: IMLS grants specifically funded military history preservation for communities of color. The Buffalo Soldiers National Museum (Houston, TX) — preserving the history of African American soldiers in post-Civil War frontier campaigns — received Museum Grants for African American History & Culture funding (FY2018) for exhibition technology, collections management, and educational programming. The Chickaloon Native Village (Alaska Native) received Native American/Native Hawaiian Museum Services funding (FY2020) for the Nay'dini'aa Na' Kayax Nahwgholnicde project — cataloging the military contributions of Indigenous peoples and the effects of military service on their communities. Indigenous peoples serve in the U.S. military at the highest per-capita rate of any demographic group. Both programs — African American museum grants ($6M FY2024) and tribal museum grants ($3.772M) — were terminated by the IMLS elimination attempt, temporarily restored, and proposed for $0 in FY2026. IMLS also co-administers Save America's Treasures (SAT) — the only federal preservation program requiring NPS + IMLS + NEA + NEH to function simultaneously — now structurally compromised as all four agencies face simultaneous degradation. (CRS R47337)

SOURCES: CRS Report IF12446 (June 29, 2023), https://crsreports.congress.gov/product/pdf/IF/IF12446; CRS Report IF12473 Version 8 (Feb. 26, 2026), https://crsreports.congress.gov/product/pdf/IF/IF12473; CRS Report IF12612 Version 3 (April 2, 2024), https://crsreports.congress.gov/product/pdf/IF/IF12612; CRS Report R48255 Version 1 (November 6, 2024), https://crsreports.congress.gov/product/pdf/R/R48255; CRS Report R47337 Version 6 (February 12, 2026), https://crsreports.congress.gov/product/pdf/R/R47337

actions.title: NEA "Friday Night Massacre" - 50%+ of Grants Terminated
actions.status: Implemented
actions.description:
NEA terminated over 50% of active grants in mass termination event.

NONPROFIT IMPACT: Arts organizations nationwide lost federal funding. Disproportionate impact on organizations serving communities of color, LGBTQ+ communities, and rural areas. Terminated grants include those for Black theater companies, Latino folk arts, Indigenous arts programs, Asian American cultural organizations, and disability arts.

ECONOMIC IMPACT: The arts and culture sector represents 4.4% of U.S. GDP — approximately $1.02 trillion in annual economic activity (BEA). The sector grew 13.7% in 2021 vs. 5.9% overall GDP, demonstrating counter-cyclical resilience that makes its defunding strategically damaging — communities lose their most resilient economic stabilizer precisely when they need it most. The Shuttered Venue Operators Grant (SVOG) disbursed approximately $14.6 billion to 13,000+ venues under P.L. 116-260 and ARPA — yet post-pandemic, movie theaters and performance venues still struggle to match pre-pandemic revenues (CRS IF12446, June 2023), meaning the mass termination compounds ongoing fragility. The mass termination eliminates the primary federal arts funding pathway for underserved communities that was the subject of emergency congressional investment: $44.5M (CARES Act, 855 organizations), $130M (ARPA, ~700 organizations). No comparable successor program authorized. NEA Challenge America — the primary federal arts access program for underserved and rural communities — was already cancelled, meaning this mass termination compounds the loss of the sole federal arts pipeline for communities with no endowment buffer and no alternative grant sources. Ann Markusen's research documents that arts and culture participants do not rely heavily on other industries but do generate downstream economic activity in publishing, printing, broadcasting, and manufacturing — meaning these cuts ripple outward into adjacent economic sectors, compounding community harm beyond the arts sector itself.

CONGRESSIONAL INTENT: These terminations reverse specific congressional emergency appropriations under P.L. 116-136 (CARES Act, $75M to NEA), P.L. 117-2 (ARPA, $135M to NEA), and operate against the explicit economic development mandate in P.L. 89-209 (1965 National Foundation on the Arts and the Humanities Act). Congress was moving toward expansion of arts economic development infrastructure — not contraction.

QUANTIFIED IMPACTS: NEA Our Town grants baseline: $3.9M (FY2022) to nonprofits, local governments, and tribal communities. ARPA relief: ~$130M awarded to ~700 organizations. CARES Act: $44.5M to 855 nonprofit arts organizations covering staff salaries, artist fees, and facility costs.

FY2024 FINANCIAL BASELINE — WHAT WAS BEING ELIMINATED: NEA received $207 million in FY2024 — the highest funding in the agency's 60-year history, following a four-year bipartisan increase from $167.5M (FY2021) to $207M (FY2023-2024), a 23.6% increase enacted through divided Congresses. The FY2026 budget proposes reducing NEA to $0. NEA and NEH are funded through the Interior, Environment, and Related Agencies Appropriations Act — the same bill funding NPS (24% staff reduction), BIA (25%+ BIE cuts), and EPA (environmental justice office eliminated). Simultaneous attacks on all these agencies reflect coordinated cuts through a single appropriations subcommittee, not independent decisions.

PROGRAM-LEVEL BASELINE (FY2024): State Partnerships: $65.18M (formula grants to state/regional arts agencies with designated funding for folk and traditional arts, arts education, and outreach to underserved communities — this designated set-aside is where Indigenous traditional arts, powwow regalia, and immigrant folk arts received guaranteed federal support). Direct Grants: $97.77M (competitive programs including Grants for Arts Projects, Our Town — arts as community development, and Challenge America — explicitly for 'underserved populations,' now suspended). National Initiatives: Creative Forces: NEA Military Healing Arts Network (veterans arts therapy, eliminated), Poetry Out Loud (high school students in all 50 states), NEA Big Read (community reading programs). By law (P.L. 111-88, Title IV, Section 438), NEA cannot give grants to individuals except for: literature fellowships, National Heritage Fellowships (honoring master folk and traditional artists — disproportionately from Indigenous, immigrant, and rural communities), and American Jazz Masters Fellowships. The $17 million redirected to the National Garden of American Heroes represents approximately 17% of the $97.77M direct grant baseline.

AMERICA250: The FY2026 budget eliminates NEA in the year of the 250th anniversary of U.S. independence — when both IMLS ($1M preparation) and NEH ($7M 'A More Perfect Union' initiative) had dedicated America250 cultural programming. The nation's 250th anniversary commemoration in 2026 proceeds without the federal arts and humanities infrastructure that was being built to contextualize it through diverse community voices.

SOURCES: CRS Report IF12446 (June 29, 2023), https://crsreports.congress.gov/product/pdf/IF/IF12446; CRS Report R48255 Version 1 (November 6, 2024), https://crsreports.congress.gov/product/pdf/R/R48255

actions.title: NEH Chair Removed; 1,400+ Grants Terminated
actions.status: Implemented - Partial Court Order
actions.description:
NEH Chair Dr. Shelly Lowe (Navajo) removed. 1,400+ humanities grants terminated.

NONPROFIT IMPACT: State humanities councils lost all federal funding. Historical societies, archives, libraries, museums, and universities lost humanities grants. Projects on African American history, Latino heritage, Indigenous languages, Asian American experiences, and civil rights history terminated.

ECONOMIC IMPACT: The arts and culture sector represents 4.4% of U.S. GDP — approximately $1.02 trillion in annual economic activity (BEA Arts and Cultural Production Satellite Account). The sector grew 13.7% in 2021 vs. 5.9% overall GDP, demonstrating counter-cyclical resilience that makes its defunding strategically damaging during or after economic crises. The 1,400+ terminated grants represent active, ongoing commitments — not future appropriations — meaning real organizations with real staff and active project obligations are experiencing immediate economic harm, not just prospective funding uncertainty. The NEH terminations interrupt a funding ecosystem that the CRS documents as generating measurable economic multiplier effects. Ann Markusen's research documents that arts and culture participants do not rely heavily on other industries but do generate downstream economic activity in publishing, printing, broadcasting, and manufacturing — meaning NEH cuts ripple outward into adjacent economic sectors, compounding community harm beyond the humanities sector itself. Following the Great Recession, state arts economies grew 3.4% in the recovery year compared to 3% overall gross state product growth — confirming the sector's role as a natural economic diversification tool, particularly for the rural, tribal, and economically distressed communities that NEH programming served. The Federal Historic Preservation Tax Credit has leveraged approximately $130 billion in private investment across 48,000+ projects since 1976 — NEH's Preservation and Access program ($21.4M FY2024) feeds the pipeline of collections and properties that benefit from this investment multiplier.

COMMUNITIES AFFECTED: CRS confirms NEH programming explicitly targets nonprofit organizations, local governments, and tribal communities. The 1,400+ terminations represent a disproportionate impact on the smallest, least-resourced cultural institutions — those with no endowment buffer and no alternative funding pipeline. Federal arts funding represents the primary economic development infrastructure for these communities — the CRS documents no equivalent private-sector substitute for underserved geographies. The loss of NEH Humanities Initiatives at Tribal Colleges compounds the CRS-documented loss of NEA tribal grant pipelines.

TRIBAL DATA DIMENSION: The removal of NEH Chair Dr. Shelly Lowe (Navajo) — the first Native American to lead NEH — compounds the tribal data infrastructure crisis documented by CRS IF12612. NEH Humanities Initiatives at Tribal Colleges supported the institutional capacity tribes need to collect, validate, and preserve their own population and cultural data. The CRS documents that not all tribes have the resources and trained personnel to collect consistent population data meeting federal methodological requirements — NEH-funded tribal college programs helped build that capacity. Their termination widens the structural disadvantage where better-resourced tribes receive more accurate data and therefore more funding. The 9,666,058 AI/AN-identifying people (2020 Census) — of whom only ~2 million are captured in BIA service population data — depend on the cultural and educational infrastructure that NEH grants supported.

CONGRESSIONAL INTENT: These terminations operate against the explicit mandate in P.L. 89-209 (1965) and reverse the trajectory of emergency congressional appropriations under P.L. 116-136, P.L. 116-260, and P.L. 117-2.

FY2024 FINANCIAL BASELINE — WHAT WAS BEING ELIMINATED: NEH received $207 million in FY2024 — the highest funding in the agency's 60-year history, following a four-year bipartisan increase from $167.5M (FY2021) to $207M (FY2023-2024), a 23.6% increase. Since 1965, NEH has awarded almost 70,000 grants worth more than $6.4 billion across all 50 states, DC, and U.S. territories. The FY2026 budget proposes reducing NEH to $0. NEH is funded through the Interior, Environment, and Related Agencies Appropriations Act — the same bill as NPS, BIA, and EPA — and simultaneous attacks on all these agencies reflect coordinated cuts through a single appropriations subcommittee.

PROGRAM-LEVEL BASELINE (FY2024): Federal/State Partnership (state humanities councils): $65.0M — the exact amount terminated in April 2025 when all General Operating Support grants to 56 state/territorial humanities councils were eliminated. This was the largest NEH program, reaching all 50 states, DC, Puerto Rico, USVI, Guam, American Samoa, and Northern Marianas. For rural communities, tribal communities, and territories, state humanities councils were often the only humanities programming source. Preservation and Access: $21.4M — where Tulsa Race Massacre survivor oral histories, Indigenous language documentation, Japanese American incarceration testimonies, and immigrant community archives were funded. Research Programs: $17.5M. Education Programs: $15.4M. Public Programs: $15.0M (exhibitions, documentaries, radio — including public television and radio programming, the same CPB/PBS/NPR infrastructure being eliminated by P.L. 119-28). Digital Humanities Initiatives: $6.2M (digitization making Indigenous language materials, civil rights archives, and immigrant community records accessible online). Special Initiatives: $7.0M — including 'A More Perfect Union' (America250 humanities scholarship for the 250th anniversary occurring in 2026) and 'American Tapestry: Weaving Together Past, Present, and Future' (explicitly listing equity and climate as program pillars — directly targeted by EO 14151). Matching Grants: $15.0M — NEH matching grants have generated more than $1.3 billion in nonfederal funds since 1977, meaning NEH elimination removes not just federal funding but the federal matching incentive that attracted private philanthropy to humanities institutions in communities that otherwise receive no philanthropic attention.

STATUTORY FOUNDATION: NEH was created by the National Foundation on the Arts and the Humanities Act of 1965 (P.L. 89-209). Only Congress can repeal this law. The President cannot unilaterally eliminate what Congress created — the same Take Care Clause and separation of powers logic that led courts to block IMLS elimination under EO 14238. As long as Congress appropriates funding, NEH continues to exist regardless of executive preference.

MILITARY HISTORY CASUALTY: The NEH Federal/State Partnership termination ($65M, April 2025) eliminated humanities council funding that specifically supported veterans' military history programming. Idaho Humanities (Idaho's state humanities council) awarded a 2024 grant to the Warhawk Air Museum for a Global War on Terror exhibit — documenting the defining military experience of post-9/11 veterans. This local military history commemoration is exactly the type of programming state humanities councils funded before termination. The Global War on Terror involved the largest National Guard/Reserve deployment since WWII, service by Muslim Americans simultaneously targeted at home, DACA recipients in uniform, and Filipino and Pacific Islander service members at elevated rates. NEH also co-administers Save America's Treasures (SAT) — the only federal preservation program requiring NPS + IMLS + NEA + NEH to function simultaneously — all four agencies degraded within the same 12-month period. (CRS R47337)

SOURCES: CRS Report IF12446 (June 29, 2023), https://crsreports.congress.gov/product/pdf/IF/IF12446; CRS Report IF12612 Version 3 (April 2, 2024), https://crsreports.congress.gov/product/pdf/IF/IF12612; CRS Report R48255 Version 1 (November 6, 2024), https://crsreports.congress.gov/product/pdf/R/R48255; CRS Report R47337 Version 6 (February 12, 2026), https://crsreports.congress.gov/product/pdf/R/R47337

actions.title: Kennedy Center Eliminates Social Impact Team and DEI Programs
actions.status: Implemented
actions.description:
Kennedy Center eliminated Social Impact team and DEI-related programming.

NONPROFIT IMPACT: Ended The Cartography Project documenting lynchings and race-based violence. Terminated 19-year Christmas Eve jazz tradition. Eliminated arts access programs for low-income communities. Disability arts initiatives terminated. Pride programming eliminated.

ECONOMIC IMPACT: The Kennedy Center is a flagship institution within a sector that represents 4.4% of U.S. GDP — approximately $1.02 trillion in annual economic activity (BEA). The sector grew 13.7% in 2021 vs. 5.9% overall GDP, demonstrating counter-cyclical resilience that makes its dismantling strategically damaging — communities lose their most resilient economic stabilizer precisely when they need it most. Ann Markusen's research documents that arts and culture participants do not rely heavily on other industries but do generate downstream economic activity in publishing, printing, broadcasting, and manufacturing — the Kennedy Center's community programming supported this multiplier effect for low-income D.C. communities. Congress explicitly recognized the arts sector as critical economic infrastructure through emergency appropriations under P.L. 116-136 (CARES Act, $75M to NEA, 855 organizations, 2020), P.L. 116-260 (SVOG, $16.25B authorized, $14.6B disbursed to 13,000+ venues, 2021), and P.L. 117-2 (ARPA, $135M to NEA, ~700 organizations, 2021). These actions operate against the explicit economic development mandate in P.L. 89-209 (1965 National Foundation on the Arts and the Humanities Act).

GOVERNANCE STRUCTURE: The Kennedy Center is a congressionally created Smithsonian entity that receives a direct congressional appropriation separate from the main Smithsonian appropriation (CRS IF12718). This means it is both more directly subject to congressional appropriations leverage and more directly exposed to executive pressure through the appropriations process than other Smithsonian entities. As a congressionally created entity, any executive order purporting to dictate its programming operates against the explicit will of Congress, which created the institution. The Smithsonian's statutory mission — 'the increase and diffusion of knowledge' — is the legal standard against which content restrictions should be measured.

LEGAL ARCHITECTURE — KENNEDY CENTER STATUS: The Kennedy Center is a bureau of the Smithsonian Institution — a trust instrumentality organizationally separate from all three branches of government (CRS IF12718). It was established by Congress and redesignated in 1964 as a living memorial to President Kennedy (20 U.S.C. § 76i). Congress structured the Kennedy Center as largely autonomous from the Smithsonian Board of Regents, with separate budget authority and trust fund management. Trust fund decisions are reviewable only by courts — not by the Smithsonian Board of Regents, not by OMB, not by the executive branch. The President has no appointment or removal authority over the Smithsonian itself; CRS flagged in February 2025 that the Smithsonian's nongovernmental status 'may have implications' for efforts to change trustee membership 'in ways that are not authorized in statute,' and that questions arise if the President 'asserts removal authority of a component of the Smithsonian, when he lacks similar authority over the broader enterprise' (CRS IF12911).

BOARD PURGE (February 7, 2025): The Kennedy Center Board of Trustees comprises specified statutory members (Cabinet secretaries, congressional leaders, Librarian of Congress, NPS Director, Smithsonian Secretary, DC Mayor) and 36 general trustees appointed by the President for 6-year terms. The statute is silent on removal except for term expiration — neither authorizing nor prohibiting presidential removal of general trustees. The Kennedy Center acknowledged 'nothing in the Center's statute would prevent a new administration from replacing board members,' while noting it had never happened before. The legal basis is statutory silence plus claimed presidential appointment authority; the legal constraint is the Smithsonian's nongovernmental character.

STATUTORY BOARD CONFLICTS: The Librarian of Congress (Carla Hayden, fired May 8, 2025) is a specified statutory Kennedy Center trustee — her firing created a vacancy filled automatically by any replacement. Secretary of State Marco Rubio serves simultaneously as statutory Kennedy Center trustee, Acting Archivist of the United States, and effectively Acting USAID Administrator. The NPS Director serves as statutory trustee while NPS has lost 24% of permanent staff. Smithsonian Secretary Lonnie Bunch serves as trustee by duty while navigating institutional independence pressures.

FUNDING ARCHITECTURE (FY2023): Federal appropriations: $45.38 million — specifically for maintaining Kennedy Center facilities as a federal memorial, NOT for programming. Performances, contributions, and other earned sources: approximately $266 million. Ratio: ~15% federal appropriations / ~85% earned revenue and contributions. The artist boycott — 26+ cancelled performances, Nutcracker sales down 33%, Hamilton 2026 run lost, major donors withdrawing — threatens the 85% of revenue that funds actual programming. This is an existential financial threat, not a symbolic one. Federal appropriations cannot substitute for earned revenue collapse. The appropriation leverage is Congress's, not the executive's — cutting Kennedy Center funding would require congressional action against a living memorial to President Kennedy.

COMMUNITY IMPACT SCALE: 2 million annual visitors; 1.6 million ticket buyers; 2,200+ performances and exhibits; 400+ free performances annually including daily Millennium Stage. The Social Impact team organized community programming connecting these 400+ free performances to underserved communities, disability arts, and equity initiatives. Its elimination does not merely affect paid programming — it eliminates the infrastructure connecting the primary equity access mechanism (free performances) to the communities least able to access ticketed events. Home of the National Symphony Orchestra and Washington National Opera.

RENAMING (December 18, 2025): Board voted to rename facility 'Donald J. Trump and John F. Kennedy Memorial Center for the Performing Arts.' Congress designated this building as a living memorial to President Kennedy in 1964 (20 U.S.C. § 76i). The Board has statutory authority over performing arts programming and trust funds, but the memorial designation and name are congressional acts — not Board prerogatives. Rep. Joyce Beatty (D-OH), an ex officio trustee, was muted during the vote and prevented from voicing opposition. Beatty v. Trump challenges the renaming as beyond the Board's statutory authority. Congress's appropriation of $45.38M annually specifically maintains Kennedy Center 'facilities as a federal memorial' — a congressional characterization the renaming arguably contradicts.

SOURCES: CRS Report IF12446 (June 29, 2023), https://crsreports.congress.gov/product/pdf/IF/IF12446; CRS Report IF12718 Version 4 (July 26, 2024), https://crsreports.congress.gov/product/pdf/IF/IF12718; CRS Report IF12911 Version 4 (February 18, 2025), https://crsreports.congress.gov/product/pdf/IF/IF12911

actions.title: Rescissions Act of 2025 - Defunding the Corporation for Public Broadcasting
actions.status: Enacted
actions.description:
P.L. 119-28 rescinds appropriated funds for Corporation for Public Broadcasting.

NONPROFIT IMPACT: Affects 1,500 local public radio and TV stations (mostly nonprofits). Rural stations serving Indigenous, Latino, and isolated communities face closure. Over 200 rural communities served only by public broadcasting lose all broadcast service. Stations serving as community cultural centers face defunding.

ECONOMIC IMPACT: Public broadcasting is included in the BEA Arts and Cultural Production Satellite Account's measurement of the arts and culture sector. CPB's defunding is not simply a media policy decision — it is the elimination of a component of the $1.02 trillion arts and cultural economy (4.4% of U.S. GDP), with particular harm to rural communities where public broadcasting often represents the only local media infrastructure. The sector grew 13.7% in 2021 vs. 5.9% overall GDP, demonstrating counter-cyclical resilience — defunding it during or after economic crises is particularly harmful because communities lose their most resilient economic stabilizer precisely when they need it most. Because arts and culture production is economically independent of complex supply chains, it serves as a natural economic diversification tool — particularly for rural, tribal, and economically distressed communities. Ann Markusen's research documents that arts activity generates downstream economic benefits in publishing, printing, broadcasting, and manufacturing — public broadcasting's destruction removes both direct cultural infrastructure and this downstream economic multiplier from 200+ rural communities with no alternative.

CONGRESSIONAL INTENT: Congress established the arts sector as a permanent federal priority through P.L. 89-209 (1965 National Foundation on the Arts and the Humanities Act) and explicitly recognized it as critical economic infrastructure through emergency appropriations under P.L. 116-136 (CARES Act, $75M to NEA, 855 organizations, 2020), P.L. 116-260 (SVOG, $16.25B authorized, $14.6B disbursed to 13,000+ venues, 2021), and P.L. 117-2 (ARPA, $135M to NEA, ~700 organizations, 2021). P.L. 119-28 reverses this trajectory. State and local governments are the primary actors in arts-based economic development but federal program eliminations shift uncompensated burdens to sub-national governments already operating under fiscal constraint.

SOURCE: CRS Report IF12446 (June 29, 2023). https://crsreports.congress.gov/product/pdf/IF/IF12446

The arts sector's documented ability to outpace overall GDP recovery post-recession (13.7% vs. 5.9% in 2021) means dissolving CPB represents a strategic economic self-wound for the communities least able to absorb it.

actions.status: Enjoined
actions.description:
Proclamation banning issuance of visas to international students, faculty, and researchers at Harvard University.

NONPROFIT IMPACT: First-ever executive action targeting a specific university. Affects 7,000+ international students. Chills academic freedom at all universities. Retaliation for Harvard's refusal to comply with political demands.

LITIGATION: Preliminary injunction granted; Harvard v. DHS ongoing.

MUSLIM STUDENT AND HAJJ TRAVEL DIMENSION: The Harvard visa ban intersects with the broader framework of anti-Muslim federal policy. Muslim international students — from Arab, South Asian, Southeast Asian, and African Muslim-majority countries — are disproportionately affected by both university-specific bans and the 75-country visa processing freeze. The five pillars of Islam include Hajj (mandatory pilgrimage to Mecca for every physically and financially able Muslim, once in their lifetime) and sawm (mandatory Ramadan fasting). Muslim students subject to visa restrictions face uncertainty about re-entry if they travel for Hajj or return home for Ramadan family gatherings. Federal workplace reductions under DOGE and EO 14210 reduce the HR compliance infrastructure that manages religious accommodation requests — including Ramadan fasting accommodation for Muslim students in federally funded university settings. The 2020 U.S. Religion Census counts 4.5 million Muslims in the United States; Muslim international students are a significant component of this community whose religious practice — prayer, fasting, Hajj travel, zakat charitable giving — is structurally disrupted by visa uncertainty. (CRS R45003)

actions.title: Ending Illegal Discrimination and Restoring Merit-Based Opportunity
actions.status: Active - Multiple Injunctions
actions.description:
Extends DEI restrictions to federal contractors and grant recipients. Targets "divisive concepts" in education.

NONPROFIT IMPACT: Universities, schools, and educational nonprofits receiving federal funds must comply. Teaching about systemic racism, implicit bias, and structural inequality potentially prohibited. Affects grants for African American studies, ethnic studies, women's studies, and LGBTQ+ research.

SMITHSONIAN STATUTORY MISSION CONFLICT: This order's 'divisive concepts' framework directly conflicts with the Smithsonian's foundational legal mandate — established in the Smithson bequest and codified in statute since 1846 — to support 'the increase and diffusion of knowledge' (CRS IF12718). Accurate historical scholarship on slavery, colonialism, civil rights, and Indigenous history is an exercise of this mandate. Political content restrictions limiting what knowledge Smithsonian museums can diffuse contradict the purpose for which Congress accepted Smithson's bequest. NMAI, NMAAHC, the Women's History Museum, and the National Museum of the American Latino are congressionally created entities — any direction to restrict their content interpretation of American history operates against the explicit will of Congress, which created these institutions to serve specific communities. The framing of colonial violence and forced removal as 'divisive' directly contradicts the congressional mandate under which NMAI was created. Congress cannot create an institution to 'increase and diffuse knowledge' and simultaneously restrict which knowledge it is permitted to diffuse.

LEGISLATIVE REVERSAL: The 118th Congress introduced bipartisan legislation (H.R. 765/S. 233, African American History Act) to expand NMAAHC's educational mandate with statutory authority to develop resources for educators, students, and families. Neither bill became law. The 119th Congress's H.R. 3840/S. 2385 would impose permanent statutory content restrictions on NMAAHC — moving in the opposite direction from the most recent congressional legislative intent. Similarly, three LGBTQ+ representation bills (H.R. 6101, H.R. 6102, H.R. 4273) all stalled in the 118th Congress — the LGBTQ+ community moved from zero institutional representation toward active erasure under EO 14253 with no congressional floor in between. The 118th Congress also introduced legislation for an African Burial Ground Memorial Museum in NYC (H.R. 1349), a Juneteenth Museum in Galveston (H.R. 10190), and jazz preservation at NMAH (H.R. 240) — all stalled. None have been reintroduced in the 119th Congress.

STATUTORY FLOOR — BLACK HISTORY MONTH: Black History Month is federally authorized by P.L. 99-244 (1986), a joint resolution of Congress designating February as National African American History Month 'in celebration of the achievements of all that Black Americans have done to help our country.' No executive order — including EO 14253 or the 'divisive concepts' framework — can revoke a congressional designation without congressional action. The statutory floor for Black History Month recognition remains intact even as the executive branch attacks every federal institution built to support it. H.Res. 181 (2025), the 'Original Black History Month Resolution,' reaffirmed congressional recognition in the 119th Congress.

2026 CENTENNIAL — MAXIMUM ATTACK IN THE YEAR OF MAXIMUM MEANING: The 2026 ASALH theme is 'A Century of Black History Commemorations' — marking 100 years since Dr. Carter G. Woodson established Negro History Week in 1926 specifically because Black history was being systematically erased from American consciousness. Woodson created the institutional infrastructure of commemoration as a defense against erasure. In the centennial year of that founding: NMAAHC is under content review; NEH terminated 1,400+ grants including Tulsa survivor oral histories; NPS lost 24% of staff at 15+ civil rights heritage sites; LOC had its first Black, first woman Librarian fired; NARA lost 100+ staff processing Freedmen's Bureau records; CPB was eliminated. The centennial of Woodson's founding response to erasure is occurring in the year of the most comprehensive federal attack on Black history institutions in that 100-year history.

SEVEN-AGENCY BLACK HISTORY NETWORK: The federal Black History Month website is a joint project of seven agencies: LOC, NARA, NEH, National Gallery of Art, NPS, Smithsonian, and U.S. Holocaust Memorial Museum (CRS R44363). These seven agencies collectively constitute the coordinated federal infrastructure for Black history preservation and access — and in 2025-2026, all seven have been subjected simultaneously to staff reductions, budget cuts, leadership removals, or political content review. LOC: Hayden fired, DOJ official installed, 10% budget cut proposed. NARA: 100+ staff RIF, $50M cut, Acting Archivist = Rubio. NEH: 1,400+ grants terminated, 65% staff fired, Tulsa oral histories canceled. NPS: 24% permanent staff lost, civil rights sites degraded. Smithsonian: EO 14253 review, diversity officers terminated. National Gallery: budget pressure. USHMM: institutional pressure. The simultaneous degradation of all seven co-agencies constitutes the comprehensive dismantling of the federal infrastructure Woodson's descendants built over 100 years.

PROCLAMATION ANOMALY: The Trump February 3, 2026 Black History Month proclamation is noted by CRS as 'not listed in the Compilation of Presidential Documents search results list' — an unusual departure from 30 years of consistent bipartisan practice during which every president from Reagan through Biden issued fully-indexed proclamations. The anomaly is consistent with either NARA's documented administrative capacity degradation or perfunctory issuance without standard records management processing.

NON-GOVERNMENTAL BACKSTOP: ASALH (founded by Woodson in 1915) is a private organization that owns the Black History Month tradition — it sets the annual theme, not any federal agency. Black History Month cannot be federally eliminated because its origin institution is not subject to executive order. Three non-federal archives constitute the institutional resilience infrastructure: Moorland-Spingarn Research Center at Howard (world's premier Black archive — dependent on HBCU funding frozen by H.R. 1), Schomburg Center for Research in Black Culture (11M items, NYC), and Archives of African American Music and Culture at Indiana University. These institutions cannot be directly targeted by executive order but depend on NEH scholar pipelines, HBCU funding, and IMLS grants that are being cut.

MUSLIM HERITAGE MONTH AND NMAAHC MUSLIM OBJECTS: H.Res. 61 (2025) proposes congressional recognition of January as 'Muslim-American Heritage Month.' If enacted as a joint resolution — like P.L. 99-244 creating Black History Month (1986) — it would create a statutory floor for Muslim-American cultural heritage that no executive order could revoke. The resolution was introduced in the same 119th Congress that enacted travel bans on 39 Muslim-majority countries. Two competing Ramadan resolutions (H.Res. 192 and H.Res. 263) were also introduced — paralleling the 'Original Black History Month Resolution' (H.Res. 181) dynamic where congressional recognition continues as contested political resistance. The 'divisive concepts' framework has a direct Muslim heritage intersection at NMAAHC: the museum houses over 1,093 Islamic religious objects — Qurans, prayer beads, prayer garments, Arabic-text talismans — representing the Muslim heritage of enslaved Africans (historians estimate 15-30% were Muslim). EO 14253's political review of NMAAHC places these Islamic religious artifacts within the scope of content review by an administration simultaneously banning travel from Muslim-majority countries during Ramadan 2026. The convergence of anti-Black history policy (EO 14253) and anti-Muslim policy (travel bans, visa revocations) meets precisely at these 1,093 objects. (CRS R45003)

SOURCES: CRS Report IF12718 Version 4 (July 26, 2024), https://crsreports.congress.gov/product/pdf/IF/IF12718; CRS Report IF12719 Version 7 (December 17, 2024), https://crsreports.congress.gov/product/pdf/IF/IF12719; CRS Report R44363 Version 17 (February 5, 2026), https://crsreports.congress.gov/product/pdf/R/R44363; CRS Report R45003 Version 16 (March 3, 2026), https://crsreports.congress.gov/product/pdf/R/R45003

actions.title: FY2026 Budget: Latino and Anacostia Museums Defunded
actions.status: Proposed
actions.description:
FY2026 budget request eliminates funding for National Museum of the American Latino and Anacostia Community Museum.

NONPROFIT IMPACT: Latino Museum construction halted despite congressional authorization. Anacostia - nation's first federally-funded Black community museum - faces closure. NMAAHC and NMAI face programming cuts. Diversity office closed.

SMITHSONIAN FUNDING STRUCTURE: Congressional appropriations comprise approximately 66% of Smithsonian annual expenditures since FY2001 (CRS IF12718). The remaining ~34% comes from grants, gifts, bequests, commercial revenue, and investments ('trust funds'). The FY2026 budget threat is real but structurally limited: approximately one-third of Smithsonian activities are funded through trust resources not subject to executive impoundment. However, OMB control of appropriations disbursement creates meaningful leverage over the appropriation-dependent 66%. The administration's funding leverage is indirect and legally constrained — appropriations are a congressional power. The distinction between OMB impoundment of already-appropriated funds (legally contested) and future congressional appropriation reductions (requiring congressional action) is critical.

GOVERNANCE TIER VULNERABILITY: NMAAHC, NMAI, and the National Museum of the American Latino are congressionally created entities — they can only be fundamentally altered by Congress (CRS IF12718). The Anacostia Community Museum is Smithsonian-established (not congressional) and has less governance protection, making it more vulnerable to Board of Regents decisions under administrative pressure. The National Museum of the American Latino has been congressionally authorized but not yet built — meaning 65 million Latiné Americans face the prospect of their national museum born under statutory content constraints before it opens.

ECONOMIC IMPACT: Smithsonian museums are anchor institutions within the $1.02 trillion U.S. arts and cultural economy (4.4% of GDP). Museums generate downstream economic activity in tourism, local tax revenue, employment, hospitality, and the informal economy surrounding cultural destinations. The Federal Historic Preservation Tax Credit has leveraged approximately $130 billion in private investment across 48,000+ projects since 1976 — African American historic properties, including HBCU campus structures and historic districts, are major beneficiaries of this pipeline. The Historic Preservation Fund peaked at $204.5M (FY2023) but was cut to $168.9M in FY2025 (P.L. 119-4) — a 10% cut from FY2024's $188.7M — before the proposed FY2026 94% cut (CRS IF12713). HPF formula grants support preservation grants to states, tribes, local governments, and nonprofits including the Save America's Treasures program. Defunding these museums while the arts sector grew at 13.7% (vs. 5.9% overall GDP in 2021) represents the destruction of proven economic infrastructure.

COMMUNITY STAKES: The Latino Museum and Anacostia Community Museum serve communities where federal arts funding represents the primary economic development infrastructure — the CRS documents no equivalent private-sector substitute for underserved geographies. African American historic districts, HBCU campus structures, and Black church buildings that leverage the federal Historic Preservation Tax Credit for rehabilitation and community reinvestment are jeopardized by reduced federal partnership capacity. Spanish colonial mission structures, ranchos, and border-region historic properties eligible for Historic Preservation Tax Credit rehabilitation face similar threats.

HERITAGE TECHNOLOGY DIMENSION: Smithsonian collections and the heritage sites these museums document depend on LiDAR for archaeological survey, collections mapping, and NHPA Section 106 compliance work. PRC firms hold 58% of the global automotive LiDAR market and PRC firm Hesai co-led U.S. and international LiDAR standards development. Budget cuts reducing Smithsonian institutional capacity also reduce the ability to evaluate and address technology supply chain risks in cultural resource management — creating unacknowledged data sovereignty vulnerabilities for the spatial data generated about Indigenous, African American, and Latino heritage sites.

SHAWL ACT GENEALOGY — NMAL CONTENT RESTRICTION: The 'political viewpoint diversity' provision in S. 1304/H.R. 1330 Section 2(c) was first introduced in H.R. 9274 (SHAWL Act, 118th Congress, introduced August 2, 2024, by Rep. Nicole Malliotakis). The SHAWL Act would have conditioned NMAL and Women's History Museum siting on compliance with viewpoint diversity requirements, including biennial Secretary reports on exhibit revisions. Though H.R. 9274 did not pass in the 118th Congress, its central mechanism was carried directly into S. 1304/H.R. 1330 in the 119th Congress by the same lead House sponsor. This is a planned, sequential legislative strategy — the 19-year fight for NMAL (first introduced 2003) is now being conditioned on political viewpoint compliance requirements embedded in the very siting legislation needed to build the museum.

SEVEN-AGENCY BLACK HISTORY NETWORK IMPACT: The Smithsonian is one of seven federal agencies that jointly constitute the coordinated federal infrastructure for Black history preservation — alongside LOC, NARA, NEH, National Gallery of Art, NPS, and U.S. Holocaust Memorial Museum (CRS R44363). FY2026 budget cuts to the Smithsonian are not an isolated institutional event but part of the simultaneous degradation of all seven co-agencies in the Black History Month network. The Smithsonian's cuts to NMAAHC, NMAL, and Anacostia compound with: NEH's 1,400+ grant terminations affecting Black history scholarship; NPS's 24% staff loss at 15+ civil rights heritage sites; NARA's degraded processing of Freedmen's Bureau records and slave manifests; LOC's loss of leadership over Frederick Douglass papers and WPA slave narratives. This occurs in 2026 — the centennial of Carter G. Woodson's 1926 founding of Negro History Week, the ASALH theme being 'A Century of Black History Commemorations.' The Smithsonian budget attack is part of a comprehensive assault on Woodson's 100-year institutional legacy.

P.L. 108-184 DUAL ATTACK — NMAAHC AND ITS COMMUNITY NETWORK: The National Museum of African American History and Culture Act (P.L. 108-184) authorized both NMAAHC itself AND an IMLS grant program supporting the broader ecosystem of 250+ African American museums and HBCUs ($6.0M in FY2024). The Museum Grants for American Latino History & Culture ($6.0M in FY2024) are similarly authorized by P.L. 116-260 — the same law authorizing the Smithsonian's National Museum of the American Latino. Both museums face EO 14253 political content review while the IMLS community grant programs authorized by the same laws face elimination at $0 in FY2026. A single statutory foundation is being attacked from two directions: the museums face content censorship while their community support networks are defunded. Combined FY2024 community-specific museum grants: $21.535M ($6.0M African American + $6.0M Latino + $5.763M Native American/Native Hawaiian Library Services + $3.772M Native American/Native Hawaiian Museum Services) — the entire IMLS investment in communities of color, proposed for elimination. (CRS R48255)

THREE-AGENCY APPROPRIATIONS ARCHITECTURE: NEA ($207M FY2024) and NEH ($207M) are funded through the Interior, Environment, and Related Agencies Appropriations Act — the same bill funding NPS, BIA, EPA, and DOI. IMLS ($294.8M) is funded through the Labor, HHS, Education Act — the same bill as NIH, CDC, and SNAP. The combined $708.8M in FY2024 cultural agency funding — peak levels following a four-year bipartisan increase of $116.8M (FY2021-2024) — is proposed for complete elimination at $0 in FY2026. Smithsonian budget cuts compound this: they degrade the institutional anchor (NMAAHC, NMAI, NMAL) while IMLS elimination destroys the community grant network (250+ African American museums, Latino museum partnerships, tribal libraries and museums) that these anchors were designed to support. (CRS R48255)

AMERICA250 CULTURAL INFRASTRUCTURE ELIMINATED: FY2024 appropriations included $1M for IMLS America250 library programming preparation and $7M for NEH's 'A More Perfect Union' humanities scholarship initiative — both building cultural infrastructure for the 250th anniversary of U.S. independence in 2026. Both programs have been terminated as America250 commemoration begins, leaving the nation's 250th anniversary without the federal cultural infrastructure being built to contextualize it through diverse community histories. (CRS R48255)

NMAACH MUSLIM RELIGIOUS HERITAGE: NMAAHC houses over 1,093 Islamic religious objects — Qurans, prayer beads, prayer garments, talismans, and amulets with Arabic text — representing the Muslim heritage of enslaved Africans brought to the Americas. Historians estimate 15-30% of enslaved Africans brought to North America were Muslim — many were literate in Arabic, some were Islamic scholars. These artifacts are not a separate 'Islam section'; they are African American history. The EO 14253 political review of NMAAHC exhibitions therefore simultaneously targets African American history and Muslim religious heritage, at the precise moment that travel bans targeting Muslim-majority countries, visa revocations of Muslim students, and Ramadan 2026 (beginning approximately March 1-2, 2026) are all active. The South Asian and Himalayan collection at the National Museum of Asian Art — also a Smithsonian museum — houses 1,200+ objects including Islamic art from South and Southeast Asia, similarly within scope of the EO 14253 review. Smithsonian budget cuts reduce institutional capacity to manage, preserve, and exhibit these irreplaceable Muslim religious heritage collections for 4.5 million American Muslims (2020 U.S. Religion Census). (CRS R45003)

SOURCES: CRS Report IF12446 (June 29, 2023), https://crsreports.congress.gov/product/pdf/IF/IF12446; CRS Report IF12473 Version 8 (Feb. 26, 2026), https://crsreports.congress.gov/product/pdf/IF/IF12473; CRS Report IF12718 Version 4 (July 26, 2024), https://crsreports.congress.gov/product/pdf/IF/IF12718; CRS Report IF12719 Version 7 (December 17, 2024), https://crsreports.congress.gov/product/pdf/IF/IF12719; CRS Report R44363 Version 17 (February 5, 2026), https://crsreports.congress.gov/product/pdf/R/R44363; CRS Report R45003 Version 16 (March 3, 2026), https://crsreports.congress.gov/product/pdf/R/R45003
