actions.title: Securing Our Borders Executive Order
actions.status: Active - Partial Injunctions
actions.description:
Declares national emergency at southern border. Deploys military for immigration enforcement.

NONPROFIT IMPACT: Immigrant services organizations face operational challenges. Legal aid nonprofits overwhelmed. Humanitarian organizations at border restricted. Nonprofit shelters and service providers affected.

SURVEILLANCE TECHNOLOGY LAYER: The border enforcement environment operates alongside expanding smart city LiDAR infrastructure that creates passive movement surveillance of immigrant communities. LiDAR-equipped sensor networks (e.g., 86 intersections in Chattanooga, TN alone) map movement through public space with geometric precision cameras cannot match — recording movement through immigrant neighborhoods, churches, cultural gathering spaces, and markets without requiring facial identification. In the enforcement climate this order creates, smart city sensor data compounds surveillance exposure for immigrant communities already living under heightened enforcement pressure. PRC firms hold 58% of the global automotive LiDAR market, and the CRS warns that PRC-manufactured sensors in U.S. infrastructure could enable mapping accessible to Chinese state actors — creating dual risks of both domestic enforcement surveillance and foreign intelligence collection in immigrant communities.

LATINO-INDIGENOUS OVERLAP: Many individuals subject to border enforcement are Indigenous Maya, Mixtec, Zapotec, and other Mesoamerican peoples — not simply 'Latino.' The 2020 Census counted 583,981 people self-identifying as Aztec and 300,519 as Maya, while 40% of AI/AN-only respondents also identified as Hispanic (CRS IF12612). Indigenous immigrants who speak Mam, K'iche', Mixtec, or Nahuatl as their first language face compounded barriers — excluded from both English-language federal services and Spanish-language accommodations. These populations are generally NOT eligible for BIA services or IHS healthcare despite having Indigenous identity, creating double exclusion from federal service systems. Their deportation is simultaneously an immigrant community harm and an Indigenous community harm.

INDIGENOUS DEPORTEE HOUSING EXCLUSION: Indigenous immigrants (Maya, Mixtec, Zapotec, and other Mesoamerican peoples) subject to border enforcement are ineligible for NAHASDA housing assistance despite Indigenous identity. NAHASDA serves only federally recognized tribes — the 583,981 self-identified Aztec and 300,519 Maya counted in the 2020 Census are not members of U.S. federally recognized tribes and cannot access IHBG-funded housing. When deported or displaced by enforcement actions, these Indigenous individuals lose access to whatever informal housing networks they had without qualifying for the tribal housing safety net. This creates a triple exclusion: ineligible for standard federal housing assistance (immigration status), ineligible for NAHASDA tribal housing (not federally recognized tribe members), and unable to access BIA services or IHS healthcare. The intersection of border enforcement and tribal housing policy produces a population that is Indigenous, homeless, and systematically excluded from every federal housing program. (CRS IF12905)

AFRO-DESCENDANT ENFORCEMENT DIMENSION: Border enforcement disproportionately affects Afro-descendant communities from Latin America and the Caribbean. The UN estimates 200 million people of African descent live in the Americas — the vast majority in Latin America and the Caribbean. Afro-Colombian (~4.7M), Afro-Venezuelan, Afro-Honduran (Garifuna), and Afro-Cuban migrants are simultaneously 'Latin American' and 'African-descendant' — the 'Afro-descendant' framework (first adopted by regional organizations in the early 2000s) identifies a population that is both, but whose racial dimension is rendered invisible when immigration enforcement is discussed only in national-origin terms. Black migrants from Haiti, Central America, and South America face compounded racial profiling in the enforcement climate this order creates. The State Department's elimination of the 'National/Racial/Ethnic Minorities' section from Country Reports on Human Rights Practices (1993-2024) removes the federal mechanism that documented the conditions Afro-descendant migrants are fleeing — making their asylum claims harder to substantiate with U.S. government data. World Bank data (June 2025) shows higher poverty rates among Afro-descendants in Brazil, Colombia, Ecuador, Peru, and Uruguay vs. total population — the economic conditions driving migration are racially stratified in ways the eliminated reporting documented. (CRS IN11790)

INDIGENOUS DEPORTEE POPULATION BASELINES: An estimated 57.7 million Indigenous peoples live in Latin America and the Caribbean (ECLAC 2022), comprising 826 distinct groups speaking 891 languages. Guatemala — one of the top deportee origin countries — is 40-45% Indigenous (Maya, Xinca, Garifuna). Mexico has 24-28 million Indigenous peoples (INEGI/ECLAC). Honduras, El Salvador, and other Central American nations have significant Indigenous populations. ILO Convention 169 — ratified by Guatemala (1996), Honduras (1995), and Mexico (1990) — establishes consultation obligations these governments have toward Indigenous peoples, obligations that are violated when U.S. deportation operations return Indigenous individuals to conditions of structural exclusion. CRS documents that Latin American Indigenous peoples face the world's largest wage gap (31.2% lower than non-Indigenous workers) and 82.6% work in informal employment with no labor protections. Deportees returned to Guatemala's highlands, Mexico's Sierra regions, or Honduras's Mosquito Coast face some of the most severe structural poverty in the Western Hemisphere — poverty that is specifically Indigenous in character. The intersection with the Afro-descendant enforcement dimension (CRS IN11790) means that border enforcement simultaneously affects 57.7 million Indigenous peoples and approximately 200 million people of African descent in the Americas — the two largest historically excluded populations in the hemisphere — yet enforcement policy discusses neither population by name. (CRS R46225)

SOURCES: CRS Report IF12473 Version 8 (Feb. 26, 2026), https://crsreports.congress.gov/product/pdf/IF/IF12473; CRS Report IF12612 Version 3 (April 2, 2024), https://crsreports.congress.gov/product/pdf/IF/IF12612; CRS Report IF12905 Version 2 (February 11, 2025), https://crsreports.congress.gov/product/pdf/IF/IF12905; CRS Insight IN11790 Version 10 (September 18, 2025), https://crsreports.congress.gov/product/pdf/IN/IN11790; CRS Report R46225 Version 5 (Updated February 11, 2025), https://crsreports.congress.gov/product/pdf/R/R46225

actions.title: Sanctuary Cities Executive Order
actions.status: Active - Litigation Pending
actions.description:
Withholds federal funding from sanctuary jurisdictions. Targets cities and states limiting cooperation with ICE.

NONPROFIT IMPACT: Nonprofits in affected jurisdictions could lose federal funding. Social service organizations face funding cuts. Immigrant services organizations face hostile environment.

SURVEILLANCE TECHNOLOGY LAYER: Smart city LiDAR networks — the CRS documents 86-intersection deployments in a single mid-size city (Chattanooga, TN) — create passive movement surveillance that functions as a permanent, ambient registration system for all bodies moving through public space. For immigrant communities in sanctuary jurisdictions already targeted by this order, LiDAR infrastructure compounds surveillance exposure without requiring any individual to actively register or identify themselves. The technology maps spatial presence, congregation patterns, and movement through neighborhoods, markets, churches, and community centers with precision that camera systems cannot match. This passive surveillance layer operates independently of sanctuary city protections — local policies limiting ICE cooperation do not address ambient sensor infrastructure collecting movement data in public space.

INDIGENOUS LANGUAGE ACCESS CRISIS: An estimated 34,000+ Indigenous-language speakers from Latin America live in the U.S. (Census ACS), speaking languages including K'iche', Mam, Q'anjob'al, Mixtec, Zapotec, Nahuatl, and Quechua. CRS R46225 documents 891 distinct Indigenous languages across Latin America's 826 Indigenous groups. Indigenous immigrants in sanctuary jurisdictions face a three-language barrier: (1) federal services available only in English, (2) immigration proceedings conducted in Spanish, (3) their home language is Indigenous — not Spanish. Sanctuary city protections that limit ICE cooperation do not address the language access gap: even in jurisdictions that refuse to cooperate with federal enforcement, Indigenous-language speakers cannot access legal aid, social services, or due process without interpreters in languages most U.S. institutions do not provide. Court-appointed interpreters for Mam, K'iche', or Mixtec are functionally unavailable in most jurisdictions. When sanctuary protections are stripped by this order, Indigenous-language communities lose the local shield without ever having had the linguistic access to navigate the federal system they are exposed to. The 583,981 self-identified Aztec and 300,519 Maya counted in the 2020 Census (CRS IF12612) are the visible fraction of a population whose actual size is obscured by census categories that classify Indigenous Latin Americans as 'Hispanic' without capturing Indigenous identity. (CRS R46225)

SOURCES: CRS Report IF12473 Version 8 (Feb. 26, 2026), https://crsreports.congress.gov/product/pdf/IF/IF12473; CRS Report R46225 Version 5 (Updated February 11, 2025), https://crsreports.congress.gov/product/pdf/R/R46225

actions.title: PRC LiDAR in U.S. Smart City and Federal Infrastructure — Surveillance and Data Sovereignty Risks
actions.status: Active — DOD relisted Hesai under P.L. 116-283 §1260H (2025); Commerce issued PRC connected vehicle technology rules (December 2025)
actions.description:
The Department of Defense relisted Chinese LiDAR firm Hesai on its list of companies with military ties (2025, P.L. 116-283 §1260H). The Department of Commerce issued rules restricting PRC connected technology in U.S. vehicles (December 2025). PRC firms hold 58% of the global automotive LiDAR market by shipments (2020). LiDAR — the core sensing technology in smart city deployments, autonomous vehicles, and precision agriculture — creates precise 3D maps of public space, movement patterns, and infrastructure.

SURVEILLANCE DIMENSION: Smart city LiDAR deployments (e.g., Chattanooga, TN — 86 traffic intersections) create persistent, detailed spatial data about where people move, gather, and congregate. When manufactured by PRC-networked firms, this equipment may compile spatial data accessible to Chinese state actors. The CRS explicitly warns that PRC-manufactured LiDAR in government use could allow China to 'acquire sensitive information or exquisite mapping of U.S. infrastructure.' This mapping risk applies equally to any location networked into smart city systems — including immigrant neighborhoods, cultural gathering spaces, churches, and community centers.

COMMUNITY IMPACTS:
- IMMIGRANT COMMUNITIES: LiDAR-equipped smart city networks map movement patterns with precision cameras cannot match. In the current enforcement climate (ICE operations in sanctuary cities, alien registration requirements), smart city sensor data creates a passive surveillance layer recording movement through immigrant neighborhoods and gathering spaces — without requiring facial identification, only body movement and spatial presence.
- AFRICAN AMERICAN AND LATINÉ URBAN COMMUNITIES: Smart city sensor networks are disproportionately deployed in urban areas with large communities of color. LiDAR adds geometric precision to surveillance in communities already facing discriminatory law enforcement and racial profiling.
- INDIGENOUS AND TRIBAL COMMUNITIES: LiDAR is the dominant technology for surveying sacred sites, tribal lands, and archaeological resources. When PRC-manufactured equipment generates spatial data about sovereign tribal lands, data sovereignty is compromised — ownership, access, and use of that data is not governed by tribal consent.
- CHINESE AMERICAN / ASIAN AMERICAN COMMUNITIES: The national security framing of Chinese technology companies intensifies anti-Asian racial profiling. The CRS documents specific firms and specific illicit practices — the appropriate response is targeted enforcement, not broad national-origin suspicion of Chinese American researchers in photonics, autonomous systems, and related STEM fields.
- RURAL / AGRICULTURAL COMMUNITIES: LiDAR drives precision farming and autonomous agricultural equipment. Latiné farmworkers face displacement by autonomous systems, while rural communities face infrastructure security risks from PRC LiDAR components in agricultural technology.

HERITAGE MAPPING DIMENSION: LiDAR is the dominant technology used to survey archaeological sites, map sacred landscapes, produce baseline data for NHPA Section 106 consultations, and document disaster damage to cultural properties. PRC firm Hesai co-led development of U.S. and international LiDAR standards (co-developed U.S. automotive LiDAR standard 2020; co-led global optical radiation safety standards 2022). Heritage professionals and federal cultural resource managers (NPS, BLM, Army Corps, tribal historic preservation offices) using affected equipment face unacknowledged data sovereignty risks for the sacred site spatial data they generate.

ANTI-ASIAN SCAPEGOATING CAUTION: The CRS report identifies named firms (Hesai, Robosense, NavTech) and specific acquisition tactics (reverse mergers, below-cost pricing, antitrust blocking). Chinese Americans are not those firms. The gap between the specific threat (documented corporate IP theft and military ties) and the broad policy response (national-origin visa revocations, STEM surveillance) is the civil rights harm. Hesai incorporated in the U.S. as 'American LiDAR' in 2024 — obscuring PRC origins. Robosense joined University of Michigan MCity research center in 2024.

KEY FIGURES: PRC global automotive LiDAR market share: 58% (2020). Hesai Shanghai expansion credit line: $98.4M. Quanergy reverse merger: $1.4B valuation to $3.2M bankruptcy sale (to LLC with possible Russia ties). DOD relisting: 2025 under P.L. 116-283 §1260H. Commerce PRC vehicle tech rules: December 2025.

POLICY GAP: DOD and Commerce have acted on military and automotive LiDAR risks, but no comparable civilian smart city procurement rules exist — creating an unaddressed surveillance infrastructure vulnerability in the communities this tracker documents as most exposed.

SOURCE: CRS Report IF12473 Version 8 (Updated February 26, 2026). https://crsreports.congress.gov/product/pdf/IF/IF12473
actions.affected_targets: Chinese Historical Society of America,Japanese American National Museum,Tribal Libraries (28+ institutions)

actions.title: Collapse of Federal AI/AN Population Data Infrastructure
actions.doc_type: Agency Action (Pre-existing failure compounded by current administration)
actions.status: Active — No biennial report since 2013; DOL has produced zero reports since P.L. 115-93 transfer; DOGE workforce cuts worsening
actions.description:
Federal agencies allocate funding and services to tribal communities based on AI/AN population data. Three systems exist — tribal enrollment, BIA service population, and Census Bureau counts — and they produce significantly different figures (ranging from ~2 million to ~9.7 million). No comprehensive federal biennial tribal population report has been published since BIA last published one in 2013. Congress transferred this responsibility to DOL via P.L. 115-93; as of early 2024, DOL had not published a single report.

DATA INFRASTRUCTURE BREAKDOWN:
- TRIBAL ENROLLMENT: Each tribe controls its own enrollment criteria (tribal sovereignty). BIA collects but does not publicly report tribal enrollment data. Tribes face documented FOIA vulnerability — enrollment data submitted to BIA for service purposes can be compelled for disclosure. This creates real disincentives for accurate data sharing.
- BIA SERVICE POPULATION (~2 million across ~326 reservations): Defined under the Snyder Act (25 U.S.C. §13) and 25 C.F.R. §20.100 as tribal individuals on/near reservations. Only tribes with reservations can report — Alaska Natives and many Oklahoma tribes are systematically undercounted. Service population data not publicly available below the national level.
- CENSUS AI/AN COUNT (9,666,058 in 2020): Relies on self-identification using OMB racial/ethnic categories. 61% of AI/AN respondents identified as multiracial. 40% of AI/AN-only respondents also identified as Hispanic. Next decennial census: 2030.

CURRENT THREATS COMPOUNDING PRE-EXISTING CRISIS:
- BIA has lost approximately 20% of workforce per tracker entries. Staff eliminated include those who maintain service population data, conduct tribal consultations about data methodology, and process enrollment submissions from 574 nations.
- DOL workforce reductions through DOGE make publication of the mandated biennial report even less likely, extending a 10+ year data gap indefinitely.
- OMB's role in DEI elimination enforcement creates direct risk to AI/AN definitional protections. OMB currently defines AI/AN as 'individuals with origins in any of the original peoples of North, Central, and South America.' Any revision through OMB rulemaking authority being used for DEI elimination would affect how 9.7 million people are counted and whether they qualify for federal programs.
- Census Bureau workforce reductions degrade the only source of granular tribal affiliation data.

DATA SOVEREIGNTY DIMENSION: Tribal enrollment data is sovereign data, not federal government data. BIA's possession creates a custodial relationship, not an ownership relationship. Not all tribes have the resources and trained personnel to collect consistent population data meeting federal methodological requirements — creating a structural disadvantage where better-resourced tribes receive more accurate counts and therefore more funding.

LATINO-INDIGENOUS OVERLAP: 40% of AI/AN-only Census respondents also identified as Hispanic. 583,981 people self-identified as Aztec (second-largest AI/AN group nationally) and 300,519 as Maya (fourth-largest). These Indigenous Mexicans and Central Americans are simultaneously counted in Latiné and AI/AN data but generally NOT eligible for BIA services or IHS healthcare — doubly excluded from federal service systems. Immigration enforcement entries should cross-reference this population.

TREATY RIGHTS DIMENSION: Data degradation is a treaty rights violation. Federal funding to tribes is allocated based on population data that federal agencies collect and maintain. When that data infrastructure is defunded, delayed, or manipulated, tribes are systematically undercounted and underfunded for services they are owed under treaty and statute.

KEY POPULATION BASELINES (CRS IF12612):
- Cherokee self-identified (2020 Census): 1,513,326 vs. Cherokee Nation enrolled (~450,000) — illustrates Census/enrollment gap
- Navajo Nation self-identified (2020): 423,412
- Alaska Native self-identified (2020): 241,797 — many lack reservation-based service population data
- Aztec self-identified (2020): 583,981 — reflects Latino-Indigenous overlap
- Maya self-identified (2020): 300,519 — reflects Latino-Indigenous overlap

HOUSING-DATA NEXUS: Population data degradation directly compounds the tribal housing crisis. NAHASDA's Indian Housing Block Grant uses a multi-variable formula that depends on accurate population data — including AI/AN households with housing problems, AI/AN population in the area, and tribal income data. When the population data infrastructure documented above is broken (no biennial report since 2013, BIA service population unavailable below national level, Census self-identification diverging from enrollment), the IHBG formula allocates ~$1.261 billion (FY2024) using outdated or inaccurate inputs. Small tribes receiving less than $200,000 are most vulnerable: they lack capacity to independently verify population counts that determine their allocation, and BIA workforce cuts (~20%) eliminate the technical assistance staff who help tribes navigate formula challenges. The Cherokee enrollment-vs-Census gap (450,000 enrolled vs. 1.5M+ self-identified) has direct IHBG consequences: formula variables could produce dramatically different allocations depending on which population measure is used. Housing data and population data are inseparable — fixing one without the other perpetuates miscounting and misallocation. (CRS IF12905)

FOOD DATA INFRASTRUCTURE PARALLEL: The tribal population data crisis has a direct food security parallel. USDA's Economic Research Service terminated its 30-year food insecurity survey in fall 2025 — the only federal mechanism tracking racial food insecurity disparities (Black households: 24.4% vs. White: 10.1% in 2024, last data). The tribal population data gap (no biennial report since 2013) and the food data gap (no future food insecurity measurement) compound: FDPIR formula allocations ($239M FY2026 to 107 ITOs) and SNAP tribal participation data both depend on the same degraded population infrastructure. SNAP work requirements (effective December 31, 2026) will disproportionately affect reservation communities with 50%+ unemployment, but there will be no federal data to measure the food insecurity impact on tribal populations whose population counts are already inaccurate. The data crisis is now multi-dimensional: population data (BIA, since 2013), food security data (ERS, since 2025), and the formula-driven programs (IHBG, FDPIR, SNAP) all use broken inputs simultaneously. (CRS R42353)

SOURCES: CRS Report IF12612 Version 3 (April 2, 2024), https://crsreports.congress.gov/product/pdf/IF/IF12612; CRS Report IF12905 Version 2 (February 11, 2025), https://crsreports.congress.gov/product/pdf/IF/IF12905; CRS Report R42353 Version 31 (February 19, 2026), https://crsreports.congress.gov/product/pdf/R/R42353

actions.title: 118th Congress Smithsonian Expansion Legislation Stalled; 119th Congress Moves to Restrict
actions.status: All expansion bills stalled except P.L. 118-144 (Weitzman commission); restriction legislation (H.R. 3840/S. 2385) introduced in 119th Congress
actions.description:
The 118th Congress (2023-2024) introduced nine pieces of substantive Smithsonian expansion legislation representing African American, LGBTQ+, Latiné, women's, Jewish American, and arts communities. Only one — P.L. 118-144 (Weitzman National Museum of American Jewish History commission study) — became law. The 119th Congress introduced H.R. 3840/S. 2385 to permanently restrict what these same museums can present. This legislative arc documents a shift from community expansion to content restriction within a single Congress.

STALLED EXPANSION BILLS (118th Congress — none became law):
- H.R. 765/S. 233 (African American History Act): Would have expanded NMAAHC's educational mandate with statutory authority to develop resources for educators, students, and families on African American history. Sponsors: Rep. Jamaal Bowman / Sen. Cory Booker.
- H.R. 1349 (African Burial Ground Museum): Would have established a memorial museum at the African Burial Ground National Monument in NYC — site of 400+ enslaved and free Black New Yorkers from the 17th-18th centuries — affiliated with NMAAHC. Sponsor: Rep. Daniel Goldman.
- H.R. 240 (National Jazz Preservation Act): Would have established permanent jazz preservation and education programs at NMAH. Sponsor: Rep. Sheila Jackson Lee (among her final legislative contributions before her death in July 2024).
- H.R. 10190 (Juneteenth Museum): Would have directed Secretary to study feasibility of a Juneteenth Museum in Galveston, TX — the historic site of Juneteenth itself — potentially affiliated with NMAAHC. Sponsor: Rep. Randy Weber.
- H.R. 6101 (National Museum of American LGBTQI+ History and Culture Act): Would have established a congressionally authorized LGBTQI+ museum within the Smithsonian. Sponsor: Rep. Mark Pocan.
- H.R. 6102 (LGBTQI+ Museum Commission Study): Would have established a bipartisan commission to study feasibility of LGBTQI+ museum, including Smithsonian affiliation. Sponsor: Rep. Mark Pocan.
- H.R. 4273 (LGBTQI+ and Women's History Education Act): Would have directed NMAH to develop educational resources on LGBTQI+ and women's history for educators, students, and families. Sponsor: Rep. Becca Balint.

CONTENT RESTRICTION BILL (118th Congress — carried into 119th):
- H.R. 9274 (SHAWL Act): Introduced 'political viewpoint diversity' requirements as a condition of NMAL and Women's History Museum siting authorization, including biennial Secretary compliance reports on exhibit revisions. Sponsor: Rep. Nicole Malliotakis. This provision was carried directly into S. 1304/H.R. 1330 (119th Congress, same lead House sponsor), establishing the legislative genealogy of the content restriction mechanism now threatening NMAL.

ENACTED (118th Congress):
- P.L. 118-144 (Weitzman Museum Commission): Established commission to study transfer of Weitzman National Museum of American Jewish History to Smithsonian. Moved from introduction to enactment in 9 months (March-December 2024). The only Smithsonian expansion legislation enacted.

COMPARATIVE URGENCY: The Weitzman commission passed in 9 months with bipartisan support. Commission studies for an LGBTQI+ museum (H.R. 6102), feasibility studies for a Juneteenth Museum (H.R. 10190), and expansion of NMAAHC's educational mandate (H.R. 765/S. 233) received no comparable action. This documents unequal legislative speed across community representation priorities.

119TH CONGRESS REVERSAL: No stalled expansion bill has been reintroduced in the 119th Congress. The only 118th Congress Smithsonian bills that moved forward are P.L. 118-144 (already enacted) and the SHAWL Act's content restriction mechanism (reintroduced as S. 1304/H.R. 1330). H.R. 3840/S. 2385 would impose permanent statutory content restrictions on the very institutions Congress was simultaneously building toward expanding. The African American community's legislative arc: expansion (H.R. 765/S. 233, H.R. 1349, H.R. 10190, H.R. 240) → restriction (H.R. 3840/S. 2385). The LGBTQ+ community's arc: zero institutional representation (H.R. 6101/6102/4273 all stalled) → active erasure (EO 14253, H.R. 3840).

SOURCE: CRS Report IF12719 Version 7 (December 17, 2024). https://crsreports.congress.gov/product/pdf/IF/IF12719
actions.affected_targets: National Museum of African American History and Culture,National Museum of the American Latino (Planned)

actions.title: Tribal Housing Crisis: NAHASDA Authorization Lapsed 10+ Years, HUD Staff Cuts Threaten $1.26 Billion Program
actions.status: Active — Authorization expired FY2013; NHHBG expired FY2005; no 119th Congress reauthorization bill as of Feb 2025; HUD DOGE cuts worsening
actions.description:
The Indian Housing Block Grant (IHBG) — the primary federal housing program for 574 federally recognized tribes — operates without statutory authorization for the 12th consecutive year. Congress has appropriated funds annually (FY2024: $1,111M formula + $150M competitive = $1.261B total), but has not reauthorized the program since 2008 (P.L. 110-411). Reauthorization bills were introduced in every Congress from the 113th through the 118th; none was enacted. The Senate-passed FY2024 NDAA contained NAHASDA reauthorization provisions — they were removed in conference. No reauthorization bill has been introduced in the 119th Congress as of February 2025 — the first Congress since 2013 to open without one filed.

HOUSING CRISIS BASELINE: AI/AN communities face overcrowding and physically inadequate housing at dramatically higher rates than the national average (roughly 3-4x for some measures). Trust land legal status — a direct consequence of federal policy — prevents standard mortgage financing for tribal families. NAHASDA's Title VI Loan Guarantee Program (95% HUD guarantee) exists because the federal government created the barrier it must remedy. Title VI utilization is minimal: HUD guaranteed only 2 loans totaling $8 million in FY2023 against $787M in formula grants — suggesting administrative barriers, limited program awareness, or insufficient underlying IHBG grants to support collateral needs.

FUNDING STRUCTURE: IHBG formula grants were essentially flat in nominal terms from FY2015 ($643M) through FY2020 ($646M) — declining in real dollars every year. The FY2024 appropriation of $1,111M formula grants represents the first time real funding reached FY2003 inflation-adjusted levels in two decades. This historic increase is now at risk from budget rescissions, continuing resolutions at lower levels, or DOGE-driven impoundment.

GRANT DISTRIBUTION: ~600 total grantees. 180 grantees receive less than $200,000 — typically small tribes with acute needs but limited administrative capacity. 45 grantees receive more than $5 million. Largest: Navajo Nation ($133M, more than 2.5x the next-highest). Second: Cherokee Nation ($49M). The extreme range means small tribes face disproportionate harm from any HUD administrative dysfunction.

HUD WORKFORCE IMPACT: HUD administers the IHBG directly. DOGE workforce cuts eliminate staff who: administer formula allocation reviews for complex, multi-variable calculations; provide technical assistance to the 180 smallest grantees; review competitive IHBG applications ($150M/year since FY2018); process Title VI loan guarantees; and work with tribes on undisbursed funds penalties that can reduce future allocations for capacity-limited tribes. For small tribes that depend on HUD technical assistance, staff cuts are functionally equivalent to program cuts.

NATIVE HAWAIIAN HOUSING: The Native Hawaiian Housing Block Grant (NHHBG) — $22.3M FY2024 to Hawaii's Department of Hawaiian Home Lands for low-income Native Hawaiians — has an authorization that expired after FY2005, making it even more legally vulnerable than the main NAHASDA programs.

CULTURAL INFRASTRUCTURE DIMENSION: Housing is not merely shelter for Indigenous communities — it is the physical space of cultural transmission. Overcrowded homes disrupt Native language transmission across generations. Physically inadequate housing prevents ceremonial practice requiring specific spatial configurations. Housing instability that forces community members to leave tribal areas severs connections to sacred landscapes. Extended family living arrangements central to many tribal cultures are disrupted by housing sized for nuclear families. NAHASDA funding degradation threatens the physical conditions necessary for cultural continuity.

TRUST RESPONSIBILITY: The trust land mortgage barrier is a federal-created problem. The same federal government that holds tribal land in trust (protecting sovereignty) simultaneously prevents that land from serving as mortgage collateral (blocking standard homeownership). NAHASDA represents the federal acknowledgment of this obligation. When NAHASDA is underfunded, its authorization lapses, or HUD loses administrative capacity, the federal government compounds harm it created.

EXPENDITURE BREAKDOWN (FY2023, $648.1M total): Indian Housing Assistance (operation/modernization of pre-NAHASDA units): 37%. Development (construction/rehabilitation): 24%. Planning and Administration: 15%. Housing Management Services: 13%. Housing Services (rental assistance, counseling): 8%. Crime Prevention and Safety: 2%. Model Activities: 1%.

FOOD-HOUSING NEXUS: NAHASDA housing inadequacy directly compounds tribal food insecurity. Overcrowded homes lack kitchen infrastructure for traditional food preparation and storage. FDPIR commodity packages ($239M FY2026, 58,000 households through 107 ITOs) require adequate kitchen and storage space that physically inadequate reservation housing often cannot provide. When H.R. 1 SNAP work requirements (effective December 31, 2026) cause mass disenrollment on reservations with 50%+ unemployment, tribal families lose SNAP's flexible purchasing power and must rely on FDPIR commodity packages — in homes that may lack the physical capacity to store and prepare them. OAA Title VI ($40M) serves 101,000 tribal elders with congregate and home-delivered meals; for elders in inadequate housing, home-delivered meals are both nutrition and the only hot meal available in a home without functioning cooking facilities. The compound crisis is circular: inadequate housing degrades food preparation capacity; inadequate food programs force reliance on commodity packages requiring storage infrastructure; housing instability that forces community members off tribal land severs access to FDPIR (which operates through ITOs on/near reservations). Three authorization lapses compound: NAHASDA (FY2013), child nutrition (FY2015), OAA (FY2024). (CRS R42353)

SOURCES: CRS Report IF12905 Version 2 (February 11, 2025), https://crsreports.congress.gov/product/pdf/IF/IF12905; CRS Report R42353 Version 31 (February 19, 2026), https://crsreports.congress.gov/product/pdf/R/R42353

actions.title: State Department Eliminates 'National/Racial/Ethnic Minorities' Section from Country Reports on Human Rights Practices (1993-2024)
actions.status: Implemented — Section absent from Country Reports beginning 2024 cycle; 31-year reporting record terminated
actions.description:
From 1993 to 2024, the U.S. Department of State included a 'National/Racial/Ethnic Minorities' section in the annual Country Reports on Human Rights Practices to Congress — the primary federal mechanism for monitoring Afro-descendant, Indigenous, and ethnic minority human rights conditions in 190+ countries. The section no longer appears in the Country Reports. Congress mandated these reports under the Foreign Assistance Act; no congressional directive authorized removing this section.

WHAT THE SECTION DOCUMENTED: The minorities section tracked conditions for racial, ethnic, and national minority communities in every country where the U.S. maintains diplomatic relations. For Latin America, it was the primary vehicle through which Congress received systematic federal documentation of Afro-descendant poverty, discrimination, and violence in Brazil, Colombia, Ecuador, Peru, Uruguay, and across the region. For all regions, it documented Indigenous community human rights and conditions for ethnic minorities in 190+ countries.

DEI ELIMINATION CONNECTION: The removal of the racial and ethnic minorities section from the Country Reports is the foreign policy arm of EO 14151 (Ending DEI). The domestic DEI elimination orders removed racial equity reporting from federal agency operations. The State Department's Country Reports change removes racial equity monitoring from U.S. foreign policy documentation. These are parallel actions with the same logic — racial minority monitoring reframed as DEI and eliminated.

CONGRESSIONAL OVERSIGHT LOSS: The Country Reports on Human Rights Practices is a statutory requirement — Congress mandated it under the Foreign Assistance Act for foreign policy and foreign aid oversight. The State Department's executive decision to remove the minorities section is an executive modification of a congressionally mandated reporting framework. Congress never directed the removal of this section. Congress expressed institutional concern for Afro-descendant human rights through H.Con.Res. 175 (109th Congress) and related oversight hearings in 2005 — the elimination contradicts this recorded congressional interest.

ICERD OBLIGATIONS: The United States ratified the International Convention on the Elimination of All Forms of Racial Discrimination (ICERD) in 1994. ICERD requires states parties to submit periodic reports on measures taken to ensure racial non-discrimination. Eliminating the minorities section from Country Reports — the primary federal mechanism for monitoring racial and ethnic discrimination internationally — is inconsistent with U.S. reporting obligations under this binding treaty.

DURBAN FRAMEWORK: The 2002 UN Durban Declaration established that 'poverty, underdevelopment, marginalization, social exclusion and economic disparities are closely associated with racism, racial discrimination, xenophobia and related intolerance, and contribute to the persistence of racist attitudes and practices which in turn generate more poverty.' The State Department's minorities reporting was the U.S. government's primary mechanism for implementing the monitoring commitments implicit in this international framework. Its elimination signals U.S. withdrawal from international anti-racism monitoring infrastructure.

AFRO-DESCENDANT POPULATION SCALE: The United Nations estimates approximately 200 million people of African descent live in the Americas — the vast majority outside the United States, primarily in Latin America and the Caribbean. Brazil alone has approximately 120 million people identifying as Black or mixed-race (~56% of population). Colombia has approximately 4.7 million Afro-descendants. The 'Afro-descendant' term — first adopted by regional organizations in the early 2000s — describes people united by common African ancestry living in widely varying conditions, including Afro-Brazilian, Afro-Colombian, Afro-Cuban, Afro-Puerto Rican, Afro-Dominican, Garifuna, Afro-Ecuadorian, Afro-Peruvian, and Afro-Venezuelan communities. World Bank LAC Equity Lab data (June 2025) shows a higher percentage of Afro-descendants are poor (below $8.30/day in 2021 PPP terms) compared to the total population in Brazil, Colombia, Ecuador, Peru, and Uruguay — with disparities tracking across educational attainment, access to water, electricity, and internet.

DIASPORA DIMENSION: U.S. Afro-Latino communities (Afro-Cuban, Afro-Puerto Rican, Afro-Dominican, Garifuna) have family and cultural connections to the Latin American countries the Country Reports covered. The elimination of Afro-descendant monitoring in those reports removes documentation these diaspora communities relied on to understand conditions in their countries of origin and to advocate for those affected.

TERRITORIAL RIGHTS AND BIODIVERSITY: Afro-descendant collective lands in Brazil, Colombia, Ecuador, and Suriname contribute to biodiversity conservation and deforestation prevention (Communications Earth & Environment, 2025). Colombia's Afro-Colombian collective territories under Law 70 of 1993 represent the most developed legal framework. These communities face threats from extractive industries, agricultural expansion, and weak state protection — the Latin American parallel to Indigenous sacred site threats in North America. U.S. withdrawal from international environmental and human rights monitoring weakens the frameworks protecting these territories.

INTERNATIONAL MONITORING CONTEXT: A UN Working Group of Experts on People of African Descent is currently drafting a 'Declaration on the Promotion and Full Respect of the Human Rights of People of African Descent.' The UN Permanent Forum of People of African Descent held its third session in April 2024. The OAS Rapporteurship on Rights of Persons of African Descent has held 100+ hearings since 1996. U.S. actions affecting Afro-descendant communities are being documented and assessed by these bodies precisely as those actions are most harmful.

MATERNAL HEALTH DIMENSION: The UN Population Fund, UNICEF, UN Women, PAHO, and National Birth Equity Collaborative jointly published 'Maternal Health Analysis of Women and Girls of African Descent in the Americas' (2023), documenting hemispheric maternal health disparities for Afro-descendant women. The same racial disparities in maternal mortality documented domestically exist throughout Latin America and the Caribbean. U.S. withdrawal from international health cooperation compounds these disparities.

INDIGENOUS-SPECIFIC STATUTORY MANDATE — P.L. 102-391 (1992): One year before the 'National/Racial/Ethnic Minorities' section was created, Congress enacted P.L. 102-391 (1992) requiring U.S. State Department Country Reports to specifically describe 'the extent to which Indigenous people are able to participate in decisions affecting their lands, cultures, traditions and the allocation of natural resources' and 'assess the extent of protection of their civil and political rights.' This is a separate, prior, and more specific congressional directive that has no expiration date and was not repealed by any executive order. Its omission from the 2024 Country Reports constitutes a potential independent statutory violation — compounding the minorities section elimination with a second concurrent congressional mandate violation.

INDIGENOUS POPULATION SCALE: ECLAC (2022) estimates 57.7 million Indigenous peoples in Latin America — 9.5% of the regional population, comprising 826 Indigenous groups speaking 891 languages across 20 countries. Guatemala: 40-45% Indigenous population. Bolivia: 40-50%. Mexico: 15-20% (24-28 million). The Country Reports' Indigenous-specific mandate (P.L. 102-391) was designed to monitor these communities. Its elimination removes monitoring at the moment USAID programs protecting Indigenous land rights in Guatemala, Honduras, Ecuador, and Peru have been suspended. ILO Convention No. 169 — ratified by 14 Latin American countries including Guatemala, Honduras, and Mexico — requires governments to consult Indigenous peoples on measures affecting them. The U.S. is not party to ILO 169 but its enforcement actions return deportees to ILO 169 signatory countries without consultation protocols.

IACHR MONITORING: The Inter-American Commission on Human Rights has held over 250 hearings since 1996 on Indigenous peoples' rights through its Rapporteurship established in 1990 — the oldest active regional monitoring mechanism. Combined with the OAS Rapporteurship on Afro-descendant rights (100+ hearings since 1996), the two monitoring systems document exactly the populations the U.S. Country Reports eliminated from coverage.

COMPANION FRAMEWORK: CRS R46225 (Indigenous Peoples in Latin America, same author as IN11790) and CRS IN11790 (Afro-descendants of Latin America) together establish the two populations most systematically excluded from standard Latin American policy discussions — and the two most directly harmed by U.S. foreign policy rollback, mass deportation, and immigration enforcement. Combined: ~57.7 million Indigenous + ~200 million Afro-descendants = the majority populations of many Latin American nations, monitored by two congressional mandates, both eliminated simultaneously.

SOURCES: CRS Insight IN11790 Version 10 (Updated September 18, 2025), https://crsreports.congress.gov/product/pdf/IN/IN11790; CRS Report R46225 Version 11 (Updated September 10, 2025), https://crsreports.congress.gov/product/pdf/R/R46225

actions.title: USDA Terminates 30-Year Food Insecurity Monitoring Survey — Last Official Racial Disparity Data Published December 2025
actions.status: Implemented — Survey terminated; 2024 findings (December 2025) are last data release; no replacement monitoring announced
actions.description:
USDA's Economic Research Service terminated the annual Food Security Supplement to the Census Current Population Survey — the only federal mechanism for measuring household food insecurity across all demographic groups. The survey ran annually since 1995 (30 years). USDA announced termination September 20, 2025, calling it 'redundant.' The 2024 findings released December 2025 are the last official data.

FINAL FOOD INSECURITY BASELINE (2024 — last official federal data):
- 13.7% of U.S. households (18.3 million) food insecure at some point during 2024
- 49.7 million individuals lived in food insecure households
- 11.1 million households experienced low food security
- 7.2 million households experienced very low food security
- Food insecure households experienced insecurity in 7 months of the year on average
- Nearly 60% of food insecure households participated in SNAP, WIC, or NSLP in prior month

RACIAL/ETHNIC DISPARITIES — LAST OFFICIAL FEDERAL DATA:
- Black, non-Hispanic headed households: 24.4% food insecure
- Hispanic headed households: 20.2% food insecure
- White, non-Hispanic headed households: 10.1% food insecure
- The 2.4:1 Black-White disparity ratio is the last official federal data point. It will not be updated.

CHILDREN:
- 18.4% of households with children (6.7 million households) were food insecure
- 7.3 million children in 3.3 million households experienced food insecurity
- 751,000 children in 318,000 households experienced very low food security — meaning they were hungry, skipped meals, or went a full day without eating because there was not enough money for food

SENIORS:
- 9.9% of households with an older adult (4.4 million households) food insecure
- 11.3% of older adults living alone were food insecure

UNDERCOUNT: CRS notes these figures are likely underestimates — CPS uses address-based sampling and does not capture homeless individuals or those in temporary housing.

CATASTROPHIC TIMING: H.R. 1/P.L. 119-21 (signed July 4, 2025) cuts SNAP by $187 billion over 10 years. CBO projects 2.4 million people will lose benefits. SNAP work requirements for ages 18-64 (80+ hours/month) take effect December 31, 2026. There will be no federal monitoring of the impact of these cuts on food insecurity rates, no racial disparity tracking, no way to measure whether the 751,000 children in very low food security became more or fewer. The monitoring system for the harm is being eliminated at the moment the harm is being intensified.

PARALLEL TO STATE DEPARTMENT COUNTRY REPORTS: This is the domestic food security equivalent of what the tracker documents with the State Department's elimination of the 'National/Racial/Ethnic Minorities' section from Country Reports on Human Rights Practices (1993-2024, CRS IN11790). Both terminate 30+ year monitoring mechanisms. Both eliminate racial disparity data. Both occur precisely when the policies they would measure are intensifying. Both are executive agency decisions with no congressional directive authorizing the termination.

ZAKAT AND RELIGIOUS FOOD NETWORK INTERSECTION: Zakat — mandatory charitable giving, the third pillar of Islam — is a religious obligation for 4.5 million American Muslims (2020 U.S. Religion Census) to support 'those who have less,' including through distribution of food and gifts during Eid celebrations. Zakat is not discretionary philanthropy; it is as fundamental to Islamic practice as Ramadan fasting or the five daily prayers. Muslim zakat networks — mosques, Islamic centers, Muslim charitable organizations — serve as community-level food assistance infrastructure that supplements federal programs. H.R. 1's $187 billion in SNAP cuts (CBO: 2.4 million people lose benefits) and the termination of federal food insecurity monitoring increase demands on exactly these religious charity networks. When federal food assistance is cut, zakat institutions absorb overflow — but without federal monitoring data, neither the scale of increased demand on religious food networks nor the adequacy of community response can be measured. The halal food dimension compounds this: Muslims purchase and distribute meat 'sacrificed according to halal standards' (CRS R45003) during Eid. Both Islam and Judaism require religiously supervised food preparation (halal and kosher), creating joint vulnerability to any USDA regulatory changes affecting religious slaughter practices. The intersection of federal food assistance cuts, religious food obligation, and terminated monitoring constitutes an undocumented compound threat to Muslim and Jewish community food practice. (CRS R45003)

TRIBAL COLLEGE FOOD SOVEREIGNTY PIPELINE: USDA administers $34.880M in annual 1994 Land-Grant Tribal College programs (P.L. 103-382) — including the Tribal College Extension Program supporting tribal farmer education and community-based agricultural learning. This is the TCU equivalent of the USDA Agricultural Extension Service at every land-grant university, specifically supporting tribal food sovereignty — traditional agriculture, subsistence farming, and community food systems. USDA workforce reductions under DOGE threaten these programs at the same time SNAP cuts ($187B over 10 years), FDPIR mutual exclusivity (7 U.S.C. §2013(b)), and food insecurity monitoring termination compound food access crises in the same tribal communities. The 32 tribally controlled colleges — many in the Great Lakes, Plains, Southwest, and Alaska — are the institutional infrastructure for tribal agricultural knowledge transmission. Their USDA funding pipeline connects to the food-housing-cultural continuity nexus documented across tracker entries: traditional food practices require agricultural knowledge (USDA extension), kitchen infrastructure (NAHASDA housing), and food access (SNAP/FDPIR). Cutting all three simultaneously dismantles the complete food sovereignty system. (CRS IF10554)

LATIN AMERICAN STATISTICAL INVISIBILITY PARALLEL: The termination of food insecurity monitoring replicates a pattern CRS R46225 documents across Latin America: the systematic rendering of vulnerable populations invisible through statistical elimination. CRS notes that '20th century Latin American nations pursued policies of assimilation, forced labor, and land seizure that made Indigenous peoples invisible in national statistics.' The U.S. is now applying the same technique domestically — not through census category manipulation, but through survey termination. The 2024 ERS data showing 24.4% Black household food insecurity and 20.2% Hispanic household food insecurity are the last federal measurements. ECLAC's experience documenting Latin American Indigenous populations shows what happens when statistical visibility is lost: it took decades of international pressure (ILO Convention 169, 1989; UN Declaration on Indigenous Rights, 2007; Inter-American Court rulings) to force governments to re-count populations they had made invisible. The U.S. food insecurity monitoring termination eliminates racial disparity data for 49.7 million food-insecure individuals — including Indigenous, Afro-descendant, and Latiné populations whose counterparts in Latin America are only now regaining statistical visibility after generations of erasure. (CRS R46225)

FY2026 FOOD ASSISTANCE FUNDING MAP (P.L. 119-37):
- SNAP: $106.766 billion (42.4M participants/month FY2025; avg $188/person/month)
- WIC: $8.200 billion (6.9M/month; includes 32 Indian Tribal Organizations)
- National School Lunch Program: $18.692 billion (29.9M students/day in 94,000 schools)
- School Breakfast Program: $6.590 billion (16.0M students/day in 91,000 schools)
- Summer EBT: $4.357 billion (8.5M children at peak; permanently authorized 2022)
- CACFP: $4.521 billion (4.7M daily; 4.6M children, 117,600 older adults)
- TEFAP (emergency food banks): $552 million (plus $1-2B bonus foods)
- CSFP (elderly): $460 million (700,000/month including 7 ITOs)
- FDPIR (tribal): $239 million (58,000/month through 107 ITOs)
- Summer Food Service: $905 million (2.4M children/day; 35,000 sites)
- OAA Congregate Nutrition: $565 million (1.4M participants; 64.6M meals)
- OAA Home Delivered: $381 million (1.2M participants; 179.2M meals)
- OAA Title VI Native Americans: $40 million (101,000 tribal elders)
- FEMA EFSP: $117 million (73.1M meals + 2.8M lodging nights FY2022)
- TOTAL: approximately $151.3 billion in FY2026 food assistance

TERRITORIES STRUCTURAL INEQUITY: Puerto Rico (~$3B), American Samoa (~$12M), and CNMI receive fixed Nutrition Assistance Program block grants in lieu of SNAP. Unlike SNAP — which automatically increases when need rises — block grants are capped and do not expand with hurricanes, economic downturns, or federal policy changes. 3.2 million Puerto Ricans lack the federal SNAP backstop available to states.

AUTHORIZATION LAPSE COMPOUND CRISIS: Three major food assistance frameworks operate without current reauthorization — child nutrition programs (expired FY2015, P.L. 111-296), Older Americans Act nutrition (expired FY2024, P.L. 116-131), and NAHASDA tribal housing (expired FY2013, P.L. 110-411). All continue through annual appropriations. All are vulnerable to DOGE administrative capacity cuts compounding lapsed statutory authority.

FEMA EFSP — HOMELESS/HUNGER INTERSECTION: The Emergency Food and Shelter Program ($117M FY2025, $6.6B disbursed over 42 years to 14,000 local providers in 2,500+ counties) operates continuously without requiring a presidential emergency declaration. Its authorization expired FY1994 — operating on annual appropriations for 31 years. Statutory non-discrimination clause explicitly includes sexual orientation, providing a legal floor for LGBTQ+ access to emergency food and shelter.

SOURCES: CRS Report R42353 Version 31 (Updated February 19, 2026), https://crsreports.congress.gov/product/pdf/R/R42353; CRS Report R46225 Version 5 (Updated February 11, 2025), https://crsreports.congress.gov/product/pdf/R/R46225; CRS Report R45003 Version 16 (March 3, 2026), https://crsreports.congress.gov/product/pdf/R/R45003; CRS Report IF10554 Version 5 (June 25, 2024), https://crsreports.congress.gov/product/pdf/IF/IF10554

actions.title: Tribal Food Security Crisis: FDPIR/SNAP Mutual Exclusivity Creates Cliff When Work Requirements Hit Reservation Economies
actions.administration: Trump II (H.R. 1 work requirements effective date)
actions.status: Pending — H.R. 1 SNAP work requirements take effect December 31, 2026; FDPIR structurally unable to absorb overflow
actions.description:
Four simultaneous food security threats converge on tribal communities with no federal monitoring mechanism in place to measure the impact.

THREAT 1 — FDPIR/SNAP MUTUAL EXCLUSIVITY: The Food Distribution Program on Indian Reservations (FDPIR) — $239 million FY2026, serving 58,000 households/month through 3 states + 107 Indian Tribal Organizations — is mutually exclusive with SNAP by law (Food and Nutrition Act, Section 4(b), 7 U.S.C. §2013(b)). Households must choose one or the other. SNAP serves over 2 million people in tribal areas; FDPIR serves 58,000. When H.R. 1 work requirements (ages 18-64 must work/train/volunteer 80+ hours/month) take effect December 31, 2026, tribal households on reservations with 50%+ unemployment rates cannot comply — not because they refuse work, but because jobs do not exist in sufficient numbers. Mass loss of SNAP benefits is structurally certain. FDPIR cannot absorb the overflow: its 107 ITOs operate with fixed commodity budgets, not flexible entitlement spending. FDPIR provides a USDA-determined commodity package — not flexible EBT purchasing power. Tribal families cannot use FDPIR to purchase traditional foods, culturally specific ingredients, or items from Native-owned food enterprises. The food sovereignty dimension of SNAP disappears entirely under FDPIR.

THREAT 2 — WIC TRIBAL ADMINISTRATION: WIC ($8.200 billion FY2026, 6.9 million participants/month) is administered through 32 Indian Tribal Organizations in addition to states and territories. WIC serves 1.5 million infants, 3.8 million children, and 1.5 million women monthly. HHS workforce cuts (HHS lost 20,000 employees) reduce WIC administrative capacity, threatening the federal technical assistance that supports tribal WIC programs. WIC administered through ITOs is tribal self-governance of a federal nutrition program — parallel to NAHASDA's self-determination model (CRS IF12905). Loss of federal support capacity for 32 ITOs degrades tribal nutrition infrastructure.

THREAT 3 — OAA TITLE VI TRIBAL ELDER NUTRITION: OAA Title VI Grants to Native Americans — $40 million FY2026 — fund congregate and home-delivered meals for older Native Americans through tribal organizations and Hawaiian Native grantees. Program served 56,000 congregate meal participants (2.8 million meals) and 45,000 home-delivered participants (3.8 million meals) in 2023. Eligibility: age 60+. Authorization expired at the end of FY2024 (P.L. 116-131 last reauthorized 2020) — the third major program serving tribal communities operating without reauthorization alongside NAHASDA (expired FY2013) and child nutrition programs (expired FY2015). The 2020 OAA reauthorization required meals adjusted for 'cultural considerations and preferences' — a statutory protection for traditional foods (fry bread, wild rice, salmon, three sisters, bison) in tribal elder nutrition. This cultural protection has no reauthorization floor as of FY2025. As discretionary appropriations (not mandatory spending), OAA Title VI can be reduced or eliminated in any appropriations cycle.

THREAT 4 — FOOD INSECURITY MONITORING ELIMINATED: USDA terminated the 30-year food insecurity survey in fall 2025. The 2024 data is the last baseline. There will be no federal mechanism to measure whether SNAP work requirements increase food insecurity on reservations, whether FDPIR commodity packages substitute adequately for SNAP purchasing power, whether tribal elder nutrition degrades as OAA authorization lapses, or whether the compound effect of housing insecurity (NAHASDA, CRS IF12905) and food insecurity creates cascading harm in tribal communities.

CULTURAL FOOD SOVEREIGNTY: For Indigenous communities, food is not merely nutrition — it is cultural continuity. Traditional food practices (hunting, fishing, gathering, agriculture) connect communities to ancestral lands, seasonal cycles, and intergenerational knowledge transmission. SNAP's flexible purchasing power allows tribal families to buy traditional foods from Native producers; FDPIR's commodity package does not. NAHASDA housing inadequacy compounds food insecurity: overcrowded homes lack kitchen infrastructure for traditional food preparation; physically inadequate housing cannot store preserved foods. The food-housing-cultural continuity nexus means each program degradation amplifies the others.

SOURCE: CRS Report R42353 Version 31 (Updated February 19, 2026). https://crsreports.congress.gov/product/pdf/R/R42353

actions.title: Presidential Muslim Holiday Recognition Practice Potentially Broken — First Gap Since Post-9/11 Bush Initiative (2001-2025)
actions.status: Active — Ramadan 2026 began approximately March 1-2, 2026; no specific Trump II presidential statement or proclamation on Muslim holidays identified in CRS R45003 (updated March 3, 2026)
actions.description:
CRS R45003 (Updated March 3, 2026, at the start of Ramadan 2026) documents that 'recent Presidents have noted the importance of [Muslim] holidays to Muslim Americans and Muslims around the globe by making remarks or press statements.' For every prior administration — Bush, Obama, Trump I, Biden — CRS identifies specific statements or proclamations. For Trump II, CRS lists only a hyperlink to the general Presidential Proclamations search page with no specific dated statement identified. This parallels the Black History Month proclamation anomaly documented for CRS R44363, where the Trump II February 3, 2026 proclamation was 'not listed in the Compilation of Presidential Documents.'

THE BUSH PRECEDENT (2001): The practice of presidential Ramadan and Eid recognition was initiated by President George W. Bush specifically as counter-messaging to anti-Muslim backlash after September 11, 2001. Bush hosted White House iftar dinners and issued specific Ramadan statements. Every president since — including Trump I — maintained this practice. If Trump II has broken it, it would be the first presidential gap in 25 years of bipartisan recognition, during a period of explicitly anti-Muslim federal policies.

RAMADAN 2026 TEMPORAL CONTEXT: Ramadan 2026 began approximately March 1-2, 2026. Every policy documented in the tracker that adversely affects Muslim communities is currently active during the holiest month of the Islamic calendar year. Travel bans separating Muslim families are active during Ramadan 2026. Visa revocations targeting Muslim students are active during Ramadan 2026. The 75-country visa freeze is in effect during Ramadan 2026. Eid al-Fitr will be observed approximately April 1-2, 2026.

U.S. MUSLIM POPULATION BASELINE: The 2020 U.S. Religion Census counts 4.5 million Muslims in the United States (CRS R45003). This is a congregational affiliation figure — actual Muslim population estimates range from 3.5 million (Pew Research, 2017) to 8-10 million (community organizations). Muslim Americans are a multiracial community spanning Arab American (largest single ethnic group), South Asian American (Pakistani, Bangladeshi, Indian Muslim — second largest), African American Muslim (historically the largest Muslim population by ancestry in U.S. history, predating mass immigration), West African, Somali, Southeast Asian, Iranian American (majority Shia), Turkish American, and converts of all backgrounds.

FIVE PILLARS AND POLICY INTERSECTIONS: The five pillars of Islam — shahada (declaration of faith), salat (five daily prayers), zakat (mandatory charity, third pillar), sawm (Ramadan fasting, fourth pillar), and Hajj (pilgrimage to Mecca, fifth pillar, mandatory once in lifetime) — are not cultural preferences but mandatory religious obligations. Federal policies that interfere with Hajj travel (travel bans), Ramadan accommodation (federal workforce reductions, ICE detention), zakat charitable networks (post-9/11 Islamic charity designations), and mosque community building are structural interferences with mandatory religious practice protected by the First Amendment's Free Exercise Clause.

HAJJ AND TRAVEL BAN: The Hajj is mandatory for every Muslim who is physically and financially able, once in their lifetime. It can only be performed at Mecca, Saudi Arabia, at a fixed time (Dhu al-Hijjah, the 12th Islamic month). Saudi Arabia is not on the current travel ban list. However, Muslim Americans traveling to Hajj whose families are from banned countries cannot bring family members; permanent residents from banned countries face re-entry uncertainty; visa-holders from banned countries risk revocation while performing Hajj. The travel infrastructure for Hajj passes through many countries subject to the 75-country visa processing freeze.

EID STAMP SERIES (2001-2016): The U.S. Postal Service issued Eid commemorative stamps annually from 2001 through 2016 — 15 years of visible federal acknowledgment of Muslim-American belonging. The last Eid stamp was issued in 2016 (final year of Obama administration). No Eid stamp has been issued since — a 9-year absence spanning Trump I, Biden, and Trump II administrations. The stamp's 2001 initiation and 2016 cessation bookend the post-9/11 era of active federal counter-messaging against anti-Muslim backlash.

H.RES. 61 (2025) — MUSLIM-AMERICAN HERITAGE MONTH: H.Res. 61 (119th Congress, 2025) proposes congressional recognition of January as 'Muslim-American Heritage Month.' A Senate companion, S.Res. 361 (2021), proposed July. If enacted as a joint resolution — like P.L. 99-244 creating Black History Month (1986) — it would create statutory protection for Muslim-American cultural heritage that no executive order could revoke. H.Res. 61 was introduced in the same Congress that enacted travel bans on 39 Muslim-majority countries, revoked Muslim student visas, and may have broken the post-9/11 bipartisan practice of presidential Muslim holiday recognition.

TWO COMPETING RAMADAN RESOLUTIONS (2025): H.Res. 192 and H.Res. 263 — two separate House resolutions recognizing Ramadan — were introduced in the 119th Congress. Two Ramadan resolutions in the same Congress is unusual (the 118th had one: H.Res. 1128). This mirrors the 'Original Black History Month Resolution' (H.Res. 181) procedural dynamic documented for R44363 — congressional recognition continuing as contested political resistance, not routine ceremony.

SHIA-SPECIFIC TARGETING: The travel bans include Iran (majority Shia), Iraq (majority Shia), and Yemen (significant Shia Houthi population). Ashura — the central Shia holy day — is associated specifically with these communities. The ban structure disproportionately targets Shia-majority and religiously diverse Muslim countries while exempting Sunni-majority Gulf states (Saudi Arabia, UAE, Qatar). Shia Muslim Americans from Iran, Iraq, and Yemen face the intersection of being Muslim in an anti-Muslim policy climate AND being from banned countries.

LOCAL RECOGNITION AS PROTECTION FLOOR: Muslim holiday recognition is expanding at the local level precisely as federal recognition contracts. New York City (2015), Detroit (2019), Fairfax County VA (2022), Massachusetts districts (2024), Montgomery County MD (updated February 2026) have adopted Eid school closures. Local school district calendar decisions are not subject to federal executive orders — they represent the community-level institutional floor that persists beneath federal policy rollback, paralleling ASALH's private Black History Month ownership and tribal nations' self-governance.

ABRAHAMIC FRAMEWORK: Islam is one of the three major Abrahamic faiths alongside Judaism and Christianity. Both Islam and Judaism require religiously supervised food preparation (halal and kosher respectively), creating joint vulnerability to any federal interference with religious slaughter regulations or food certification. The shared Ibrahim/Abraham patriarch connects Eid al-Adha to Jewish and Christian traditions — framing Islam as 'foreign' to America denies this shared Abrahamic heritage.

SOURCE: CRS Report R45003 Version 16 (Updated March 3, 2026). https://crsreports.congress.gov/product/pdf/R/R45003
