actions.title: NPS 24% Staff Reduction Degrades Six Named Preservation Programs Including Save America's Treasures (Only Four-Agency Program) and Underrepresented Communities Pipeline
actions.status: Implemented — NPS lost 24% of permanent staff (4,000+ employees) through Valentine's Day Massacre and subsequent DOGE-directed reductions; all six programs operationally understaffed
actions.description:
NPS administers six preservation grant programs now operating with 24% fewer permanent staff. CRS R47337 (Updated February 12, 2026) identifies each program by name, statutory authority, and eligible communities — providing the specific map of what NPS's workforce reduction has degraded.

PROGRAM 1 — SAVE AMERICA'S TREASURES (SAT): The only federal preservation program jointly administered by NPS, IMLS, NEA, and NEH. NPS manages preservation grants for National Register-listed properties; IMLS manages collections grants. In 2025-2026, all four SAT co-administering agencies were simultaneously degraded: NPS lost 24% of staff; IMLS was eliminated by executive order (restored by court injunction November 2025); NEA terminated 50%+ of grants with 50% staff reduction; NEH terminated 1,400+ grants with 65% staff fired. No other federal program faces this compound inter-agency administrative failure. SAT grant cycles require NPS staff to certify National Register eligibility, IMLS staff to process collections grants, and NEA/NEH coordination — when all four are degraded simultaneously, the program's operational continuity is structurally compromised regardless of whether it has been explicitly terminated.

PROGRAM 2 — AMERICAN BATTLEFIELD PROTECTION PROGRAM (ABPP): Congressional authorization for battlefield preservation, interpretation, and restoration grants. Eligible entities include state, local, and tribal governments and nonprofit organizations. Many significant battlefields are sites of U.S. military campaigns against tribal nations — the program funding interpretation of these conflicts is among those now 24% understaffed. ABPP Battlefield Interpretation Grants fund park interpreters who explain military history to the public; those interpreters are among NPS staff categories most directly eliminated. Revolutionary War and Civil War ABPP sites are central to America250 commemoration — degraded as NEH ($7M 'A More Perfect Union') and IMLS ($1M preparation) America250 funding was also terminated.

PROGRAM 3 — JAPANESE AMERICAN CONFINEMENT SITES (JACS): P.L. 109-441 (2006) — congressionally mandated preservation and interpretation of the 10 War Relocation Authority incarceration camps (Gila River, Granada, Heart Mountain, Jerome, Manzanar, Minidoka, Poston, Rohwer, Topaz, Tule Lake) plus other significant sites. Eligible entities include tribal governments — reflecting the Alaska dimension of Japanese American WWII history (Alaska Chapter of the Japanese American Citizens League received a 2021 JACS oral history grant). Program now operating with 24% fewer NPS staff while NEH (which funded Japanese American incarceration oral histories) has terminated 1,400+ grants.

PROGRAM 4 — NORMAN Y. MINETA JAPANESE AMERICAN CONFINEMENT EDUCATION (JACE): P.L. 117-328 (2022) — authorized in the same Consolidated Appropriations Act that permanently authorized Summer EBT. Named for Norman Y. Mineta, a Japanese American WWII incarceration survivor who became a bipartisan Cabinet secretary (Secretary of Transportation under Bush, Secretary of Commerce under Clinton). Active 2025 grants include Heart Mountain Wyoming Foundation's Nikkei Community Platform — a social media tool connecting living incarceration survivors with their descendants. This is precisely the type of living witness documentation the tracker documents being canceled for Tulsa Race Massacre survivors (aged 102-110). The same administration canceling NEH oral history grants for aging Tulsa survivors had active JACE grants running at Japanese American incarceration sites when NPS workforce reductions took effect.

PROGRAM 5 — UNDERREPRESENTED COMMUNITIES GRANT PROGRAM: The federal equity pipeline to the National Register of Historic Places. Funds surveys, inventories, and nominations for sites associated with African American, Indigenous, Latino, Asian American, and Pacific Islander communities. National Register listing is the structural prerequisite for eligibility for most federal preservation funding (SAT grants, Historic Preservation Fund grants, ABPP grants, tax incentives for historic rehabilitation). NPS's 24% staff reduction eliminates the preservation specialists who provide technical assistance to communities without institutional preservation infrastructure. Structural cascade: staff cuts → fewer nominations → fewer sites eligible for preservation grants → permanent loss of underrepresented community historic sites. Example: City of Pasco, Washington received funding to examine historic properties associated with African American communities during WWII.

PROGRAM 6 — MARITIME HERITAGE GRANTS: Joint NPS-MARAD (Maritime Administration, DOT) program. NPS and MARAD both face DOGE workforce reductions, creating compound administrative failure in this co-administered program. Pacific WWII maritime heritage — naval battles at Guadalcanal, Leyte Gulf, Coral Sea, Midway Atoll — is documented through Maritime Heritage resources directly relevant to Chamorro, Filipino, and Pacific Islander WWII memory.

MONUMENT REINSTATEMENT PARADOX: EO 14253 directs DOI to reinstate monuments removed since 2020. CRS R47337 confirms 'at the federal level, there are no grant programs that provide direct funding specifically for military memorials or monuments.' The administration is ordering monument reinstatement without a federal funding mechanism, while simultaneously defunding community-specific preservation programs — Museum Grants for African American History & Culture ($6M), Native American/Native Hawaiian Museum Services ($3.772M), JACS grants, Underrepresented Communities grants — through which communities of color tell their own commemorative stories.

ARTS ECONOMIC BASELINE — PRESERVATION AS ECONOMIC INFRASTRUCTURE (CRS IF12446): The six NPS programs described above operate within the arts and culture sector that represents 4.4% of U.S. GDP — approximately $1.02 trillion in annual economic activity (BEA). The sector grew 13.7% in 2021 vs. 5.9% overall GDP, demonstrating counter-cyclical resilience that makes its defunding strategically damaging. The Historic Preservation Fund (HPF), appropriated at $204.5 million in FY2023, is the primary funding stream for preservation grants to states, tribes, local governments, and nonprofits — including the Save America's Treasures program (Program 1 above). The Federal Historic Preservation Tax Credit has leveraged approximately $130 billion in private investment across 48,000+ completed projects since 1976, making it one of the most cost-effective federal economic development programs in U.S. history. Program 5 (Underrepresented Communities) feeds properties into the National Register — the structural prerequisite for Historic Preservation Tax Credit eligibility. When NPS staff reductions eliminate the preservation specialists who process National Register nominations, they simultaneously constrict the $130 billion private investment pipeline. Heritage tourism and preservation rehabilitation generate exactly the downstream economic effects the CRS documents: activity in publishing, printing, broadcasting, and manufacturing that compounds community harm when federal partnerships are degraded. For rural, tribal, and low-income urban communities, federal preservation funding represents the primary economic development infrastructure — the CRS documents no equivalent private-sector substitute for underserved geographies. Congress explicitly recognized the arts sector as critical economic infrastructure through emergency appropriations: $75M (CARES Act, 2020), $16.25B (SVOG, 2021), and $135M in ARPA arts relief (2021) — the current degradation of NPS preservation programs reverses this congressional trajectory.

TERRITORY EQUITY GAP: U.S. states have 2-3 active grant program pathways for military history preservation; territories have minimal infrastructure. American Samoa has only a mailing address for its preservation office. The Marshall Islands has only phone and email — no website. Yet territories bear profound WWII military heritage: Guam (the only Japanese occupation of U.S. territory), Puerto Rico (centuries of Caribbean military fortifications including San Juan NHS), American Samoa (Samoan service in WWII Marine battalions). NPS's 24% staff reduction compounds this — territories with the least state-level preservation infrastructure simultaneously lose their federal NPS support.

VA VETERANS CEMETERY TRIBAL DIMENSION: The VA Veterans Cemetery Grant program specifically authorizes grants to federally recognized tribes and territories for establishing and improving tribal and territorial veterans cemeteries. Indigenous peoples serve at the highest per-capita military rate of any demographic group. NARA's 100+ staff RIF and $50M budget cut delay the military service records tribal veterans' families need to access VA cemetery benefits.

NPS FY2025 APPROPRIATIONS BASELINE (CRS IF12713): P.L. 119-4 (Full-Year Continuing Appropriations and Extensions Act, 2025, enacted March 15, 2025) provided $3.337 billion total discretionary appropriations to NPS for FY2025 — slightly above (+<1%) the FY2024 enacted level of $3.325 billion (P.L. 118-42), but 7% BELOW the Biden Administration's FY2025 request of $3.576 billion — a gap of approximately $239 million. This means NPS was already significantly underfunded BEFORE DOGE cuts began. Account breakdown: (1) Operation of the National Park System (ONPS): $2.894 billion (87% of discretionary) — $196 million below request; covers resource stewardship, visitor services, park protection, facility operations; (2) Construction: $172.3 million — 27% below the $237.2M request, directly worsening the $22.3 billion deferred maintenance backlog (CRS R42757, FY2022); (3) Historic Preservation Fund (HPF): $168.9 million — DOWN 10% FROM FY2024's $188.7M — a critical cut because HPF formula grants flow directly to State Historic Preservation Offices (SHPOs) and Tribal Historic Preservation Offices (THPOs) serving 574 federally recognized tribes under NHPA (54 U.S.C. §§300101 et seq.); (4) National Recreation and Preservation (NR&P): $89.6 million — down 2% from FY2024's $91.2M — funds grants to state, local, tribal, and private land managers for cultural preservation and outdoor recreation planning, including national heritage areas; (5) Centennial Challenge: $12.0 million. NPS estimated $1.240 billion in mandatory appropriations for FY2025 (1% decrease from FY2024), funded from entrance/recreation fees, concessioner fees, donations, and LWCF (designated mandatory by GAOA P.L. 116-152).

LEGACY RESTORATION FUND SUNSET — MOST CRITICAL NEW FINDING (CRS IF12713): FY2025 is the FINAL YEAR of authorized funding for the Legacy Restoration Fund (LRF) established by the Great American Outdoors Act (GAOA; P.L. 116-152; 54 U.S.C. §§200401 et seq.). The LRF provided annual deposits over five years (FY2021-FY2025) from federal energy revenues, capped at $1.9 billion annually, with 70% (~$1.3 billion/year) allocated to NPS — approximately $6.5 billion total over five years for deferred maintenance at culturally significant sites. After FY2025, this $1.3 billion annual stream DISAPPEARS ENTIRELY unless Congress reauthorizes it. This dramatically accelerates the deferred maintenance crisis: the $22.3 billion backlog (CRS R42757, FY2022) was being reduced by LRF at ~$1.3B/year; without reauthorization, the backlog's growth rate becomes significantly faster. Community-specific LRF sunset impacts: Indigenous sacred site infrastructure at Mesa Verde, Chaco Culture, Canyon de Chelly, Bandelier lose maintenance pipeline; African-descendant civil rights sites (Selma-to-Montgomery Trail, Birmingham Civil Rights NM, Harriet Tubman/Frederick Douglass sites) lose dedicated maintenance; Latine mission sites and borderlands parks lose support; Asian American/Pacific Islander incarceration sites (Manzanar, Minidoka, Tule Lake) and Angel Island lose infrastructure funding; 433 NPS units across 81 million federal acres lose their primary dedicated maintenance funding source.

LEGISLATIVE HISTORY — P.L. 119-4 CONTEXT (CRS IF12713): Before P.L. 119-4, the House passed H.R. 8998 (H.Rept. 118-581, July 24, 2024) with only $3.122 billion for NPS — $215 million less than enacted. The Senate Appropriations Committee reported S. 4802 (S.Rept. 118-201, July 24, 2024) with $3.491 billion — closer to the Biden request. The enacted $3.337 billion was a compromise closer to the House's lower figure. HAC Summary noted HPF and NR&P reductions were to remove community project funding/congressionally directed spending (earmarks) — meaning specific communities that had secured targeted historic preservation and recreation grants lost those funds. The ONPS account received a $6.0 million increase specifically for planning and security for the 2025 Presidential Inauguration — meaning parks lost operational funding to subsidize an inauguration.

FY2023 BASELINE — THE HIGH-WATER MARK (CRS IF12112): FY2023 (P.L. 117-328) was the NPS funding peak: $3.475 billion total discretionary, +6% from FY2022's $3.265B. Four of five accounts increased; two accounts exceeded even the Biden administration's requests. HPF: $204.5M enacted vs. Biden's $151.8M request — Congress gave $52.7M MORE than asked (+35%). NR&P: $92.5M enacted vs. Biden's $74.6M request — $17.9M more (+24%), including $29.2M for national heritage areas (vs. $22.3M requested). Construction peaked at $239.8M before the 28% FY2024 collapse. This FY2023 congressional override demonstrates that bipartisan support for historic preservation and community grants was robust — the reversal in FY2024/FY2025 (Congress falling $439M and $239M below requests) represents a deliberate turn driven by the change in House majority (117th Congress Democratic → 118th Congress Republican).

HOUSE/SENATE DYNAMIC REVERSAL (CRS IF12112): In FY2023, the House proposed $3.643B for NPS (H.R. 8294) — MORE than enacted AND more than Biden's request. The Senate proposed $3.578B. Both chambers wanted more than was enacted. This is the exact opposite of FY2024 (House: $3.043B, $432M below enacted) and FY2025 (House: $3.122B, $215M below enacted). The FY2023 generosity reflects the 117th Congress (Democratic majority); the FY2024 reversal reflects the 118th Congress (Republican majority). The DOGE workforce cuts landed on NPS after two years of Republican-led congressional underfunding following a Democratic-led funding renaissance.

IRA $700M NPS MANDATORY APPROPRIATION (CRS IF12112): The Inflation Reduction Act (P.L. 117-169) provided $700 million in one-time mandatory NPS funding in FY2022 for natural resource and climate resilience — the largest single mandatory NPS infusion in the tracker's time window. This explains why FY2022 mandatory NPS funding was high ($1.886B) and FY2023's $1.220B appeared as a 35% decline. The Trump administration subsequently targeted IRA grants for termination and impoundment, meaning this $700M NPS investment was retroactively undermined.

NPS SYSTEM GROWTH: EXPANDING MANDATE ON SHRINKING BUDGET (CRS IF12112 + IF12436 + IF12713): The NPS system grew from 424 units (FY2023) to 429 units (FY2024) to 433 units (FY2025) — nine new units added while the budget declined 4% from FY2023 to FY2024. Per-unit funding was squeezed even more sharply than headline numbers suggest. The agency was managing a growing system on a shrinking budget BEFORE DOGE fired 24% of the permanent workforce.

NATURAL DISASTER SUPPLEMENTALS — THE INVISIBLE FUNDING STREAM (CRS IF12112 + IF12436 + IF12713): Excluded from regular totals but significant: FY2022 $229.5M, FY2023 $1.500B (P.L. 117-328 Div. N — 43% of regular discretionary), FY2025 $2.313B (P.L. 118-58). These respond to climate-driven disasters at parks in Indigenous territories, rural gateway communities, and coastal regions. The $2.313B FY2025 supplemental was appropriated at the same moment DOGE was firing the NPS workforce needed to spend it.

MULTI-YEAR UNDERFUNDING TRAJECTORY (CRS IF12436 + IF12713): Two consecutive CRS reports reveal a sustained multi-year underfunding trajectory that preceded and compounded DOGE workforce actions. NPS discretionary: FY2023 $3.475B (baseline high) → FY2024 $3.325B (–4%, all five accounts declined, P.L. 118-42) → FY2025 $3.337B (+<1%, P.L. 119-4) → FY2026 ~$2.12B proposed (–36.6%). The FY2024 request-to-enacted gap of $439 million (12%) was the largest in the three-year window — worse than FY2025's $239M gap. The Construction account collapsed 28% from FY2023 to FY2024 ($239.8M → $172.3M) — the single sharpest cut — then held flat through FY2025, directly compounding the $22.3 billion deferred maintenance backlog (CRS R42757, FY2022). The 24% DOGE staffing cut landed on two consecutive years of appropriations cuts, hitting an agency that had already absorbed $439M below its FY2024 request. This is not efficiency — it is institutional destruction through accumulated blows.

CONSTRUCTION COLLAPSE COMMUNITY IMPACT (CRS IF12436): Indigenous heritage site infrastructure (Mesa Verde, Chaco Culture, Canyon de Chelly) received $67.5M less annually in construction funding starting FY2024 — two full years before DOGE cuts. Civil rights site infrastructure (Selma-to-Montgomery Trail, Birmingham Civil Rights NM, Little Rock Central High School NHS, Harriet Tubman sites) lost construction funding for two consecutive years before staff cuts eliminated the maintenance workers. Japanese American incarceration site infrastructure (Manzanar, Minidoka, Tule Lake) deferred repairs compounded by simultaneous LRF sunset.

AMERICAN BATTLEFIELD PROTECTION PROGRAM (CRS IF12436): NPS received $429.9 million from LWCF for FY2024, including $20 million for the ABPP — a grant program directly relevant to African American military history (United States Colored Troops fought at many preserved battlefields) and Indigenous treaty-era conflicts (Little Bighorn/Greasy Grass intersects Lakota, Cheyenne, and Arapaho heritage). ABPP grants go to states, localities, tribes, and nonprofits.

LRF FY2024 — CONGRESS EXCEEDED NPS'S OWN REQUEST (CRS IF12436): For FY2024, NPS proposed $1.330 billion in LRF-funded deferred maintenance projects; Congress appropriated $1.338 billion — EXCEEDING NPS's own request, including a higher-than-requested contingency fund. In the year before DOGE cuts, Congress was actively accelerating deferred maintenance resolution. The administration then fired the employees who would have executed those projects.

CENTENNIAL CHALLENGE 20% CUT (CRS IF12436): The matching fund fell 20% from FY2023 to FY2024 ($15.0M → $12.0M), reducing the federal incentive for community-driven park projects. This matching mechanism is particularly important for communities with less robust philanthropic infrastructure — rural gateway communities, Indigenous nations, and organizations serving communities of color.

NPS SHUTDOWN IMPACT (CRS IF11079): The October 1 – November 12, 2025 government shutdown (43 days, longest in U.S. history) compounded the damage documented above. The NPS September 2025 contingency plan projected 64% of the workforce for furlough — nominally lower than the 87% in prior shutdown plans, but only because DOGE had already eliminated 24% of the permanent workforce. Total operational capacity was catastrophically lower than any previous shutdown. Of 429 NPS units, only 157 collected entrance fees under FLREA; the remaining 272 were subject to complete closure. Parks that remained open used $348M in unobligated FLREA fee balances (FY2024) — drawing down funds normally directed 55% toward deferred maintenance, further eroding the $22.3 billion backlog (CRS R42757, FY2022). GAO Opinion B-330776 found this fee use violated the purpose statute (31 U.S.C. §1301(a)) and the Antideficiency Act; DOI disputed; the legal question remains unresolved. See id 73 (NPS Government Shutdown) for full analysis. (CRS IF11079, https://crsreports.congress.gov/product/pdf/IF/IF11079)

NPS AFFILIATED AREAS VULNERABILITY (CRS IF11281): The 24% NPS workforce reduction documented above also threatens 32 NPS affiliated areas — nationally significant sites outside the National Park System that depend on NPS technical assistance and cooperative agreements. Without staff to maintain these agreements, affiliated areas including the Iñupiat Heritage Center (Utqiaġvik, AK) and the five Brown v. Board of Education schools (designated January 2025 under P.L. 117-123) lose their federal preservation partnership. Congress has never enacted a legal definition for affiliated area status, meaning these sites have no statutory protection against administrative rescission. DOGE cuts also reduce NPS capacity to conduct reconnaissance surveys and special resource studies — the pipeline through which new culturally significant sites gain federal recognition. See id 74 (NPS Affiliated Areas Threat). (CRS IF11281, https://crsreports.congress.gov/product/pdf/IF/IF11281)

NPS FTE BASELINE CORRECTION (CRS R42757): The CRS-documented FY2023 staffing baseline was 19,390 FTE. A 24% DOGE cut eliminates approximately 4,654 positions (not ~4,000 as previously estimated), leaving ~14,736 FTE — smaller than at any point in NPS's modern history and 26% below the FY2014 high of 19,894. In inflation-adjusted terms, the proposed FY2026 NPS appropriation of ~$2.12B represents approximately 39–40% less purchasing power than FY2014's $2.562B — erasing the entire decade's gains. Of 23 units added FY2014–FY2023, 11 honor African American, LGBTQ+, women's, or Asian American history — the newest, most interpretive-dependent units most threatened by these cuts. (CRS R42757, https://crsreports.congress.gov/product/pdf/R/R42757)

SOURCES: CRS Report R47337 Version 6 (Updated February 12, 2026), https://crsreports.congress.gov/product/pdf/R/R47337; CRS Report IF12446 (June 29, 2023), https://crsreports.congress.gov/product/pdf/IF/IF12446; CRS In Focus IF12713 Version 7 (Updated September 30, 2025), https://crsreports.congress.gov/product/pdf/IF/IF12713; CRS In Focus IF12436 Version 6 (Updated July 23, 2024), https://crsreports.congress.gov/product/pdf/IF/IF12436; CRS In Focus IF12112 Version 5 (Updated June 26, 2023), https://crsreports.congress.gov/product/pdf/IF/IF12112

actions.title: Native Postsecondary Education: $339.7M Three-Agency System Under Simultaneous Attack (DOI, ED, USDA)
actions.status: Active — DOI lost 20%+ workforce (Haskell 25%+ staff, SIPI ~25% staff); ED 50% workforce cut; USDA significant reductions; H.R. 899 threatens ED elimination
actions.description:
The complete FY2024 federal investment in Native postsecondary education totaled $339.687 million across nine program lines and three agencies. All three administering agencies have experienced significant workforce reductions — creating a three-agency compounding vulnerability with no equivalent for any other community's education funding.

EDUCATION BASELINE — LOWEST IN THE NATION: According to 2022 U.S. Census data, AIAN students have the lowest postsecondary enrollment rate (60%) and near-lowest degree completion rate (33%) of all racial/ethnic groups. Pacific Islander students are equally positioned at the bottom (58% enrollment, 32% completion). These are the two communities whose federal-specific education programs are simultaneously being defunded. Every education cut that affects all students disproportionately harms those starting from the lowest baseline. CRS notes 'data are not available on Indian educational achievement nationally' — Census AIAN racial identification is not the same as tribal membership, creating a built-in data gap that pre-dates current data terminations.

THREE INSTITUTION TYPES:

TYPE 1 — FEDERALLY OPERATED (DOI-owned): Haskell Indian Nations University (Lawrence, KS) — 4-year liberal arts, 800+ undergrads, NO tuition (less than $500 fees), students from 574 tribes, founded 1884 as federal Indian boarding school, now National Historic Landmark. Southwestern Indian Polytechnic Institute (SIPI) (Albuquerque, NM) — 2-year college, 400+ students, NO tuition. Combined FY2024: $30.325M. Because DOI owns and operates both, DOGE-directed cuts directly eliminate the federal employees who ARE the faculty and staff — there is no institutional buffer.

TYPE 2 — FEDERALLY CHARTERED INDEPENDENT: Institute of American Indian Arts (IAIA) (Santa Fe, NM) — the only federally chartered U.S. institution dedicated to 'study, creative application, preservation, and care of Indian arts and culture' (20 U.S.C. Chapter 56). 1,100+ students, under $6,000 tuition, Board of Trustees appointed by the President. FY2024: $13.482M (DOI-administered). IAIA trains the artists, writers, filmmakers, and cultural practitioners who produce the work NEA, NEH, and IMLS are defunding — it is the pipeline institution for the entire Indigenous arts ecosystem.

TYPE 3 — TRIBALLY CONTROLLED (32 TCCUs): 20,000+ students, tuition $900-$6,700, located in Great Lakes, Plains, Southwest, Northwest, and Alaska. Almost two-thirds are 4-year colleges. Admissions not limited to tribal members. Reflect 'tribal ideals of self-determination and culturally relevant educational attainment.' Tribally controlled but primarily federally funded — federal agency cuts withdraw funding from tribal institutions.

FY2024 COMPLETE FUNDING ARCHITECTURE:
- DOI: Haskell and SIPI — $30.325M
- DOI: IAIA — $13.482M
- DOI: TCU program (TCCUAA Titles I & II, P.L. 95-471) — $89.146M
- DOI: Tribal Technical Colleges (TCCUAA Title V) — $9.156M
- DOI: Tribal Colleges Facilities Improvement & Repair — $16.000M
- DOI: Scholarships and Adult Education — $54.648M
- ED: Strengthening TCCU (HEA Title III) — $80.097M
- ED: Tribally Controlled Postsecondary Career and Technical Institutions (P.L. 109-270) — $11.953M
- USDA: Programs for 1994 Land-Grant Institutions (P.L. 103-382) — $34.880M
- TOTAL: $339.687M

DOI PROGRAMS ($212.757M): All vulnerable to DOGE-directed cuts (SO 3429, EO 14210). All administered through BIE/BIA — the bureau that lost 20%+ of workforce. Includes the $54.648M Scholarships and Adult Education program providing tribal-member-specific financial aid (distinct from Pell Grants) from GED completion through STEM doctoral education via the Science Post-Graduate Scholarship Fund. BIA administrative capacity to distribute congressionally appropriated scholarship funds has been gutted — producing the 'financial aid delayed' conditions documented at Haskell.

ED PROGRAMS ($92.050M): The Strengthening TCCU program ($80.097M) is the largest single ED-administered tribal college program, authorized under HEA Title III — the same title covering Strengthening HBCUs and Strengthening Hispanic-Serving Institutions. H.R. 899 (Department of Education Termination Act) would eliminate the agency administering all three. ED's 50% workforce reduction degrades existing program administration.

USDA PROGRAMS ($34.880M) — COMPLETE TRACKER GAP: The 1994 Land-Grant Tribal College programs are authorized by P.L. 103-382 (Equity in Educational Land-Grant Status Act) — the third extension of the original 1862 Morrill Act land-grant tradition (extended to HBCUs by the Second Morrill Act of 1890, to tribal colleges in 1994). Four programs: Tribal Colleges Equity Grant (preparing students for tribal leadership and agricultural careers), Tribal College Extension Program (community-based farmer education and food sovereignty), Tribal College Research Program (scientific capacity building), and Tribal College Endowment Program (discretionary capacity grants). The Extension Program supporting tribal farmer education is directly connected to the food sovereignty crises documented in the tracker: SNAP cuts, FDPIR mutual exclusivity, and traditional food access. USDA workforce reductions under DOGE threaten tribal college agricultural education at the same time federal food assistance is being cut in the same communities.

MORRILL ACT THREE-GENERATION LINEAGE: The First Morrill Act (1862) gave land to states for agricultural colleges — explicitly excluding tribal lands. The Second Morrill Act (1890) required states to either desegregate or establish separate land-grant colleges for Black students — creating the 1890 HBCU land-grant system. The 1994 Act completed the extension 132 years after the original exclusion. H.R. 899 and USDA workforce reductions simultaneously threaten the ED Title III programs supporting 1890 HBCUs and the USDA programs supporting 1994 tribal land-grant colleges — cutting both legs of the same inclusionary tradition.

COBELL EDUCATION SCHOLARSHIP: The Cobell Education Scholarship Fund — created by the Claims Resolution Act of 2010 (P.L. 111-291) as part of the $3.4 billion Cobell settlement, the largest class-action against the federal government — provides tribal member scholarships as restitution for systematic federal trust mismanagement of Individual Indian Money accounts. Led by Elouise Cobell (Blackfeet Nation), the lawsuit represented ~500,000 Native Americans. DOI fulfilled its $60M contribution in 2017; the fund operates as an endowment. The current administration's BIE cuts, BIA funding shortfalls, and IHS reductions continue the pattern of trust mismanagement that produced the Cobell litigation.

HASKELL NATIONAL HISTORIC LANDMARK: Haskell was founded in 1884 as the United States Indian Industrial Training School — a federal Indian boarding school, part of the system that forcibly separated Indigenous children from families to eliminate Native languages and cultures. Its evolution from boarding school to contemporary university is physically documented in its landmark buildings. The campus faces: (1) BIE staff cuts (25%+) gutting its academic function; (2) NPS historic preservation staff reductions reducing landmark building support; (3) potential 'divisive content' review (SO 3431) of the boarding school history the campus documents and transcends. The same government that founded Haskell to eliminate Indigenous culture is now cutting the investment that makes it an institution for preserving that culture.

TRUST RESPONSIBILITY DEFINITION: The federal trust relationship in Native education 'is a responsibility derived from federal statutes, treaties, court decisions, executive actions, and the Constitution, which assigns authority over federal-Indian relations to Congress' (CRS IF10554). This four-source legal architecture is being violated by DOGE-directed BIE/BIA workforce reductions implemented without tribal consultation.

SOURCE: CRS Report IF10554 Version 5 (Updated June 25, 2024). https://crsreports.congress.gov/product/pdf/IF/IF10554

actions.title: H.Res.789 — Expressing Support for the Designation of October 13, 2025, as Columbus Day
actions.status: Pending — referred to House Committee on Oversight and Accountability
actions.description:
H.Res.789, introduced by Rep. Andrew Garbarino (R-NY-2) on October 6, 2025, expresses support for designating October 13, 2025, as Columbus Day and 'the celebration of Italian-American heritage.' The resolution has 10 Republican cosponsors: Reps. D'Esposito, LaLota, Lawler, Langworthy, Malliotakis, Molinaro, Williams (NY), Zinerman, Stefanik, and Gimenez — predominantly New York delegation members.

TRACKER SIGNIFICANCE: This resolution operates within the broader Indigenous Peoples' Day vs. Columbus Day political contest. The Biden administration recognized Indigenous Peoples' Day (first proclaimed October 2021); the Trump II administration has not continued this recognition. The resolution's framing as 'Italian-American heritage' positions European-American ethnic celebration in direct competition with Indigenous recognition — a pattern documented in the tracker's coverage of Indigenous community threats (EO 14168 renaming Denali, tribal population data crises, Native postsecondary defunding). The resolution contains no policy mechanism but signals Congressional priorities that affect cultural institution programming at the Smithsonian's National Museum of the American Indian, which manages Columbus Day/Indigenous Peoples' Day interpretive content.

COMMUNITY IMPACT: Italian-American communities (approximately 16.5 million, 5% of U.S. population per Census) have historically anchored Columbus Day celebration. The resolution does not acknowledge the growing movement among states and municipalities to replace Columbus Day with Indigenous Peoples' Day (now recognized in 14+ states and 130+ cities). For the 9.7 million AIAN population documented in the tracker (CRS IF12612), Columbus Day celebrations remain a site of contested heritage that cultural institutions must navigate.

actions.title: H.R.2121 — Irish American Museum and Cultural Center Commission Act
actions.status: Pending — referred to House Committee on Natural Resources and House Committee on House Administration
actions.description:
H.R.2121, introduced by Rep. Brian Fitzpatrick (R-PA-1) on March 14, 2025, establishes a commission to study the potential creation of a National Irish American Museum and Cultural Center in the Smithsonian or as an independent entity. The bill authorizes $2.1 million for the study commission and $1.1 million for fundraising activities. It has 17 bipartisan cosponsors including Reps. Boyle (D-PA), Case (D-HI), Connolly (D-VA), Fitzpatrick (R-PA), Neal (D-MA), and others.

TRACKER SIGNIFICANCE: This bill follows the established Congressional pathway for new Smithsonian museums — the same study commission process that created NMAAHC (P.L. 108-184, 2003) and NMAL (P.L. 116-260, 2020). Its introduction during the same period when existing Smithsonian museums face FY2026 budget cuts ($86.2M reduction) and DOGE-directed workforce reductions creates a contradiction: Congress considers creating a new ethnic heritage museum while the administration defunds existing ones. The $2.1M study commission authorization is modest compared to the billions eventually needed for construction (NMAAHC cost $540M), but the bill's existence demonstrates continued Congressional appetite for ethnic heritage commemoration even as existing cultural institutions are under attack.

MUSEUM CREATION PATTERN: NMAAHC (authorized 2003, opened 2016), NMAL (authorized 2020, under development), Women's History Museum (authorized 2020, under development). Each followed the study commission → authorization → construction pathway. If enacted, the Irish American Museum would join a pipeline of museums competing for Smithsonian resources during an era of budget contraction.

COMMUNITY CONTEXT: Irish Americans (approximately 31.5 million claiming Irish ancestry per Census) represent the second-largest European-ancestry group after German Americans. The bill's bipartisan support reflects Irish-American political influence across both parties. The commission model mirrors the successful NMAAHC precedent but launches during unprecedented Smithsonian fiscal stress.
actions.affected_targets: Smithsonian Institution

actions.title: H.Res.199 — Condemning the Use of Taxpayer Dollars to Fund Woke Foreign Aid Programs
actions.status: Pending — referred to House Committee on Foreign Affairs
actions.description:
H.Res.199, introduced by Rep. Tim Burchett (R-TN-2) on March 6, 2025, condemns approximately 20 specific USAID and State Department programs as 'woke foreign aid.' The resolution lists targeted programs including: LGBTQI+ rights programming, gender equality initiatives, climate change adaptation, DEI training for foreign service officers, and cultural exchange programs. It calls for a GAO audit of all foreign aid programs using DEI or 'woke' criteria.

TRACKER SIGNIFICANCE: The resolution's 'woke' targeting framework directly parallels the domestic DEI elimination (EO 14151) and 'divisive concepts' ban (EO 14156/SO 3431) already in the tracker. Its demand for a GAO audit of programs using DEI criteria creates a Congressional mandate for the same kind of ideological screening being applied to domestic cultural institutions. The resolution's targeting of cultural exchange programs intersects with State Department programs that fund international museum partnerships, cultural preservation, and heritage site protection — programs that benefit the same communities served by IMLS, NEA, and NEH domestically.

SPECIFIC PROGRAMS TARGETED: The resolution names programs including Democracy, Human Rights, and Labor Bureau initiatives; Global Equality Fund (LGBTQI+); Women's Global Development and Prosperity Initiative; PEPFAR gender components; climate resilience programs; and diversity training within the Foreign Service. Each represents international extensions of domestic policy frameworks being simultaneously dismantled.

CROSS-REFERENCE: This resolution provides Congressional backing for USAID's ongoing dismantlement, the State Department Country Reports termination (id 20), and the broader 'divisive concepts' framework (EO 14156, id 13). The GAO audit demand mirrors the financial accountability framing used to justify DOGE-directed cuts across cultural agencies.
actions.affected_targets: Open Society Foundations

actions.title: S.776 — UNITED Act (US-UK Trade Agreement)
actions.status: Pending — referred to Senate Committee on Finance
actions.description:
S.776, the Unleashing Nations' Investments Through Economic Deals (UNITED) Act, introduced by Sen. Chris Coons (D-DE) and Sen. Jerry Moran (R-KS) on February 27, 2025, directs the U.S. Trade Representative to negotiate a comprehensive trade agreement with the United Kingdom. The bill has bipartisan sponsorship and reflects post-Brexit US-UK trade alignment efforts.

TRACKER SIGNIFICANCE: While primarily a trade bill, the UNITED Act intersects with the tracker's cultural institution concerns in several ways: (1) Trade agreements routinely include intellectual property and cultural heritage provisions that affect museum lending, cultural property repatriation, and heritage site protections; (2) The UK's British Museum, Victoria and Albert Museum, and other institutions are major lending partners for Smithsonian exhibitions — trade framework changes could affect these partnerships; (3) Cultural services are increasingly included in trade negotiations, potentially affecting nonprofit cultural organizations' international programming; (4) The bill's bipartisan nature contrasts with the partisan cultural defunding documented elsewhere in the tracker.

CULTURAL TRADE DIMENSION: US-UK cultural exchange includes museum loans, traveling exhibitions, academic partnerships, and heritage preservation cooperation. Any comprehensive trade agreement would establish frameworks affecting these exchanges. The UK's post-Brexit cultural policy independence means bilateral agreements replace EU-level cultural frameworks.
actions.affected_targets: Smithsonian Institution

actions.title: S.1893 — Henrietta Lacks Congressional Gold Medal Act
actions.status: Pending — House companion H.R.3498 also introduced
actions.description:
S.1893, introduced by Sen. Chris Van Hollen (D-MD) in May 2025, awards a Congressional Gold Medal to Henrietta Lacks (posthumously) in recognition of her 'immortal' contribution to biomedical research. Henrietta Lacks (1920-1951) was a Black woman whose cervical cancer cells were taken without her knowledge or consent at Johns Hopkins Hospital in 1951, becoming the HeLa cell line — the first 'immortal' human cell line, used in over 75,000 studies including development of the polio vaccine, cancer research, and COVID-19 vaccines. The House companion bill is H.R.3498.

TRACKER SIGNIFICANCE: The Henrietta Lacks story is a foundational narrative in the history of medical racism, informed consent, and the exploitation of Black bodies by American institutions. NMAAHC's permanent collection includes materials documenting the Lacks family's experience and the broader history of medical experimentation on African Americans (including the Tuskegee Syphilis Study, J. Marion Sims, and enslaved persons used in medical research). The Congressional Gold Medal would create a federally sanctioned recognition of medical racism — a narrative category that falls squarely within the 'divisive concepts' framework (EO 14156/SO 3431) being applied to federal cultural institutions.

MEDICAL RACISM AND CULTURAL HERITAGE: The HeLa cell line has generated billions in commercial value with no compensation to the Lacks family until a 2023 settlement with Thermo Fisher Scientific. This exploitation narrative connects to: (1) NMAAHC's documentation of systemic racism in American institutions; (2) the broader pattern of Black intellectual and bodily labor extracted without consent or compensation; (3) ongoing debates about genetic privacy, bioethics, and racial justice that cultural institutions interpret for public audiences.

CONGRESSIONAL GOLD MEDAL CONTEXT: The Congressional Gold Medal is Congress's highest expression of national appreciation. Awarding it to Henrietta Lacks would officially recognize the injustice of her cells' unconsented use — a federal acknowledgment of institutional racism during an administration actively suppressing such acknowledgments through DEI elimination and 'divisive concepts' bans.
actions.affected_targets: National Museum of African American History and Culture

actions.title: H.R.821 — Fred Korematsu Congressional Gold Medal Act (House)
actions.status: Pending — referred to House Committee on Financial Services; 29 bipartisan cosponsors
actions.description:
H.R.821, introduced by Rep. Mark Takano (D-CA-39) on January 28, 2025, awards a Congressional Gold Medal posthumously to Fred Toyosaburo Korematsu (1919-2005) in recognition of his 'dedication and contribution to civil rights, his ## courage in standing up against injustice, and his lifelong commitment to justice and equality.' The bill has 29 bipartisan cosponsors. Senate companion bill is S.338.

TRACKER SIGNIFICANCE: Fred Korematsu is the central figure in the Japanese American incarceration narrative documented throughout the tracker. In 1942, Korematsu defied Executive Order 9066's mass incarceration order, was arrested, convicted, and his case reached the Supreme Court (Korematsu v. United States, 323 U.S. 214, 1944) — which upheld the incarceration in a decision now universally regarded as one of the Court's worst. His conviction was vacated in 1983 through a coram nobis petition after government documents revealed the Solicitor General had suppressed evidence that Japanese Americans posed no security threat. He received the Presidential Medal of Freedom in 1998.

JACS/JACE CONNECTION: This bill directly intersects with the Japanese American Confinement Sites (JACS, P.L. 109-441) and Japanese American World War II History (JACE, P.L. 117-328) programs documented in tracker entry id 26 (NPS preservation programs). Korematsu's story is the individual narrative that these preservation programs contextualize through physical site documentation. Awarding a Congressional Gold Medal to Korematsu while simultaneously defunding the NPS programs that preserve the sites where his community was incarcerated creates a recognition-without-preservation paradox.

CIVIL LIBERTIES PRECEDENT: Chief Justice Roberts explicitly repudiated Korematsu in Trump v. Hawaii (2018): 'Korematsu was gravely wrong the day it was decided.' The Gold Medal would further cement this repudiation through Congressional action. January 30 is Fred Korematsu Day of Civil Liberties and the Constitution in California, Virginia, Hawaii, and other states.
actions.affected_targets: Japanese American National Museum

actions.title: S.338 — Fred Korematsu Congressional Gold Medal Act (Senate)
actions.status: Pending — referred to Senate Committee on Banking, Housing, and Urban Affairs; 22 cosponsors
actions.description:
S.338, introduced by Sen. Mazie Hirono (D-HI) on January 30, 2025 — Fred Korematsu Day of Civil Liberties and the Constitution — is the Senate companion to H.R.821. The bill has 22 cosponsors. The deliberate introduction on Korematsu Day underscores the symbolic significance.

TRACKER SIGNIFICANCE: See H.R.821 (id 33) for full analysis. The Senate companion's 22 cosponsors combined with the House bill's 29 cosponsors demonstrate significant bipartisan support (51 total) for recognizing Korematsu's civil rights legacy. This Congressional momentum toward Japanese American recognition occurs simultaneously with: (1) JACS/JACE program funding threatened by NPS workforce reductions; (2) Japanese American National Museum facing IMLS grant termination; (3) 'divisive concepts' framework (SO 3431) potentially restricting federal interpretation of the incarceration history Korematsu resisted.

BICHAMBER STRATEGY: The simultaneous House-Senate introduction mirrors the legislative strategy used for the USCT Gold Medal bills (S.498/H.R.1087) and reflects organized advocacy by the Japanese American community, JACL (Japanese American Citizens League), and civil liberties organizations.

actions.title: S.498 — United States Colored Troops Congressional Gold Medal Act (Senate)
actions.status: Pending — referred to Senate Committee on Banking, Housing, and Urban Affairs
actions.description:
S.498, introduced by Sen. Cory Booker (D-NJ) on February 10, 2025, awards a Congressional Gold Medal collectively to the United States Colored Troops (USCT) — approximately 180,000 African American soldiers and 19,000 African American sailors who served in the Union military during the Civil War (1863-1865). House companion bill is H.R.1087.

TRACKER SIGNIFICANCE: The USCT represents the first large-scale military service by African Americans, authorized by the Emancipation Proclamation (1863) and formalized through the Bureau of Colored Troops (War Department, May 1863). Their service is documented at multiple NPS sites now facing preservation program cuts (tracker entry id 26): African American Civil War Memorial (Washington, DC), Fort Wagner (SC), Port Hudson (LA), and numerous ABPP-eligible battlefields. The Gold Medal would create Congressional recognition of Black military service while the NPS programs preserving USCT battle sites face DOGE-directed workforce reductions.

BUFFALO SOLDIERS CONNECTION: The USCT were the direct predecessors of the Buffalo Soldiers (post-Civil War regular Army units), whose museum in Houston, TX is documented in the tracker as an IMLS grant recipient losing federal support (id 6). Congressional Gold Medal recognition of the USCT while defunding the museums that preserve their legacy creates the same recognition-without-preservation paradox documented for the Korematsu Gold Medal bills.

NMAAHC COLLECTION: NMAAHC holds extensive USCT materials including recruitment posters, uniforms, service records, and the narrative of the 54th Massachusetts Infantry Regiment (depicted in the film 'Glory'). The museum's FY2026 budget cuts and DOGE workforce reductions directly affect its capacity to interpret the history this Gold Medal would honor.

MILITARY MEMORIAL CROSS-REFERENCE: CRS R47337 documents the military memorial ecosystem including the African American Civil War Memorial (dedicated 1998) and the broader pattern of delayed recognition for communities of color in military commemoration. The USCT served 160+ years ago; Congressional Gold Medal recognition in 2025 demonstrates the multi-generational timeline of acknowledgment.
actions.affected_targets: National Museum of African American History and Culture

actions.title: H.R.1087 — United States Colored Troops Congressional Gold Medal Act (House)
actions.status: Pending — referred to House Committee on Financial Services; 6 cosponsors
actions.description:
H.R.1087, introduced by Rep. Eleanor Holmes Norton (D-DC-At Large) on February 6, 2025, is the House companion to S.498, awarding a Congressional Gold Medal collectively to the United States Colored Troops. The bill has 6 cosponsors. Rep. Norton's sponsorship is significant — as the delegate for the District of Columbia, she represents the constituency where the African American Civil War Memorial stands and where the Bureau of Colored Troops was headquartered.

TRACKER SIGNIFICANCE: See S.498 (id 35) for full USCT analysis. The House companion's introduction by the DC delegate connects the USCT legacy directly to Washington's memorial landscape and to the broader question of DC representation — Norton cannot vote on the House floor, meaning the USCT's Congressional Gold Medal must ultimately be carried by voting members.

BICHAMBER STRATEGY: Like the Korematsu Gold Medal bills (H.R.821/S.338), the simultaneous House-Senate introduction reflects organized advocacy. The relatively modest 6 House cosponsors compared to the Korematsu bills' 29 suggests the USCT campaign may be earlier in building Congressional support.

DIVISIVE CONCEPTS INTERSECTION: The USCT narrative — enslaved and free African Americans fighting to end slavery while the U.S. government debated their humanity — is precisely the kind of historical content that the 'divisive concepts' framework (EO 14156/SO 3431) is designed to restrict in federal interpretation. Congressional Gold Medal recognition would create a federal mandate to honor this history while executive orders simultaneously restrict how federal institutions can teach it.

actions.title: H.R.925 — Dismantle DEI Act of 2025 (House)
actions.status: Pending — referred to 9 House committees; 96 Republican cosponsors; predecessor H.R.8706 passed House Oversight Committee 23-17 (118th Congress)
actions.description:
H.R.925, the Dismantle DEI Act of 2025, introduced by Rep. Michael Cloud (R-TX-27) on February 4, 2025, is a 47-page, 8-title bill that would comprehensively dismantle diversity, equity, and inclusion infrastructure across the entire federal government, its contractors, grantees, educational accreditors, financial regulators, military, and intelligence community. The bill has 96 Republican cosponsors and no Democratic support. Senate companion is S.382. The predecessor bill (H.R.8706) passed the House Oversight Committee on November 21, 2024, on a party-line vote of 23-17.

LEGISLATIVE PURPOSE — CODIFICATION: The bill's primary function is to codify into permanent statute the substance of EO 14151 (End Federal DEI, tracker id 1) so that DEI elimination cannot be reversed by a future president through executive order. This transforms the tracker's most foundational threat from an executive action (reversible) to a statutory prohibition (permanent).

KEY PROVISIONS:

TITLE I — FEDERAL OFFICES: Statutorily rescinds EO 13985 (Advancing Racial Equity), EO 13988 (Gender Identity/Sexual Orientation Discrimination Prevention), EO 14020 (White House Gender Policy Council), EO 14031 (Advancing Equity for AANHPI), EO 14035 (Federal Workforce DEIA), and EO 14091 (Further Advancing Racial Equity). All programs must close within 90 days. OPM must terminate the Office of Diversity, Equity, Inclusion, and Accessibility (ODEIA) and disband the Chief Diversity Officers Executive Council within 180 days.

TITLE I — PROHIBITED USES OF FUNDS: Federal funds may NOT be used for: DEI offices, Chief Diversity Officers, DEI plans/reports/surveys, employee resource groups or affinity groups based on protected characteristics, agency equity teams, White House Environmental Justice Interagency Council, Equitable Data Working Group, or DEI training courses. EXCEPTION: Historical EEO offices and ADA enforcement offices are explicitly preserved.

TITLE II — TRAINING: Prohibits all federal training related to diversity, equity, inclusion, accessibility, critical race theory, critical gender theory, intersectionality, sexual orientation, or gender identity.

TITLE III — CONTRACTING: All federal contracts exceeding $10,000 must include provisions prohibiting contractor participation in DEI practices. Contractors may use their own non-federal funds for DEI at their discretion.

TITLE IV — GRANTS: All federal grants and cooperative agreements must prohibit recipients from using federal funds for DEI offices, officers, or training. EXCEPTION: Does not prevent funding for EEO offices, ADA enforcement, or HBCUs.

TITLE V — ADVISORY COMMITTEES: DEI violations trigger mandatory 30-day committee termination. Private right of action with minimum $1,000 per violation per day.

TITLE VI — EDUCATION: Accrediting agencies cannot require, encourage, or coerce DEI practices. Cannot assess institutional ideology, belief, or viewpoint. Cannot prohibit religious mission or discriminate against religious speech. Department of Education funds prohibited from supporting DEI offices.

TITLE VII — SECTOR-SPECIFIC: Repeals Section 342 of Dodd-Frank (Offices of Minority and Women Inclusion at financial regulators). Eliminates DOD Chief Diversity Officer. Repeals DHS diversity reporting. Repeals intelligence community DEI programs (Section 5704, Damon Paul Nelson Act).

TITLE VIII — ENFORCEMENT: Private cause of action in any federal district court. Prevailing plaintiffs recover minimum $1,000 per violation per day, attorney's fees, compensatory damages, and equitable relief. No EEOC exhaustion requirement.

DEFINITION OF 'PROHIBITED DEI PRACTICE': Discriminating for or against any person based on race, color, ethnicity, religion, biological sex, or national origin; requiring training asserting any group is inherently or systemically superior/inferior, oppressive/oppressed, or privileged/unprivileged; requiring employees to sign or assent to DEI statements. The deliberate use of 'biological sex' rather than 'sex' is designed to exclude Bostock v. Clayton County (2020) reasoning extending sex discrimination protections to sexual orientation and gender identity.

TRACKER CROSS-REFERENCES: This bill would permanently codify the threats documented in: EO 14151 (id 1, End Federal DEI), EO 14156/SO 3431 (id 13, Divisive Concepts), and extend prohibitions to federal contractors and grantees — capturing the entire ecosystem of cultural institutions funded through NEA, NEH, IMLS, and Smithsonian grants. The Title IV grant prohibition would require every NEA, NEH, and IMLS grant agreement to include anti-DEI provisions — affecting the 250+ museums, HBCUs, tribal libraries, and community organizations documented throughout the tracker. The Title VI accreditation provision would affect every university in the tracker (Harvard, id 12; Columbia, id 22) by prohibiting accreditors from considering institutional diversity commitments.

ARTS AND CULTURE ECONOMIC IMPACT (CRS IF12446): The bill's Title IV grant prohibitions would permanently constrain a sector that represents 4.4% of U.S. GDP — approximately $1.02 trillion in annual economic activity (BEA). The arts sector grew 13.7% in 2021 vs. 5.9% overall GDP, demonstrating counter-cyclical resilience that makes its statutory restriction strategically damaging. Every NEA ($207M FY2024), NEH ($207M), and IMLS ($294.8M) grant agreement would be required to include anti-DEI provisions — affecting the 250+ museums, HBCUs, tribal libraries, and community organizations documented throughout the tracker. Congress explicitly invested $75M (CARES Act), $16.25B (SVOG), and $135M (ARPA) to sustain this sector's economic infrastructure — the Dismantle DEI Act would make it permanently illegal to use federal arts funding for the diversity programming that connects these institutions to underserved communities. The Federal Historic Preservation Tax Credit has leveraged $130 billion in private investment since 1976 — the bill's anti-DEI contracting provisions (Title III) could affect the NPS-IRS-State Historic Preservation Office partnerships that administer this pipeline. For rural, tribal, and low-income urban communities, federal arts funding represents the primary economic development infrastructure — the CRS documents no equivalent private-sector substitute for underserved geographies.

NINE-COMMITTEE REFERRAL: The bill's referral to 9 House committees (Oversight, Judiciary, Education/Workforce, Armed Services, Foreign Affairs, Financial Services, Energy/Commerce, Transportation/Infrastructure, Intelligence) reflects its sweeping scope but also creates significant legislative friction — each committee must act for the bill to advance. GovTrack assigns 2% chance of passing committee and 0% chance of enactment.

SOURCES: CRS Report IF12446 (June 29, 2023), https://crsreports.congress.gov/product/pdf/IF/IF12446

actions.title: S.382 — Dismantle DEI Act of 2025 (Senate)
actions.status: Pending — referred to Senate Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs; 19 Republican cosponsors
actions.description:
S.382, the Dismantle DEI Act of 2025, introduced by Sen. Eric Schmitt (R-MO) and Sen. Jim Risch (R-ID) on February 4, 2025, is the Senate companion to H.R.925. The bill has 19 Republican cosponsors including Sens. Cotton (AR), Lankford (OK), Daines (MT), Tuberville (AL), Blackburn (TN), Marshall (KS), Lummis (WY), Cassidy (LA), Crapo (ID), Cramer (ND), Banks (IN), Sheehy (MT), Hyde-Smith (MS), Scott (FL), Lee (UT), Johnson (WI), Hawley (MO), and Budd (NC). No Democratic cosponsors.

TRACKER SIGNIFICANCE: See H.R.925 (id 37) for full provision-by-provision analysis. The Senate bill is substantively identical to the House companion — a 47-page bill adding Title XII to the Civil Rights Act of 1964. The Senate version's referral to a single committee (Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs) versus the House bill's 9-committee referral theoretically provides a faster path to markup, though no committee action has occurred as of March 2026.

BICHAMBER STRATEGY: The simultaneous February 4, 2025 introduction in both chambers (96 House cosponsors + 21 Senate sponsors/cosponsors = 117 total members) represents the largest Congressional coalition specifically targeting DEI infrastructure. Combined with EO 14151 (already in effect), these bills represent a legislative-executive pincer strategy: the executive order provides immediate action while the legislation provides permanence.

PREDECESSOR LINEAGE: The original Senate version was S.4516 (118th Congress), introduced by then-Senator JD Vance (now Vice President). The bill's evolution from Vance's Senate office to the Vice Presidency — with Vance now presiding over the Senate that would vote on S.382 — represents a unique institutional alignment.

GovTrack assigns 4% chance of enactment and 15% chance of passing committee.

actions.title: S.2932 — Student Athlete Fairness and Enforcement (SAFE) Act
actions.status: Pending — referred to Senate Committee on Commerce, Science, and Transportation; 2 cosponsors
actions.description:
S.2932, the Student Athlete Fairness and Enforcement (SAFE) Act, introduced by Sen. Maria Cantwell (D-WA) on September 29, 2025, with cosponsors Sens. Cory Booker (D-NJ) and Richard Blumenthal (D-CT), is a comprehensive college athletics reform bill creating a federal framework for Name, Image, and Likeness (NIL) rights, athlete health protections, transfer rules, and media rights. This is NOT a transgender athlete participation bill — the transgender sports bills in the 119th Congress are H.R.28 and S.9 (Protection of Women and Girls in Sports Act).

KEY PROVISIONS:

NIL RIGHTS: Establishes a federal right to Name, Image, and Likeness compensation, preempting the current patchwork of state NIL laws. Schools cannot restrict NIL deals except during team activities. Written endorsement contracts required for deals exceeding $600. Sports agent fee capped at 5%.

ATHLETE PROTECTIONS: Two penalty-free transfers without sitting out. Five years of post-eligibility medical coverage for Division 1 sports-related injuries. Mandatory concussion, heat exertion, sickle cell trait, and asthma safety protocols enforced by independent medical officers. Whistleblower protections for safety violation reporters.

SCHOLARSHIP GUARANTEES: 10-year scholarship guarantee for degree completion post-eligibility. Schools must maintain 2023-2024 levels of scholarships and roster spots for non-revenue sports, women's sports, and Olympic sports.

INTERNATIONAL ATHLETES: F-visa student athletes may receive NIL compensation without jeopardizing visa status.

MEDIA RIGHTS: Amends the Sports Broadcasting Act of 1961 to authorize collective media rights negotiation. 22% revenue-sharing cap on media rights distributions. Mandates free local broadcast access for football and basketball games.

ENFORCEMENT: FTC authority over agents and NIL collectives. State Attorneys General enforcement powers. Private right of action for student athletes.

TRACKER SIGNIFICANCE: While primarily an athletics bill, S.2932 intersects with tracker concerns in several ways: (1) The scholarship and roster protections for women's sports and Olympic sports operate alongside the administration's gender-related executive orders; (2) The F-visa NIL provision intersects with the international student restrictions documented in the Harvard ban (id 12); (3) The sickle cell trait screening mandate has particular relevance for African American athletes (sickle cell trait prevalence: ~8% in African Americans vs. ~0.1% in white Americans), connecting to the health equity dimensions tracked across NMAAHC-related entries; (4) The bill's requirement to maintain non-revenue and Olympic sport scholarships at 2023-2024 levels protects programs disproportionately serving athletes of color and international athletes; (5) HBCUs with Division 1 athletic programs would benefit from federal NIL standardization, as their athletes currently operate under the same state-by-state patchwork.

COMPETING LEGISLATION: The House Republican-backed SCORE Act (H.R.4312, Rep. Bilirakis R-FL) takes a fundamentally different approach — allowing only one penalty-free transfer, granting limited NCAA antitrust immunity, and explicitly prohibiting athlete employee classification. The SAFE Act and SCORE Act represent the Democratic (athlete-advocacy) and Republican (NCAA-aligned) frameworks respectively.

actions.title: H.R.2702 — Financial Integrity and Regulation Management (FIRM) Act (House)
actions.status: Ordered reported by House Financial Services Committee (May 20-21, 2025); committee report filed June 20, 2025; awaiting House floor vote; 19 bipartisan cosponsors
actions.description:
H.R.2702, the Financial Integrity and Regulation Management (FIRM) Act, introduced by Rep. Andy Barr (R-KY-6) on April 8, 2025, eliminates 'reputational risk' as a component of federal banking supervision. The bill has 19 cosponsors (18 Republicans, 1 Democrat — Rep. Ritchie Torres D-NY). Senate companion is S.875. GovTrack assigns 74% chance of enactment — unusually high.

LEGISLATIVE PURPOSE: The bill responds to what proponents call 'Operation Choke Point' (Obama era, 2013-2017) and 'Operation Choke Point 2.0' (Biden era, 2022-2024), where banking regulators allegedly used subjective 'reputational risk' standards to pressure banks into denying services to lawful but politically disfavored industries — particularly cryptocurrency firms, firearms dealers, and payday lenders.

KEY PROVISIONS:

DEFINITION: 'Reputational risk' is defined as 'the potential that negative publicity or negative public opinion regarding a depository institution's business practices, whether true or not, will cause a decline in confidence in the institution.' Exception: does NOT apply to negative publicity regarding transactions involving state sponsors of terrorism or foreign terrorist organizations.

MANDATORY REMOVAL (Section 4): All five federal banking agencies must remove all references to 'reputational risk' from guidance documents, rules, examination manuals, and all similar documents.

FIVE PROHIBITIONS (Section 5): Federal banking agencies are prohibited from: (1) establishing any rule or supervisory expectation concerning reputational risk; (2) conducting examinations or data collection related to reputational risk; (3) issuing examination findings or supervisory criticisms concerning reputational risk; (4) making CAMELS supervisory ratings decisions based on reputational risk; (5) taking enforcement action based on reputational risk.

REPORTING (Section 6): Within 180 days, each agency must report to Congress confirming implementation and describing all internal policy changes.

CONGRESSIONAL FINDINGS: The bill includes seven findings establishing that 'reputational risk' lacks statutory basis, enables 'political weaponization' of banking agencies, and that all 'lawful businesses and law-abiding citizens deserve equal access to financial services regardless of political ideology.'

TRACKER SIGNIFICANCE: The FIRM Act intersects with the tracker's DEI and nonprofit concerns in critical ways: (1) 'Reputational risk' has been the primary regulatory mechanism through which banks evaluate whether to serve politically controversial nonprofit clients — its elimination removes a tool that has been used both to restrict access (Operation Choke Point targeting legal industries) and to encourage due diligence on organizations engaged in controversial activities; (2) The bill's framing that financial services should be available 'regardless of political ideology' parallels the Dismantle DEI Act's framing (ids 37-38) — both position diversity/equity considerations as 'political ideology' rather than risk management; (3) Nonprofit organizations, including civil rights groups, advocacy organizations, and arts institutions, have historically faced banking access challenges — the FIRM Act could either help (by preventing politically-motivated debanking of progressive nonprofits) or harm (by removing regulators' ability to flag institutions serving organizations under terrorism-financing investigation, except for designated FTOs); (4) The bill's bipartisan support (Rep. Torres D-NY) reflects genuine cross-partisan concern about government-directed financial discrimination.

REGULATORY MOMENTUM: Before the bill's enactment, regulatory agencies began acting in the same direction: the Federal Reserve announced June 23, 2025 it would no longer include reputational risk in examination programs; OCC and FDIC issued joint proposed rulemaking to codify elimination; President Trump signed an executive order August 7, 2025 titled 'Guaranteeing Fair Banking for All Americans.'
actions.affected_targets: Open Society Foundations

actions.title: S.875 — Financial Integrity and Regulation Management (FIRM) Act (Senate)
actions.status: Ordered reported by Senate Banking Committee (March 13, 2025 — 7 days after introduction); reported with amendment March 18, 2025; awaiting Senate floor vote; 12 Republican cosponsors
actions.description:
S.875, the Financial Integrity and Regulation Management (FIRM) Act, introduced by Sen. Tim Scott (R-SC) — Chairman of the Senate Banking Committee — on March 6, 2025, is the Senate companion to H.R.2702. The bill has 12 Republican cosponsors including Sens. Crapo (ID), Rounds (SD), Tillis (NC), Kennedy (LA), Hagerty (TN), Lummis (WY), Britt (AL), Ricketts (NE), Cramer (ND), Moreno (OH), McCormick (PA), and Banks (IN). GovTrack assigns 72% chance of enactment.

TRACKER SIGNIFICANCE: See H.R.2702 (id 40) for full provision analysis. The Senate version is substantively identical. Sen. Scott called the FIRM Act the 'first step in ending debanking.' The bill's rapid committee advancement — ordered reported within 7 days of introduction — reflects Chairman Scott's prioritization. During markup, Sen. Elizabeth Warren's (D-MA) amendment to cap overdraft fees at $5 was rejected, signaling the committee's focus on deregulation over consumer protection.

BICHAMBER MOMENTUM: Both chambers have advanced the FIRM Act through committee with bipartisan elements (House) and rapid timelines (Senate). Combined with the Federal Reserve's voluntary elimination of reputational risk from examination programs (June 2025), OCC/FDIC joint proposed rulemaking, and the President's August 2025 executive order, the FIRM Act represents a legislative-executive-regulatory convergence on eliminating reputational risk — the most coordinated deregulatory effort in the 119th Congress.

CBO SCORE: Increases net direct spending by $15 million over 2026-2035 (FDIC, NCUA, OCC implementation costs); decreases revenues by $15 million (Federal Reserve costs reducing Treasury remittances). No budgetary impact for CFPB.

NONPROFIT BANKING ACCESS: The elimination of 'reputational risk' from banking supervision has dual implications for nonprofits documented in the tracker. Organizations like Open Society Foundations (id 2, RICO investigation threatened) face banking access risks from the administration's hostile rhetoric — without reputational risk as a regulatory consideration, banks may face less pressure to preemptively close accounts of organizations under political attack, but also less regulatory incentive to maintain banking relationships with controversial clients.
