actions.title: H.R.4859 — Disclosure of Engagements with Attorney Law Firms (DEAL) Act of 2025
actions.status: Pending — referred to House Committee on Ways and Means; 3 Democratic cosponsors; 1% chance of enactment
actions.description:
H.R.4859, the Disclosure of Engagements with Attorney Law Firms (DEAL) Act of 2025, introduced by Rep. Dave Min (D-CA-47) on August 1, 2025, directs the Comptroller General (GAO) to investigate whether the Trump administration's deals with nine major law firms — collectively valued at approximately $1 billion in pro bono legal services — violate the Miscellaneous Receipts Act (31 U.S.C. 3302). The bill has 3 Democratic cosponsors: Reps. McClain Delaney (D-MD), Johnson (D-GA), and Thanedar (D-MI).

THE NINE LAW FIRM DEALS: In early-to-mid 2025, nine major law firms struck agreements with the Trump administration involving massive pro bono commitments directed toward causes the administration selected, in exchange for withdrawal of executive orders, enforcement threats, and investigations targeting the firms. Reported commitments: Paul Weiss (~$40M), Willkie Farr (~$100M), Milbank (~$100M), Skadden Arps (~$100M), Cadwalader Wickersham (~$100M), Kirkland & Ellis (~$125M), A&O Shearman (~$125M), Latham & Watkins (~$125M), Simpson Thacher (~$125M). Total estimated value: approximately $940 million to $1 billion.

DEAL STRUCTURE: The agreements generally required firms to: (1) provide massive pro bono legal services directed toward causes championed by the administration; (2) cease DEI practices in hiring and operations. In exchange, the administration would: (1) withdraw or refrain from issuing executive orders targeting the firms; (2) lift restrictions on access to federal buildings; (3) halt investigations into hiring practices.

KEY PROVISIONS:

DEFINITION OF 'SETTLEMENT': Any written or oral agreement between a law firm and any Executive Branch officer (including the President) that: provides legal services including pro bono representation; implies services are directed toward causes identified or approved by the Executive Branch; was entered into in connection with withdrawal of executive orders or regulatory threats; has estimated value exceeding $1,000,000; was entered into on or after February 1, 2025.

GAO INVESTIGATION (Section 3): The Comptroller General must submit a report within 180 days examining whether the settlements violate the Miscellaneous Receipts Act — which requires that money or value received for the government be deposited into the U.S. Treasury. The legal theory: when the Executive Branch pressures firms into providing ~$1 billion in directed pro bono services, those services constitute something of value equivalent to a government receipt that should flow through the appropriations process.

TRACKER SIGNIFICANCE: The DEAL Act directly intersects with the tracker's DEI concerns: (1) The law firm deals explicitly required firms to cease DEI practices as a condition — making them enforcement mechanisms for the same DEI elimination documented in EO 14151 (id 1) and the Dismantle DEI Act (ids 37-38), but operating through private-sector coercion rather than statutory or executive mandate; (2) The ~$1 billion in coerced pro bono services represents a shadow appropriation — resources directed by the Executive Branch without congressional authorization, potentially violating the same separation of powers principles that courts cited in blocking IMLS elimination (EO 14238); (3) Several of the targeted law firms represent nonprofit organizations, civil rights groups, and cultural institutions — the coercion of their counsel creates a chilling effect on legal representation for organizations under administration attack; (4) The firms that resisted — Perkins Coie, WilmerHale, Jenner & Block, Susman Godfrey — face ongoing executive retaliation, demonstrating the cost of non-compliance; (5) The DEI cessation requirement in the law firm deals extends DEI elimination from the federal government (EO 14151) and federal grantees (Dismantle DEI Act Title IV) into the private legal profession — the sector that defends nonprofits, universities, and cultural institutions against government overreach.

MISCELLANEOUS RECEIPTS ACT THEORY: 31 U.S.C. 3302(b) requires that money received for the government be deposited into the Treasury. If ~$1 billion in pro bono services directed by the Executive Branch constitutes government receipts diverted outside the appropriations process, it represents a constitutional separation-of-powers violation — Congress controls the power of the purse, not the President. Congressional Democrats have also raised potential violations of federal bribery statutes, anti-fraud laws, RICO, and extortion laws, though these are not addressed in the bill.

LEGISLATIVE PROSPECTS: With only Democratic sponsorship and Republican House majority, the bill has minimal prospects for advancement in the 119th Congress. Its primary function is to create a legislative record documenting the law firm deals and to establish the Miscellaneous Receipts Act framework for potential future enforcement.
actions.affected_targets: Harvard University,Open Society Foundations

actions.title: National Archives (NARA) Staff RIF, $60M Budget Cut, NHPRC Defunded, Office of Innovation Eliminated
actions.status: Implemented — 64 probationary employees fired (February 2025); 150+ staff lost by June 2025; ~100 additional RIF notices (June 2025); FY2026 proposes $414.7M ($60M below FY2025, $93M below FY2024)
actions.description:
NARA has experienced cascading staff reductions and a proposed $60 million budget cut that threaten the nation's archival infrastructure. The Office of Innovation (50 FTE, responsible for digitization, online catalog, and 'Citizen Archivist' public transcription) was eliminated in the first RIF round. The field office and presidential library support division was also eliminated. The FY2026 budget proposes cutting 136 additional FTE including 23 positions across the presidential library system, and completely defunding the National Historical Publications and Records Commission (NHPRC) — the federal grant-making body for historical records preservation. The Electronic Records Initiative faces a 33% cut; if enacted, electronic records funding would be 56% below 2007 levels despite exponential growth in digital government records.

MILITARY AND VETERANS RECORDS: NARA had just cleared its pandemic-era backlog of veterans records requests at the National Personnel Records Center (NPRC) in St. Louis as of January 2024. Staff reductions risk re-creating backlogs for the military service records that veterans and their families need for VA benefits, burial eligibility, and historical documentation. Tribal veterans — who serve at the highest per-capita military rate of any demographic — depend on NARA records to access VA cemetery benefits documented in the tracker (id 26, NPS preservation programs). The $50M+ budget cut delays the digitization of decades of paper military personnel records.

TRIBAL AND INDIGENOUS RECORDS: NARA maintains Bureau of Indian Affairs Record Group 75, including tribal rolls, land records, treaty documents, and allotment records — many existing only in paper or microfilm at physical NARA locations. These records are the evidentiary foundation for tribal enrollment, land claims, trust responsibility enforcement, and the Cobell-type litigation documented in the tracker (id 27). Reduced field office staffing and elimination of digitization programs impede access for the 574 federally recognized tribes whose sovereignty documentation is held in federal archives.

IMMIGRATION AND NATURALIZATION RECORDS: NARA holds Immigration and Naturalization Service records (Record Group 85) essential for citizenship verification, family reunification, and historical research — particularly relevant for communities targeted by border enforcement (EO 14159, id 16) and sanctuary city actions (EO 14287, id 17).

FEDERAL REGISTER: The Office of the Federal Register, which publishes the official journal of the federal government, is a division of NARA. While no specific OFR staff cuts have been reported, the $60M overall budget reduction creates operational risk for the publication mechanism through which executive orders, agency rules, and public notices reach the public.

ARCHIVIST VACANCY: Former Archivist Colleen Shogan publicly stated she did not see how NARA could withstand a $60M cut. Secretary of State Marco Rubio served simultaneously as Acting Archivist — adding NARA to his concurrent roles as statutory Kennedy Center trustee and effectively Acting USAID Administrator.

TRACKER CROSS-REFERENCES: NARA's degradation compounds threats documented across the tracker: (1) NPS preservation programs (id 26) depend on NARA-held documentation for National Register nominations; (2) tribal population data crisis (id 19) is worsened by reduced access to BIA archival records; (3) Japanese American incarceration documentation (JACS/JACE programs, id 26) relies on War Relocation Authority records held at NARA; (4) African American genealogical research depends on NARA-held Freedmen's Bureau, slavery-era, and military records — directly relevant to NMAAHC programming; (5) the NHPRC defunding eliminates the only federal grant program specifically supporting state and local historical records preservation.

SOURCE: Government Executive (June 2025); Federal News Network (June 2025); Society of American Archivists.
actions.affected_targets: National Museum of African American History and Culture,Japanese American National Museum,Tribal Libraries (28+ institutions),National Congress of American Indians

actions.title: America250 Semiquincentennial Commemoration: Federal Funding Gutted, White House Parallel Entity Created, Programming Fractured
actions.status: Active — NEH 'A More Perfect Union' grants terminated (1,400+ grants April 2025; partial court-ordered restoration August 2025); NEA Challenge America cancelled; IMLS preparation funding terminated; Freedom 250 parallel entity created; only $25M of $150M congressional appropriation disbursed
actions.description:
The United States Semiquincentennial — the 250th anniversary of the Declaration of Independence (July 4, 2026) — is proceeding in a deeply fractured state. Congress created the America250 Commission in July 2016, composed of 8 congressional members, 16 citizen members, and 12 nonvoting ex officio members representing federal agencies (NEH, NEA, IMLS, NPS, Smithsonian). Congress authorized $150 million for the commemoration. As of early 2026, only $25 million has been disbursed. The remaining funds face redirection to Freedom 250, a White House-created private fundraising vehicle.

FEDERAL CULTURAL INFRASTRUCTURE ELIMINATED: The commemoration's cultural programming depended on the same agencies being simultaneously defunded: NEH had awarded more than $85 million through its 'A More Perfect Union' initiative for humanities projects across all 50 states — 1,400+ grants terminated April 2025 (partially restored by court order August 2025, then reorganized toward 'patriotic celebrations'). NEA's Challenge America program — the primary rural arts access mechanism — cancelled February 6, 2025 (id 48). IMLS preparation funding ($1M FY2024) terminated. The nation's 250th anniversary commemoration in 2026 proceeds without the federal arts, humanities, and library infrastructure that was being built to contextualize it through diverse community voices.

FREEDOM 250 PARALLEL ENTITY: The White House created Freedom 250 as a parallel private fundraising vehicle, diverting an estimated $10 million from the congressional commission by end of 2025. Freedom 250 donor tiers offer VIP access: $500,000 for preferred seating at all events; $1 million for a private reception with President Trump and a 'historic photo opportunity.' A February 2026 congressional hearing featured accusations that the administration was using Freedom 250 to 'hijack the country's 250th anniversary and sell access, hide donors, and rewrite history.' Democratic senators launched a transparency probe.

COMMISSION LEADERSHIP CRISIS: Two Trump allies were added to the America250 board in November 2025: Meredith O'Rourke (Trump's top fundraiser) and Chris LaCivita (Trump's 2024 campaign manager). A prior Trump appointee, Ariel Abergel, was terminated as executive director and told Fox News the commission 'has been plagued by years of dysfunction, greed and cronyism.'

STATE-LEVEL COLLAPSE: 25 states report receiving no federal funding beyond a one-time $10,000 grant from the commission. For comparison, the 1976 Bicentennial provided states the inflation-adjusted equivalent of more than $250,000 each in 1972 alone — four years before the celebration. The overall federal investment in the 250th anniversary is dramatically smaller than the Bicentennial.

COMMUNITY IMPACT — WHO TELLS THE STORY: The commemoration's fracture determines whose history is told in the nation's 250th anniversary. NEH's terminated 'A More Perfect Union' initiative explicitly listed equity and climate as program pillars — directly targeted by EO 14151. The reorganization toward 'patriotic celebrations' and 'American exceptionalism' narrows the interpretive framework, potentially excluding: African American history (slavery, Reconstruction, civil rights — foundational to the 250-year arc); Indigenous perspectives (the Declaration's characterization of Native peoples as 'merciless Indian Savages'); Latino history (Spanish colonial presence predating 1776 by centuries); Asian American and immigrant contributions; and the contested histories of communities documented throughout the tracker. The state humanities councils that would have organized local programming lost their $65M in NEH funding (id 8). NMAAHC, NMAI, the planned Latino Museum, and the Japanese American National Museum — all facing budget cuts and content review — are the institutions that would contextualize the 250th anniversary for their communities.

SOURCES: America250 Commission; Congressional hearing records (February 2026); NEH 'A More Perfect Union' initiative; NPR; Center for Western Priorities.
actions.affected_targets: National Museum of African American History and Culture,National Museum of the American Indian,National Museum of the American Latino (Planned),Japanese American National Museum,Smithsonian Institution

actions.title: Librarian of Congress Carla Hayden Fired — First African American, First Woman in Role; Executive Branch Seizes Legislative Branch Institution
actions.status: Implemented — Hayden fired May 8, 2025 via two-sentence email; Deputy AG Todd Blanche (Trump's former personal defense lawyer) installed as acting Librarian; Principal Deputy Robert Newlen also fired; constitutionality disputed
actions.description:
Dr. Carla Hayden, the 14th Librarian of Congress, was fired on May 8, 2025, via a two-sentence email from the Deputy Director of Presidential Personnel: 'Carla, on behalf of President Donald J. Trump, I am writing to inform you that your position as the Librarian of Congress is terminated effective immediately.' No reason was provided. White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt later cited 'quite concerning things that she had done at the Library of Congress in the pursuit of DEI and putting inappropriate books in the library for children.'

HISTORIC FIRSTS: Dr. Hayden was the first African American Librarian of Congress, the first woman to hold the post (in its 216-year history), and the first professional librarian in the role since 1974. She was nominated by President Obama on February 24, 2016, confirmed by the Senate 74-18 on July 13, 2016, and sworn in by Chief Justice John Roberts on September 14, 2016. Under the 2016 reform (first term limit for the position), her 10-year term was set to expire September 2026 — more than a year after her firing.

SEPARATION OF POWERS CRISIS: The Library of Congress is a legislative branch agency serving Congress under Article I of the Constitution. The President (executive branch) unilaterally removed the head of a legislative branch institution — an action with no clear precedent. Daniel Schuman, executive director of the American Governance Institute, stated: 'You can't name an executive branch official to head a legislative branch agency. It is unconstitutional, illegal and imprudent.' The firing represents an erosion of Article I constitutional prerogatives — the executive branch asserting control over an institution Congress created to serve itself.

ACTING LIBRARIAN — TODD BLANCHE: President Trump named Deputy Attorney General Todd Blanche — his former personal defense lawyer in the 2024 hush money trial — as acting Librarian of Congress. Blanche has no library, archives, or cultural institution experience. When Blanche and another Trump-appointed official arrived at LOC with an appointment letter, library staff called Capitol Police; the general counsel informed them that Principal Deputy Librarian Robert Newlen was the recognized acting librarian per standard protocol. Newlen was subsequently also fired. The legality of an executive branch official (DOJ's Deputy AG) heading a legislative branch agency remains disputed.

LOC INSTITUTIONAL SCALE: Dr. Hayden had requested an FY2026 budget of $946.2 million — a $48.4M increase (5.4%) over FY2025. The Library of Congress is the world's largest library, with 173 million items in 470 languages. Its collections include the papers of 23 presidents, original copies of the Declaration of Independence and Constitution, and the Gershwin, Coolidge, and Koussevitzky collections. LOC houses the Congressional Research Service (the source of every CRS report in this tracker's analytical sources), the Copyright Office, and the Law Library.

COPYRIGHT OFFICE IMPACT: Register of Copyrights Shira Perlmutter — appointed by and responsible to the Librarian — sued to dispute the legality of her own dismissal. The Copyright Office administers the nation's intellectual property registration system, affecting every artist, musician, author, filmmaker, and cultural creator whose work is documented by the institutions in this tracker.

CONGRESSIONAL RESPONSE: House Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries condemned the firing. Rep. Joe Morelle (Ranking Member, House Administration Committee) announced legislation to make the Librarian of Congress appointed by Congress rather than the President. Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer called for 'making the position of Librarian of Congress appointed by a congressional commission.' After her firing, Hayden was appointed senior fellow at the Andrew W. Mellon Foundation (July 7, 2025).

KENNEDY CENTER STATUTORY CONNECTION: The Librarian of Congress is a specified statutory member of the Kennedy Center Board of Trustees. Hayden's firing created a vacancy automatically filled by whatever replacement the executive branch installs — giving the President indirect influence over a Kennedy Center board seat that was designed to represent the legislative branch (id 9).

TRACKER CROSS-REFERENCES: The firing intersects with: (1) CRS reports — every analytical source in this tracker was produced by LOC's Congressional Research Service, whose institutional independence is threatened by executive branch control of its parent institution; (2) Kennedy Center governance (id 9) — Librarian is a statutory trustee; (3) DEI elimination (EO 14151, id 1) — the stated rationale; (4) NARA degradation (id 43) — parallel targeting of the nation's archival/library infrastructure; (5) NMAAHC (Hayden's firing removes the first Black leader of a cultural institution who helped shape the relationship between LOC and NMAAHC's research collections).

SOURCES: NPR (May 9, 2025); Library of Congress official records; Roll Call; Library Journal; Baltimore Sun.
actions.affected_targets: National Museum of African American History and Culture,John F. Kennedy Center for the Performing Arts

actions.title: H.R.899 — Department of Education Termination Act
actions.status: Pending — referred to House Committee on Education and Workforce; 36 Republican cosponsors; reintroduced every Congress since 115th (2017); companion executive order signed March 20, 2025
actions.description:
H.R.899, introduced by Rep. Thomas Massie (R-KY-4) on January 31, 2025, is a single-sentence bill: 'The Department of Education shall terminate on December 31, 2026.' The bill has 36 Republican cosponsors (including Reps. Biggs, Boebert, Brecheen, Burchett, Burlison, Cline, Clyde, Collins). Massie has introduced this identical bill in every Congress since the 115th (2017). Senate companion introduced by Sens. Rand Paul (R-KY), Mike Lee (R-UT), and Bernie Moreno (R-OH).

NO TRANSITION PLAN: The bill contains no provisions for transferring existing programs. It does not specify what happens to: Pell Grants (~$30B/year in student financial aid); Title I funding for low-income schools; IDEA special education mandates; Title IX civil rights enforcement; federal student loan administration (~$1.6 trillion portfolio); or the $92.050M in tribal college programs documented in the tracker (id 27).

EXECUTIVE ORDER PARALLEL: On March 20, 2025, President Trump signed an executive order directing the closure of the Department of Education 'to the maximum extent appropriate under applicable law,' directing student loan functions to the Small Business Administration and special education programs to HHS. These transfers require congressional authorization to be legally completed.

TRACKER SIGNIFICANCE — EDUCATION FUNDING AT STAKE:

TRIBAL COLLEGE PROGRAMS ($92.050M, id 27): ED administers the two largest tribal college programs — Strengthening TCCU ($80.097M, HEA Title III) and Tribally Controlled Postsecondary Career and Technical Institutions ($11.953M, P.L. 109-270). These are the same Title III programs covering Strengthening HBCUs and Strengthening Hispanic-Serving Institutions. H.R. 899 would eliminate the agency administering all three simultaneously.

HBCU FUNDING: The Department of Education is the primary federal funder of Historically Black Colleges and Universities through Title III (Strengthening HBCUs), Title V (Developing HSIs), and institutional aid programs. Elimination without transfer provisions would create immediate funding crises for 100+ HBCUs — institutions whose historic properties are documented through IMLS grants (P.L. 108-184, id 6) and the Historic Preservation Tax Credit ($130B pipeline, id 47).

MORRILL ACT LINEAGE (id 27): H.R. 899 and USDA workforce reductions simultaneously threaten the ED Title III programs supporting 1890 HBCUs and the USDA programs supporting 1994 tribal land-grant colleges — cutting both legs of the Morrill Act's 132-year inclusionary tradition (1862→1890→1994).

TITLE IX AND CIVIL RIGHTS: ED's Office for Civil Rights enforces Title IX (gender equity), Title VI (racial discrimination), and disability rights across all federally funded educational institutions. Elimination removes the federal enforcement mechanism for civil rights in education.

HARVARD AND UNIVERSITY TARGETS: The Department of Education is the agency threatening federal funding to Harvard (id 12) and Columbia (id 22). Paradoxically, eliminating ED would remove the enforcement mechanism the administration is using against these institutions — but would also eliminate the civil rights framework protecting students at those institutions.

LEGISLATIVE PROSPECTS: Requires an act of Congress; faces the 60-vote Senate filibuster threshold. GovTrack assigns 25% progression rating. Despite the companion executive order, the actual legislative elimination remains unlikely under current political conditions.

SOURCES: Congress.gov; GovTrack; 19th News; Rep. Massie press releases.
actions.affected_targets: National Museum of African American History and Culture,Tribal Libraries (28+ institutions),National Congress of American Indians

actions.title: Historic Preservation Infrastructure Under Compound Attack: HPF 94% Cut Proposed, NPS Certification Staff Decimated, SHPOs Defunded, $130B Tax Credit Pipeline Constricted
actions.status: Active — FY2026 presidential budget proposes HPF cut from $204.5M to $11M (94% reduction, preserving only $11M for HBCUs); Congress pushing back with $205.059M (marginally above FY2023's $204.5M peak — CRS IF12112); SHPOs already laying off staff; NPS preservation staffing cut from 209 to 32 FTE; Section 106 regulatory review initiated February 2026
actions.description:
The federal historic preservation infrastructure — a three-agency partnership (NPS, IRS, State Historic Preservation Offices) that has leveraged approximately $130 billion in private investment since 1976 — faces compound degradation from simultaneous budget proposals, staff reductions, and regulatory review. This entry consolidates the preservation-specific threats dispersed across multiple tracker entries (ids 6, 14, 26) into a single structural analysis.

HISTORIC PRESERVATION FUND (HPF): The FY2026 presidential budget proposes slashing HPF from $204.5M (FY2023 baseline) to $11 million — a 94% reduction. The only preserved funding: $11M specifically for HBCU preservation. This would eliminate: all State Historic Preservation Office (SHPO) grants, all Tribal Historic Preservation Office (THPO) grants, Save America's Treasures, Underrepresented Communities grants, and all competitive grant programs. Congress is pushing back: a bipartisan conference agreement provides $205.059M — the highest HPF level in the program's history, with $40M for Save America's Treasures. FY2025 funds ($188.7M appropriated) were withheld for months; a Notice of Funding Opportunity was not released to SHPOs until July 1, 2025, creating severe cash flow crises.

STATE HISTORIC PRESERVATION OFFICES (SHPOs) — PIPELINE COLLAPSE: SHPOs are the first step in the Historic Preservation Tax Credit pipeline — they review Part 1 applications certifying buildings as historic before NPS reviews rehabilitation plans. Ohio SHPO laid off 12 of 36 employees (one-third of staff) due to non-payment of approximately $1.45 million in federal funds. When SHPOs cannot process applications, the entire $130 billion private investment pipeline stalls. State and local governments are the primary actors in historic preservation, but federal program eliminations shift uncompensated burdens to sub-national governments already operating under fiscal constraint (CRS IF12446).

NPS TECHNICAL PRESERVATION SERVICES: NPS approves approximately 1,200 Historic Preservation Tax Credit projects per year, leveraging nearly $6 billion annually in private investment. NPS recreation and preservation funding faces an 85% cut with staffing reduced from 209 to 32 FTE. Approximately 250 NPS archaeologists have already been cut. While specific Technical Preservation Services cuts have not been separately reported, the broader NPS staffing decimation creates serious risk for processing the annual Part 2/Part 3 HTC certification applications that are the gateway to the 20% federal tax credit.

HISTORIC PRESERVATION TAX CREDIT (20% HTC): The credit itself was not modified in the 'One Big Beautiful Bill' reconciliation (P.L. signed July 4, 2025). The Historic Tax Credit Growth and Opportunity Act (HTC-GO, H.R. 2941/S. 1459), which would have reverted the HTC from its weakened post-2017 form to a more usable one-year credit, was NOT included. The credit remains in its Tax Cuts and Jobs Act form — less usable than pre-2017 — while the administrative infrastructure to process it is simultaneously degraded.

SECTION 106 REGULATORY REVIEW: In February 2026, ACHP Vice Chairman Travis Voyles directed a comprehensive review of the 36 CFR Part 800 regulations implementing Section 106 of the National Historic Preservation Act, looking to 'streamline' requirements. Section 106 requires federal agencies to consider effects on historic properties before undertaking projects. Weakening Section 106 undermines the foundational review process that protects historic properties from federal undertakings — and removes the consultation requirement that gives tribal nations a voice in protecting ancestral sites.

COMMUNITIES OF COLOR — DISPROPORTIONATE IMPACT:

AFRICAN AMERICAN PROPERTIES: The Equal Justice Initiative has documented 'the widespread failure to preserve African American history,' noting National Register designations are rarely granted in less affluent areas where they are most needed. HBCU preservation ($11M preserved in FY2026 proposal) is a fraction of total need — NPS has awarded over $60M to 80+ HBCUs since the 1990s. The National Trust has listed HBCUs on its 11 Most Endangered Historic Places list since 1998. African American historic districts, Black church buildings, and Freedmen's communities depend on the SHPO-NPS pipeline now being constricted.

TRIBAL PROPERTIES: The proposed FY2026 budget includes a 94% cut in THPO funding, effectively shutting down Tribal Historic Preservation Offices. This eliminates the capacity for tribes to conduct Section 106 reviews on their own properties and manage their own cultural heritage — a direct attack on tribal self-determination in historic preservation.

LATINO HERITAGE: Spanish colonial mission structures, ranchos, and border-region historic properties eligible for Historic Preservation Tax Credit rehabilitation face reduced SHPO capacity to process nominations and certifications.

COUNTER-CYCLICAL ECONOMIC IMPACT (CRS IF12446): The arts and culture sector — of which historic preservation is a measured component — represents 4.4% of U.S. GDP ($1.02 trillion). The sector grew 13.7% in 2021 vs. 5.9% overall GDP, demonstrating counter-cyclical resilience. The $130 billion in private investment leveraged through the Historic Preservation Tax Credit since 1976 across 48,000+ projects makes it one of the most cost-effective federal economic development programs in U.S. history. Constricting this pipeline during economic uncertainty is strategically damaging — communities lose a proven private investment catalyst precisely when they need economic diversification most.

NPS FY2025 APPROPRIATIONS BASELINE — PRECISION CORRECTION (CRS IF12713): The CRS confirms HPF FY2025 enacted was $168.9 million — DOWN 10% from FY2024's $188.7 million. The tracker's existing '$204.5M' figure is the FY2023 peak from CRS IF12446; the FY2025 level represents an 18% decline from that peak in just two fiscal years before the FY2026 proposed 94% cut. The 10% FY2025 cut has a DIRECT, MECHANISTIC connection to THPO and SHPO capacity: under NHPA (54 U.S.C. §§300101 et seq.), HPF formula grants flow directly to SHPOs and THPOs. For the 574 federally recognized tribes with THPOs, this is a formula-based cut — automatic, not discretionary. The HAC Summary noted HPF reductions were specifically to remove community project funding/congressionally directed spending (earmarks) — meaning communities that had secured targeted historic preservation grants lost those funds.

LEGACY RESTORATION FUND SUNSET — COMPOUND CRISIS (CRS IF12713): FY2025 is the FINAL YEAR of authorized funding for the Great American Outdoors Act's Legacy Restoration Fund (GAOA; P.L. 116-152; 54 U.S.C. §§200401 et seq.). The LRF provided ~$1.3 billion annually (70% of $1.9B cap) to NPS for deferred maintenance at historic structures and cultural sites — approximately $6.5 billion over five years (FY2021-FY2025). Without Congressional reauthorization, the $22.3 billion deferred maintenance backlog (CRS R42757, FY2022) loses its primary dedicated funding source. This means the 94% proposed HPF cut, the 209→32 FTE staffing decimation, AND the LRF sunset hit simultaneously — a triple compound crisis for historic preservation infrastructure with no precedent.

CONSTRUCTION ACCOUNT GAP (CRS IF12713): The FY2025 Construction account ($172.3M) was 27% below the Biden request ($237.2M) — a $64.9 million gap in facility repair and replacement. Combined with LRF sunset and HPF cuts, this means every funding pipeline for maintaining historic structures is simultaneously constricted.

PRE-EXISTING $239M SHORTFALL (CRS IF12713): NPS total FY2025 discretionary of $3.337 billion was already 7% below the Biden request of $3.576 billion — a $239 million shortfall BEFORE DOGE cuts began. The 209→32 FTE reduction compounds an already underfunded system, not one at baseline adequacy.

NR&P TRIBAL LAND MANAGER CUTS (CRS IF12713): The National Recreation and Preservation account was cut 2% (from $91.2M to $89.6M). This is the account funding outdoor recreation planning and cultural preservation grants to tribal governments as land managers and national heritage area programming — further reducing tribal preservation capacity on top of the HPF formula grant cut.

LWCF AND SACRED SITE ACCESS (CRS IF12713; CRS IF12256): The Land and Water Conservation Fund (LWCF), designated mandatory under GAOA (P.L. 116-152), funds NPS acquisition of nonfederal inholdings inside park unit boundaries. Failure to direct LWCF funds toward acquisitions adjacent to tribal cultural sites leaves private inholdings that can impede tribal access to sacred places. P.L. 119-4 did not specify LWCF allocations, leaving decisions to executive discretion under an administration that has deprioritized tribal co-stewardship.

COMPLETE FOUR-YEAR HPF TRAJECTORY (CRS IF12112 + IF12436 + IF12713): FY2022 $173.1M → FY2023 $204.5M (+18%, PEAK — Congress exceeded Biden's $151.8M request by $52.7M) → FY2024 $188.7M (–8%) → FY2025 $168.9M (–10%) → FY2026 $205M enacted (+21% from depressed FY2025, but only $500K above FY2023 peak). The FY2026 '$205M record' claim in this tracker is CORRECTED: it marginally exceeds a level Congress already achieved in FY2023. Cumulative FY2023-FY2025 loss: $35.6M (–17.4%). The FY2023 congressional override (35% above Biden's HPF request) demonstrates that bipartisan HPF support existed and was deliberately reversed by the Republican House majority in FY2024/FY2025.

FY2023 CONSTRUCTION PEAK — ORIGIN OF THE COLLAPSE (CRS IF12112): Construction peaked at $239.8M in FY2023 (+6% from FY2022's $226.0M) before collapsing 28% to $172.3M in FY2024 and holding flat through FY2025. The complete four-year trajectory: $226.0M → $239.8M → $172.3M → $172.3M. The FY2023 peak makes the FY2024 collapse even more striking — a $67.5M single-year cut to the account that funds infrastructure at culturally significant sites.

NPS SYSTEM GROWTH COMPOUNDS THE CRISIS (CRS IF12112-IF12713): 424 units (FY2023) → 429 (FY2024) → 433 (FY2025). Nine new units added while the budget declined — compressing per-unit historic preservation resources before DOGE cuts.

DISASTER SUPPLEMENTALS AND PRESERVATION (CRS IF12112): FY2023 received $1.500 billion in disaster supplementals (P.L. 117-328 Div. N) — 43% of the regular NPS discretionary total. FY2025 received $2.313 billion (P.L. 118-58). These respond to climate-driven disasters at parks containing historic structures. DOGE cuts eliminated staff who would administer these disaster response funds — meaning appropriated disaster money may go unspent while historic structures deteriorate.

MULTI-YEAR HPF TRAJECTORY — TWO CONSECUTIVE CUTS BEFORE DOGE (CRS IF12436 + IF12713): The full HPF trajectory reveals systematic erosion: FY2023 $204.5M → FY2024 $188.7M (–8%, P.L. 118-42) → FY2025 $168.9M (–10%, P.L. 119-4) → FY2026 proposed $11M (–94%). Cumulatively, HPF lost $35.6 million (–17.4%) between FY2023 and FY2025 BEFORE the proposed 94% FY2026 cut. THPO capacity — the ability of 574 tribes to hire staff, conduct archaeological surveys, participate in Section 106 consultation, and protect sacred sites — was being systematically reduced for TWO CONSECUTIVE YEARS before DOGE. The FY2024 HPF also funded HBCU preservation, Save America's Treasures, and U.S. Semiquincentennial (250th) sites — making it directly relevant to African-descendant communities beyond NHPA offices.

CONSTRUCTION ACCOUNT COLLAPSE (CRS IF12436): The Construction account fell 28% from FY2023 to FY2024 ($239.8M → $172.3M) — the single sharpest cut in the NPS budget — then held flat through FY2025. The FY2024 request-to-enacted gap was $114.3M (40% below Biden's $286.6M request). Combined with the LRF sunset after FY2025, every funding pipeline for maintaining historic structures was simultaneously constricted across multiple fiscal years.

NR&P HERITAGE AREAS — $29.2M COMMUNITY PIPELINE (CRS IF12436): Of the $91.2M FY2024 NR&P appropriation, $29.2 million was specifically for national heritage areas — congressionally designated landscapes where NPS partners with communities. These include: Gullah Geechee Cultural Heritage Corridor (African American, SC/GA/FL/NC); Freedom's Frontier NHA (Underground Railroad); Mississippi Delta NHA; Yuma Crossing NHA (Quechan and Cocopah tribes); El Camino Real de Tierra Adentro (Latino heritage). The 1% NR&P cut from FY2023 to FY2024 reduced these community-facing grants.

LRF FY2024 IRONY (CRS IF12436): Congress appropriated $1.338 billion for LRF deferred maintenance in FY2024 — EXCEEDING NPS's own request of $1.330B. Congress was actively accelerating deferred maintenance resolution the year before DOGE fired the workforce that would have done the work.

CENTENNIAL CHALLENGE CUT (CRS IF12436): The matching fund fell 20% from FY2023 ($15.0M → $12.0M), reducing the federal incentive for community-driven park preservation projects — disproportionately affecting communities with less robust philanthropic infrastructure.

NPS SHUTDOWN IMPACT ON HISTORIC PRESERVATION (CRS IF11079): During the 43-day October–November 2025 shutdown, national historic sites consisting solely of enclosed buildings were fully closed per the September 2025 contingency plan — CRS IF11079 confirms this category 'would be fully closed in a shutdown.' This disproportionately affected civil rights sites (Selma to Montgomery, Birmingham Civil Rights, Harriet Tubman, Frederick Douglass, Brown v. Board), Indigenous heritage sites, and immigration history sites (Angel Island, Ellis Island) — the NPS units most central to communities of color. Meanwhile, recreational parks with FLREA fee infrastructure remained partially open, creating a two-tier system where cultural heritage received less protection than recreation. All interpretive programming, cultural resource monitoring, archaeological site protection, NAGPRA compliance, Section 106 consultation, and museum collection care were suspended. Volunteer programs involving tribal cultural practitioners and community historians were discontinued. The $348M FLREA balance used for shutdown operations reduced future maintenance funding (55% of fees directed to deferred maintenance), compounding the HPF and Construction funding trajectories documented above. See id 73 for full shutdown analysis. (CRS IF11079, https://crsreports.congress.gov/product/pdf/IF/IF11079)

AFFILIATED AREAS: PRECARIOUS PRESERVATION TIER (CRS IF11281): Beyond the 433-unit National Park System, 32 NPS affiliated areas receive only NPS technical assistance and cooperative agreement support — no guaranteed appropriations. The Brown v. Board of Education affiliated schools (5 sites in DE, VA, DC) were designated by Secretary Haaland in January 2025 under P.L. 117-123 — days before the administration changed — as the most recently designated and most legally precarious federal civil rights sites. Affiliated areas consisting solely of enclosed buildings were fully closed during the 43-day 2025 shutdown (id 73). The absence of any statutory definition for affiliated area status means the current administration could rescind administrative designations without congressional action. See id 74 for full analysis. (CRS IF11281, https://crsreports.congress.gov/product/pdf/IF/IF11281)

HPF DECADE OF GROWTH NOW REVERSING (CRS R42757): HPF grew 185% inflation-adjusted ($56.4M FY2014 → $204.5M FY2023). THPO grants approximately doubled in real dollars — a bipartisan commitment now being reversed. Heritage Partnership Program (61 national heritage areas) grew 26% inflation-adjusted, overriding Obama and Trump I cut requests. The Trump II FY2026 proposal is the third attempt — now with a Congress that may not override. Centennial Challenge ($15M FY2023, 1:1 match) cut to $12M. Supplementals totaled $2.115B (FY2018–FY2023), including $50M HPF for Section 106 hurricane compliance in Puerto Rico/USVI. (CRS R42757, https://crsreports.congress.gov/product/pdf/R/R42757)

SOURCES: National Trust for Historic Preservation; NCSHPO; CRS Report IF12446 (June 29, 2023); Providence Preservation Society; ACHP meeting records (February 2026); CRS In Focus IF12713 Version 7 (Updated September 30, 2025), https://crsreports.congress.gov/product/pdf/IF/IF12713; CRS In Focus IF12436 Version 6 (Updated July 23, 2024), https://crsreports.congress.gov/product/pdf/IF/IF12436; CRS In Focus IF12112 Version 5 (Updated June 26, 2023), https://crsreports.congress.gov/product/pdf/IF/IF12112; CRS In Focus IF12256 (LWCF FAQs), https://crsreports.congress.gov/product/details?prodcode=IF12256.

actions.title: NEA Challenge America Cancelled — Primary Federal Arts Access Program for Underserved and Rural Communities Eliminated
actions.status: Implemented — NEA announced cancellation February 6, 2025; FY2025 grants (announced January 2025) suspended; FY2026 cycle eliminated; partial private philanthropy replacement ($800K from Warhol/Frankenthaler Foundations covers ~30% of lost funding)
actions.description:
On February 6, 2025, the NEA announced the cancellation of Challenge America — its dedicated grant program providing $10,000 matching grants to small arts organizations (operating budgets under $250,000) that extended arts access to underserved groups and communities. The program funded approximately 272 grants per year (~$2.72M) from roughly 563 eligible applications. Each grant required a minimum $10,000 cost share/match, meaning the program leveraged $5.44M+ annually in combined federal and matched investment.

PROGRAM DEFINITION — WHAT WAS LOST: Challenge America was the ONLY federal arts program specifically designed for small organizations serving communities whose access to arts is limited by geography, ethnicity, economics, or disability — the four categories defined in NEA's authorizing legislation. The $250,000 operating budget cap ensured grants reached organizations too small to compete in the broader Grants for Arts Projects (GAP) category. These are the organizations with no endowment buffer, no development staff, no alternative federal funding pipeline.

STATED RATIONALE: The NEA stated it would refocus resources on 'celebrating and honoring the 250th anniversary of the signing of the Declaration of Independence' — but the America250 commemoration itself is proceeding in a deeply fractured state (id 44), and the administration's FY2026 budget proposes eliminating NEA entirely. The cancellation aligned with EO 14151's 60-day deadline for terminating DEI-related programs — Challenge America's explicit focus on 'underserved communities' made it a target under this framework.

COMMUNITIES MOST AFFECTED:

RURAL COMMUNITIES: Organizations in rural Alaska, Appalachia, and other remote areas that had few alternative funding sources. Roger Schmidt, executive director of Sitka Fine Arts Camp (rural Alaska), called the cancellation 'a political and ideological stunt.' CRS IF12446 documents that rural arts organizations have less capacity to absorb federal funding loss than urban counterparts — no endowments, no alternative grant pipelines. The arts sector's independence from complex supply chains makes it especially valuable for rural economic diversification.

DISABILITY COMMUNITIES: Organizations like ConnectAbility, Inc. in rural Dahlonega, Georgia, offering workshops for people with developmental and physical disabilities. These organizations serve populations explicitly protected by the ADA but excluded from the market-based arts economy.

COMMUNITIES OF COLOR: The program's explicit targeting of communities limited by 'ethnicity' and 'economics' meant Indigenous, African American, Latino, and Asian American communities in underserved areas lost their primary federal arts access pathway.

CHILDREN AND FAMILIES: Programs like Kids & Art Foundation providing healing arts workshops for pediatric cancer patients.

NO SUCCESSOR PROGRAM: Former Challenge America applicants were told to apply to the broader Grants for Arts Projects (GAP) category instead — but GAP is more competitive, lacks the small-organization focus, and has no $250,000 budget cap. For FY2027, the NEA has indicated Challenge America will be 'embedded within Grants for Arts Projects' as a subcategory rather than a standalone program — diluting the dedicated pipeline.

PRIVATE PHILANTHROPY RESPONSE: The Andy Warhol Foundation and Helen Frankenthaler Foundation jointly committed $800,000 to provide $10,000 grants to 80 organizations whose Challenge America grants were cancelled — covering approximately 30% of the ~$2.7M that NEA was supposed to disburse. The Warhol Foundation expanded its grant program in February 2026 to support small-scale organizations. This private response, while commendable, confirms the CRS finding (IF12446) that there is no equivalent private-sector substitute for federal arts funding in underserved geographies.

ECONOMIC IMPACT (CRS IF12446): Challenge America funded organizations in the arts and culture sector that represents 4.4% of U.S. GDP ($1.02 trillion). The sector grew 13.7% in 2021 vs. 5.9% overall GDP, demonstrating counter-cyclical resilience that makes its defunding strategically damaging. Ann Markusen's research documents that arts activity generates downstream economic benefits in publishing, printing, broadcasting, and manufacturing — meaning even $2.72M in Challenge America grants produced multiplier effects disproportionate to their modest federal investment. For rural and tribal communities, these were often the only arts organizations — their loss removes both cultural programming and the economic diversification tool the CRS documents as having no private-sector equivalent.

TRACKER CROSS-REFERENCES: Challenge America's cancellation compounds: NEA mass terminations (id 7) — the broader 50%+ grant termination; America250 collapse (id 44) — the cancellation was justified by 250th anniversary refocusing that itself is fractured; IMLS tribal library/museum cuts (id 6) — the same rural and tribal communities losing library grants simultaneously lost their arts grants; CPB defunding (id 10) — rural communities losing public broadcasting, arts access, and library services simultaneously.

SOURCES: NEA press release (February 6, 2025); Hyperallergic; NPR; Washington Post; CRS Report IF12446 (June 29, 2023); Glasstire; Warhol Foundation.

actions.title: S.949 — Protect our Parks Act of 2025 (Senate)
actions.status: Introduced — referred to Senate Committee on Energy and Natural Resources; no hearing scheduled
actions.description:
Introduced March 11, 2025, by Sen. Mark Kelly (D-AZ) with 7 Democratic cosponsors including Sens. Gallego (D-AZ), Hickenlooper (D-CO), Shaheen (D-NH), Smith (D-MN), Van Hollen (D-MD), Slotkin (D-MI), and Baldwin (D-WI). The bill directs the Secretary of the Interior, using previously appropriated funds, to: (1) ensure all National Park System units are fully staffed for visitor safety, enjoyment, and resource protection; (2) fill all maintenance staff positions; and (3) reinstate any NPS employees involuntarily removed or terminated between January 20, 2025, and February 25, 2025. Section 4 mandates continuation of NPS projects previously funded under four laws: Federal Lands Recreation Enhancement Act (16 U.S.C. 6801 et seq.), Great American Outdoors Act (P.L. 116-152), Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act (P.L. 117-58), and Inflation Reduction Act of 2022 (P.L. 117-169). No new appropriations — relies entirely on previously appropriated funds. GovTrack estimates ~3% enactment probability. Two cosponsors sit on the Energy and Natural Resources Committee but the committee chair is not a cosponsor. No Republican support. The bill is a legislative response to NPS workforce reductions tracked in id 26 (NPS Preservation Programs) and id 47 (Historic Preservation Infrastructure Threat), where NPS staffing fell from 209 to 32 FTE.

TRACKER CROSS-REFERENCES: NPS Preservation Programs Degraded (id 26) — the staffing cuts this bill seeks to reverse; Historic Preservation Infrastructure Threat (id 47) — the 209→32 FTE reduction and HPF 94% cut; NARA RIF and Budget Cuts (id 43) — parallel federal cultural workforce decimation.

NPS FOUR-YEAR UNDERFUNDING CONTEXT (CRS IF12112 + IF12436 + IF12713): This bill operates against a four-year erosion of NPS capacity: NPS discretionary peaked at $3.475B in FY2023 — the high-water mark, where Congress overrode the President's request by 35% for HPF and funded Construction at $239.8M. FY2024 fell to $3.325B (–4%, all five accounts declined, Construction collapsed 28% to $172.3M). FY2025 was $3.337B (+<1%, nominal recovery masking real-dollar decline). The FY2026 proposal would cut to ~$2.12B (–36.6%). The Historic Preservation Fund trajectory: $173.1M (FY2022) → $204.5M (FY2023, the actual peak) → $188.7M (FY2024, –8%) → $168.9M (FY2025, –10%) → proposed $205M (FY2026, marginally above FY2023 but paired with 94% staffing cuts). The GAOA Legacy Restoration Fund (~$1.3B/year to NPS, 70% of the $1.9B cap) expired after FY2025, removing the primary dedicated funding source for the $22.3 billion deferred maintenance backlog (CRS R42757, FY2022). The Inflation Reduction Act sent $700M in one-time mandatory funding to NPS in FY2022. Meanwhile, the NPS system grew from 424 to 433 units on a shrinking budget. Congress appropriated $1.338B for deferred maintenance in FY2024 — exceeding NPS's own request — then DOGE fired the workforce that would have done the work. (CRS IF12112, https://crsreports.congress.gov/product/pdf/IF/IF12112; CRS IF12436, https://crsreports.congress.gov/product/pdf/IF/IF12436; CRS IF12713, https://crsreports.congress.gov/product/pdf/IF/IF12713)

SOURCES: Congress.gov; GovTrack; Sen. Kelly press release.

actions.title: H.R.1429 — Activating National Parks in Cities Act
actions.short_title: Activating National Parks in Cities Act
actions.status: Introduced — referred to House Committee on Natural Resources; no hearing scheduled; zero cosponsors
actions.description:
Introduced February 18, 2025, by Del. Eleanor Holmes Norton (D-DC) with zero cosponsors. The bill amends Title 54, United States Code (National Park Service and Related Programs) to expand the NPS mission to include 'active use' of urban park units. Specifically: (1) amends Section 100101(a) to add language promoting 'active use of System units located in urban areas'; (2) defines 'active use' as 'use that prioritizes current enjoyment by people and improves quality of life,' including playgrounds, pedestrian- and bicycle-friendly infrastructure, sports and recreation facilities, community events, programming, and concessions; (3) defines 'urban area' per the most recent decennial census. The bill expressly does not apply to wilderness areas. Motivated by a George Washington University report finding NPS parks in Washington, D.C. are 'inconsistently managed and inequitably maintained' and 'do not meet the needs of people who both live near and experience the park system as a part of their daily lives.' Nearly 90% of D.C.'s parkland is NPS-managed, with over two-thirds consisting of small neighborhood parks under one acre. GovTrack estimates ~4% enactment probability. Del. Norton has introduced similar legislation in prior Congresses.

COMMUNITY IMPACT: This bill addresses the equity dimension of NPS management — urban communities (disproportionately communities of color) whose neighborhood parks are federally managed but not maintained for active community use. The bill reframes parks as community infrastructure rather than purely conservation assets.

Bill findings note over 1,200 such settlements were established after the Civil War, with at least 200 towns founded by formerly enslaved individuals between 1866 and 1930.

GRANT PROGRAM: Funds identification of qualifying settlements; cultural heritage preservation and restoration; tourism development; research and documentation; capacity-building for site operations; and educational programming. PRIVATE PROPERTY PROTECTIONS: Grants require property owner consent; no mandated management changes without written agreement.

actions.title: H.R.177 — Yosemite National Park Equal Access and Fairness Act
actions.status: Introduced — referred to House Committee on Natural Resources; no hearing scheduled
actions.description:
Introduced January 3, 2025, by Rep. Tom McClintock (R-CA-5) with 3 Republican cosponsors: Reps. Calvert (R-CA), LaMalfa (R-CA), and Valadao (R-CA). The bill amends the Raker Act of 1913 — the federal law that granted San Francisco the right to dam Hetch Hetchy Valley in Yosemite National Park. Key provisions:

RENTAL FEE INCREASE: Raises San Francisco's annual rental fee from the current $30,000 (unchanged since 1913) to $2,000,000, with annual CPI adjustments. San Francisco's Hetch Hetchy Water and Power generates over $200M/year in revenue ($453M in water and power sales in 2018), making the $30K fee strikingly disproportionate. A 2020 study estimated the inflation-adjusted rental value at $800K/year; an 8% profit-sharing model would yield $36M/year.

COST RECOUPMENT PROHIBITION: Explicitly prohibits San Francisco from passing increased rental costs to water or power ratepayers.

RECREATIONAL ACCESS: Directs the Secretary of the Interior to administer Hetch Hetchy Reservoir and Lake Eleanor Basin for public recreation — swimming, non-motorized watercraft, camping (above high-water mark), picnicking, daytime hiking, and other appropriate activities. Currently road access to Hetch Hetchy Valley is closed, and boating and swimming are prohibited — unlike nearly every other major California reservoir.

WILDFIRE MITIGATION: Revenue from increased rent may fund wildfire mitigation in Yosemite and other California national parks.

This is a reintroduction (Rep. Conway introduced similar bill in 2022; McClintock reintroduced as H.R.519 in 118th Congress). GovTrack estimates ~2% enactment probability. Faces strong opposition from San Francisco's political delegation.

actions.title: S.3785 — North Rim and Kaibab National Forest Restoration Act of 2026 (Senate)
actions.status: Introduced — referred to Senate Committee on Energy and Natural Resources; no hearing scheduled
actions.description:
Introduced February 5, 2026, by Sen. Ruben Gallego (D-AZ) with 1 Democratic cosponsor: Sen. Mark Kelly (D-AZ). Senate companion to H.R.5729 (Rep. Crane) but with BROADER SCOPE covering both Grand Canyon National Park and Kaibab National Forest (involving both NPS and U.S. Forest Service). The bill responds to two devastating 2025 wildfires:

DRAGON BRAVO FIRE: Began July 4, 2025, in the North Rim of Grand Canyon National Park; contained September 29, 2025.
WHITE SAGE FIRE: Began near Fredonia, AZ, July 9, 2025; contained September 12, 2025.
Combined impact: 200,000+ acres burned, 113+ structures destroyed including the HISTORIC GRAND CANYON LODGE.

KEY PROVISIONS:

EMERGENCY ACQUISITION: Grants NPS and USFS authority to use emergency contracting procedures (Part 18, Title 48, CFR) including increased micro-purchase thresholds and simplified acquisition for: forest management/restoration (slash removal, erosion control, slope stabilization, tree regeneration, wildlife rehabilitation, native reseeding); rebuilding fire-affected structures; ground/facility improvements; and other recovery.

TRIBAL AND LOCAL BUSINESS PRIORITY: To the maximum extent practicable, must prioritize contracting with Tribal businesses and local businesses. Must invite applicable Indian Tribes to participate and engage with local stakeholders including governments, nonprofits, and community groups. This is a SIGNIFICANT DISTINCTION from the House companion (H.R.5729), which contains NO tribal provisions.

actions.title: S.1858 — National Cemeteries Holiday Access Act
actions.status: Introduced — referred to Senate Committee on Veterans' Affairs; no hearing scheduled; House companion H.R.5942 introduced by Rep. Downing (R-MT)
actions.description:
Introduced May 22, 2025 (ahead of Memorial Day), by Sen. Steve Daines (R-MT) with 1 Republican cosponsor. House companion is H.R.5942 (National Cemetery Access Act) introduced by Rep. Troy Downing (R-MT-02) with cosponsors Reps. Steube (R-FL) and Lawler (R-NY). The bill requires national cemeteries managed by THREE federal agencies to remain open to visitors on all federal legal public holidays as defined by 5 U.S.C. Section 6103(a).

AFFECTED AGENCIES AND CEMETERIES:
— Department of Veterans Affairs: 155+ national cemeteries under the National Cemetery Administration
— Department of Defense: Arlington National Cemetery and Soldiers' and Airmen's Home National Cemetery
— National Park Service: 14 national cemeteries within the NPS system

FEDERAL HOLIDAYS COVERED: New Year's Day, MLK Jr. Birthday, Presidents' Day, Memorial Day, Juneteenth, Independence Day, Labor Day, Columbus Day, Veterans Day, Thanksgiving, Christmas.

The bill responds to reports that some national cemeteries were closed to visitors on key remembrance holidays — preventing families from visiting graves and veteran organizations from conducting wreath-laying and memorial ceremonies. GovTrack estimates ~5% enactment probability. The bill has a narrow, non-controversial scope that could be folded into larger veterans' affairs legislative vehicles.

COMMUNITY IMPACT: Veteran families and Gold Star families; veteran service organizations that conduct memorial ceremonies; communities that rely on national cemeteries for remembrance traditions. The NPS dimension (14 cemeteries) connects this to the broader NPS staffing crisis — cemetery closures on holidays may be a staffing-driven problem rather than a policy choice.

TRACKER CROSS-REFERENCES: NPS Preservation Programs Degraded (id 26) — NPS manages 14 national cemeteries affected by this bill, and staffing decimation may be driving holiday closures.

SOURCES: Congress.gov; GovTrack; Sen. Daines press release.

actions.title: H.R.4931 — National Park System Long-Term Lease Investment Act
actions.status: Subcommittee on Federal Lands legislative hearing held December 2, 2025; Senate companion S.2498
actions.description:
Introduced August 8, 2025, by Rep. Gregory F. Murphy (R-NC-03) with 0 cosponsors. Senate companion S.2498. The bill authorizes the NPS Director to extend existing leases within National Park System units WITHOUT requiring competitive rebidding.

ELIGIBILITY: Lease in place at least 5 years; lessee in full compliance; NPS Director determines extension is in best interests of park administration.

REGULATORY BYPASS: Exempts qualifying extensions from Sections 18.7 and 18.8 of Part 18, Title 36, CFR — which currently require competitive RFPs and public bidding.

IMPLEMENTATION: Secretary must incorporate new authority into regulations within 90 days of enactment.

SCOPE: NPS manages 340+ leased structures (commercial, residential, nonprofit uses) across the system.

NPCA OPPOSES: 'Fair market value rent ensures the National Park Service secures the highest and best permitted use for leased structures.' Critics argue removing competitive bidding reduces park revenue.

GovTrack estimates ~3% enactment probability.

TRACKER CROSS-REFERENCES: NPS Preservation Programs Degraded (id 26) — NPS revenue from leases is part of the broader funding crisis.

GovTrack estimates 23% enactment probability.

SCOPE: 21 islands and 12 miles of mainland Lake Superior shoreline. Sand Island specifically designated as 'National Preserve' to ensure continued hunting access. Department of Interior administers hunting, trapping, and fishing in the Preserve with no permitting fees.

TRIBAL TREATY RIGHTS: Explicitly affirms all treaty rights and tribal sovereignty — nothing in the Act affects rights granted by treaty, statute, or executive order pertaining to any Tribe, including rights to hunt, trap, fish, and gather within the boundary.

LOCAL OPPOSITION: Red Cliff Band of Lake Superior Chippewa filed joint resolution opposing with Bayfield County Board of Supervisors. Ashland County Board also opposed. The tribal opposition is significant given the treaty rights provisions — the Red Cliff Band may view the redesignation as potentially complicating existing treaty-protected access despite the bill's affirmation language.

SUPPORT: Washburn County Board of Supervisors. Passed committee on near-party-line 20-17 vote. No Senate companion identified.

actions.title: S.1516 — Cahokia Mounds Mississippian Culture Study Act
actions.status: Ordered reported (with substitute amendment) by Senate Energy and Natural Resources Committee February 4, 2026; awaiting full Senate consideration

The bill directs the Secretary of the Interior to conduct a Special Resource Study (SRS) of three Mississippian culture sites in Illinois:

1. CAHOKIA MOUNDS STATE HISTORIC SITE, Collinsville, IL (St. Clair and Madison Counties) — a UNESCO World Heritage Site, National Historic Landmark, and Illinois State Historic Site. 3. PULCHER MOUNDS, Monroe and St. Clair Counties, IL

actions.title: H.R.504 — Miccosukee Reserved Area Amendments Act (House) — VETOED
actions.status: VETOED — Passed House by voice vote July 14, 2025; passed Senate by unanimous consent December 11, 2025; vetoed by President Trump December 29, 2025; House override failed January 8, 2026. BILL IS DEAD.
actions.description:
Introduced January 16, 2025, by Rep. Carlos Gimenez (R-FL-28) with 3 bipartisan cosponsors: Reps. Diaz-Balart (R-FL), Salazar (R-FL), Soto (D-FL). Passed BOTH chambers with bipartisan support (House voice vote, Senate unanimous consent) — then VETOED by President Trump on December 29, 2025. Override attempt failed in the House on January 8, 2026.

BILL PROVISIONS: Amends the Miccosukee Reserved Area Act (1998) to expand the Miccosukee Reserved Area within Everglades National Park to include Osceola Camp (Camp Osceola) — established in 1935 as a residential community for multiple Miccosukee families containing housing, water systems, and infrastructure. Section 3 required the Secretary of Interior, in consultation with the Miccosukee Tribe, to implement flood protection measures within two years. Flooding concerns stem from Everglades ecosystem restoration projects increasing water levels.

CBO estimated 'insignificant costs' over 2025-2030.

PRESIDENTIAL VETO RATIONALE (December 29, 2025):
1. Osceola Camp 'constructed in 1935, without authorization, in a low area raised with fill material' — never included in 1998 Reserved Area
2. Fiscal objection: 'not the Federal Government's responsibility to pay to fix problems in an area that the Tribe has never been authorized to occupy'
3. Political claim: Tribe had 'actively sought to obstruct reasonable immigration policies'
4. No structures meet National Register criteria

The veto message cited potential costs of up to $14 million for flood protection — contradicting CBO's 'insignificant costs' estimate.

SIGNIFICANCE: This was one of the FIRST VETOES of Trump's second term. A bipartisan bill that passed both chambers unanimously was vetoed with explicit reference to the Tribe's political opposition to administration immigration policies — establishing a precedent for punitive vetoes targeting tribal communities based on policy disagreements.
