targets.entity_name: National Museum of African American History and Culture
targets.threat_nature: DEI program elimination; budget cuts; programming restrictions; content censorship under 'divisive concepts' framework
targets.evidence_description:
EO 14151 affects Smithsonian museums; diversity office closed. CRITICAL GOVERNANCE CONTEXT: NMAAHC is a congressionally created entity (CRS IF12718) — it can only be fundamentally altered by Congress, not by executive order. The President is not a member of the Smithsonian Board of Regents and has no appointment or removal authority over the Secretary or museum directors. Executive orders purporting to restrict NMAAHC programming operate against the explicit will of Congress, which created this institution after a century-long campaign by African Americans for accurate national representation. NMAAHC's congressional authorization gives it the strongest legal protection available to a community-specific museum. The Smithsonian's statutory mission — 'the increase and diffusion of knowledge' (Smithson bequest, codified since 1846) — is the legal standard against which content restrictions must be measured. The Commission for Museum of African Art is also congressionally created — a complementary entity often overlooked in coverage of African-descendant cultural resources at the Smithsonian. LEGISLATIVE REVERSAL: The 118th Congress moved on four fronts for Black institutional expansion: African American History Act (H.R. 765/S. 233, expanding NMAAHC educational mandate), African Burial Ground Museum (H.R. 1349, NMAAHC-affiliated museum at NYC site of 400+ enslaved Black New Yorkers), Juneteenth Museum (H.R. 10190, feasibility study at historic Galveston site), and Jazz Preservation Act (H.R. 240, NMAH mandate — among Rep. Sheila Jackson Lee's final contributions before her death July 2024). All stalled. The 119th Congress's H.R. 3840/S. 2385 would permanently restrict NMAAHC content — the legislative arc moved from expansion to restriction within a single Congress. (CRS IF12719)

AFRO-DESCENDANT HEMISPHERIC CONTEXT: NMAAHC's mission encompasses the full African diaspora, not only U.S. domestic Black history. The UN estimates 200 million people of African descent live in the Americas — the Afro-descendant framework connects U.S. African American experience to Afro-Brazilian, Afro-Colombian, Afro-Cuban, Afro-Puerto Rican, Afro-Dominican, Garifuna, and Afro-Peruvian communities. The State Department's elimination of the 'National/Racial/Ethnic Minorities' section from Country Reports on Human Rights Practices (1993-2024) removes 31 years of federal documentation of Afro-descendant conditions across Latin America that informed the broader context NMAAHC was created to represent. The 2002 Durban Declaration — which the U.S. participated in — established that poverty and racism are mutually reinforcing for communities of African descent. NMAAHC's congressional creation positioned it as the institutional embodiment of U.S. acknowledgment of this reality; current restrictions on its programming contradict both its congressional mandate and the international anti-racism framework. (CRS IN11790)

CENTENNIAL CONTEXT AND STATUTORY FLOOR: NMAAHC faces maximum institutional attack in the centennial year of Black history commemoration — the 2026 ASALH theme is 'A Century of Black History Commemorations,' marking 100 years since Carter G. Woodson established Negro History Week in 1926 because Black history was being systematically erased. NMAAHC is one of seven federal agencies (with LOC, NARA, NEH, National Gallery of Art, NPS, USHMM) that jointly constitute the coordinated federal Black history preservation infrastructure — all seven simultaneously under attack. Black History Month itself is federally authorized by P.L. 99-244 (1986) — a congressional act no executive order can revoke. NMAAHC's congressional creation (the strongest legal protection for a community museum) parallels this statutory floor. The non-federal institutional backstop — ASALH (private, controls BHM theme), Moorland-Spingarn at Howard (world's premier Black archive, HBCU-dependent), Schomburg Center (11M items), and AAAMC at Indiana University — provides institutional resilience when federal institutions are compromised. Moorland-Spingarn's security depends partly on HBCU funding frozen at 2024 levels by H.R. 1. (CRS R44363)

MORRILL ACT THREE-GENERATION PARALLEL: NMAAHC's P.L. 108-184 statutory foundation connects to a broader pattern of inclusionary federal legislation being attacked simultaneously. The Second Morrill Act (1890) extended land-grant university status to HBCUs — the same 1890 institutions whose ED Title III Strengthening HBCU funding is threatened by H.R. 899 and ED workforce cuts. The 1994 Equity in Educational Land-Grant Status Act (P.L. 103-382) extended the same tradition to 29 tribal colleges — whose USDA programs ($34.880M) face DOGE-directed workforce reductions. NMAAHC (P.L. 108-184, 2003) represents the museum counterpart of this three-generation inclusionary arc: just as the Morrill Acts extended higher education access from white institutions (1862) to Black institutions (1890) to tribal institutions (1994), the Smithsonian's community museum tradition extended from general collections to NMAAHC (2003) to NMAL (authorized 2020). All three generations of inclusion — HBCUs, tribal colleges, and community museums — are under simultaneous attack from the same administration. (CRS IF10554)

BLACK MILITARY HISTORY PRESERVATION: The P.L. 108-184 IMLS grant program that supports 250+ African American museums and HBCUs ($6M FY2024) specifically funded the Buffalo Soldiers National Museum (Houston, TX) — preserving the military history of African American soldiers in post-Civil War frontier campaigns — with an FY2018 grant for exhibition technology, collections management, and educational programming. Black military service from the Revolutionary War through the Buffalo Soldiers, Tuskegee Airmen, and present day is simultaneously military history (eligible for NPS ABPP and Save America's Treasures grants), African American history (eligible for IMLS African American museum grants), and civil rights history (eligible for NPS Underrepresented Communities grants and Historic Preservation Fund African American Civil Rights grants). All of these funding pathways are simultaneously degraded: NPS lost 24% of staff, IMLS faces elimination, and Save America's Treasures — the only program jointly administered by NPS + IMLS + NEA + NEH — cannot function when all four agencies are degraded. The African American Veterans Monument (Buffalo, NY, 2022) and General 'Chappie' James statue (Pensacola, FL, 2021) — the first African American four-star general — were funded entirely by state/local/private sources because CRS confirms no federal grant programs fund military monuments. Black military commemoration has no federal funding backstop. (CRS R47337)

P.L. 108-184 COMMUNITY NETWORK: The National Museum of African American History and Culture Act (P.L. 108-184) authorized both NMAAHC and an IMLS grant program — Museum Grants for African American History & Culture ($6.0M in FY2024) — supporting more than 250 African American museums and HBCUs nationwide. EO 14253's political review of NMAAHC and the IMLS elimination proposal attack the museum and its community network simultaneously through a single statutory foundation. NMAAHC was designed as an anchor institution for a broader ecosystem of African American cultural preservation. The $6.0M IMLS program funded capacity-building, professional development, and collection access for the 250+ institutions that form this ecosystem — HBCUs with archive collections, community museums in Black neighborhoods, and cultural centers preserving local African American history. When both the anchor (NMAAHC content review) and the network ($6.0M elimination) are attacked through one law, the entire infrastructure of federal support for African American cultural heritage is under simultaneous assault. (CRS R48255)

MUSLIM RELIGIOUS HERITAGE COLLECTION: NMAAHC houses over 1,093 Islamic religious objects — Qurans, prayer beads, prayer garments, talismans, and amulets with Arabic text — making it simultaneously the primary federal museum for African American history AND a significant repository of Muslim religious and cultural heritage. Historians estimate 15-30% of enslaved Africans brought to North America were Muslim — many were literate in Arabic, some were Islamic scholars. Their religious practices, texts, and objects are inseparable from African American history. These 1,093+ Muslim religious objects are not a separate 'Islam section'; they are evidence of the African Muslim presence in American history from the earliest period of enslavement. EO 14253's political review of NMAAHC exhibitions therefore places Islamic religious artifacts within the scope of a content review conducted by an administration simultaneously implementing travel bans on 39 Muslim-majority countries, revoking Muslim student visas, and — as CRS R45003 documents (updated March 3, 2026, at the start of Ramadan 2026) — potentially breaking 25 years of bipartisan presidential Muslim holiday recognition. The convergence of anti-Black history policy and anti-Muslim policy meets at precisely these objects. For 4.5 million American Muslims (2020 U.S. Religion Census), including African American Muslims who are historically the largest Muslim population by ancestry in U.S. history, NMAAHC is a site of both racial and religious heritage under simultaneous attack. (CRS R45003)

targets.entity_name: National Museum of the American Indian
targets.threat_nature: DEI program elimination; consultation requirements weakened; tribal data infrastructure degradation; content censorship under 'divisive concepts' framework
targets.evidence_description: EO 14151 affects tribal consultation framed as 'equity.' CRITICAL GOVERNANCE CONTEXT: NMAI is a congressionally created entity — it carries the strongest legal protection of any community-specific museum in the Smithsonian system (CRS IF12718). Any executive order purporting to change its content interpretation of Indigenous history, colonization, boarding schools, or treaty violations operates against the specific will of Congress, which created NMAI for exactly that purpose. The President is not a member of the Board of Regents and has no authority over NMAI's content or leadership. When EO 14253 targets 'divisive' narratives about American history, the framing of colonial violence and forced removal as 'divisive' directly contradicts the congressional mandate under which NMAI was created. TRIBAL DATA CONTEXT: NMAI serves a community of 9,666,058 AI/AN-identifying people (2020 Census), of whom ~2 million are captured in BIA service population data and ~3.7 million identified as AI/AN alone. The museum's weakened consultation capacity compounds the tribal population data crisis: no biennial federal tribal population report has been published since 2013 despite congressional mandate (P.L. 115-93). NMAI's programming and community engagement functions help validate and preserve the cultural infrastructure through which tribal identity — and therefore tribal data — is maintained. (CRS IF12612, CRS IF12718)

targets.entity_name: National Museum of the American Latino (Planned)
targets.entity_type: Museum (Congressionally Created Smithsonian Entity — Authorized, Not Yet Built)
targets.threat_nature: Construction halted; funding eliminated in FY2026 budget; content restrictions imposed before museum opens
targets.evidence_description:
FY2026 budget request eliminates funding despite congressional authorization. CRITICAL GOVERNANCE CONTEXT: The National Museum of the American Latino is a congressionally created entity (CRS IF12718) — it exists in law but not yet in physical form. This creates a unique injustice: EO 14253's content restrictions and H.R. 3840/S. 2385's proposed statutory content constraints would be imposed on a museum that doesn't yet exist — meaning 65 million Latiné Americans would see their national museum born under political content restrictions before it opens. No other community-specific museum has faced content censorship before its first day of operation. As a congressionally created entity, it carries the strongest legal protection — the same Congress that authorized NMAL would need to restrict it. The Smithsonian's statutory mission of 'the increase and diffusion of knowledge' is the standard against which these pre-construction restrictions should be measured. SHAWL ACT GENEALOGY: The 'political viewpoint diversity' requirement in S. 1304/H.R. 1330 Section 2(c) was first introduced in H.R. 9274 (SHAWL Act, 118th Congress, August 2, 2024, Rep. Nicole Malliotakis — same lead House sponsor). The SHAWL Act conditioned NMAL siting on viewpoint diversity compliance with biennial Secretary reports on exhibit revisions. This mechanism was carried directly into the 119th Congress. The 19-year legislative fight for NMAL (first introduced 2003) is now being conditioned on political viewpoint compliance embedded in the very siting legislation needed to build the museum. This is a planned, sequential legislative strategy, not an ad hoc provision. (CRS IF12719)

AFRO-LATINO DIMENSION: NMAL represents 65 million Latiné Americans, a significant proportion of whom are Afro-Latino — Afro-Cuban, Afro-Puerto Rican, Afro-Dominican, Afro-Colombian, Garifuna, and other communities of African descent whose cultural identity is expressed through Latin American and Caribbean frameworks. The 'Afro-descendant' term (first adopted by regional organizations in the early 2000s) describes people united by common African ancestry living in widely varying conditions — a cross-community reality NMAL must represent. The State Department's elimination of the 'National/Racial/Ethnic Minorities' section from Country Reports (1993-2024) removes federal documentation of the conditions Afro-Latino communities' families face in their countries of origin. Content restrictions on NMAL that limit how it addresses race within Latiné communities would erase the Afro-Latino experience from the very institution Congress created to represent Latiné American diversity. World Bank data (June 2025) shows higher poverty rates among Afro-descendants in Brazil, Colombia, Ecuador, Peru, and Uruguay — the communities NMAL's diaspora narrative must include. (CRS IN11790)

INDIGENOUS LATINÉ DIMENSION: NMAL represents 65 million Latiné Americans, millions of whom are Indigenous — Maya, Mixtec, Zapotec, Nahua, Quechua, Aymara, Guaraní, Mapuche, and hundreds of other peoples whose cultural identity is both Indigenous and Latiné. CRS R46225 documents 57.7 million Indigenous peoples in Latin America (ECLAC 2022), comprising 826 distinct groups speaking 891 languages. Guatemala is 40-45% Indigenous; Mexico has 24-28 million Indigenous peoples; Bolivia is 48% Indigenous; Peru has 26% Indigenous population. These are not marginal populations — they are foundational to the cultures NMAL is congressionally mandated to represent. Content restrictions imposed before NMAL opens (EO 14253, H.R. 3840/S. 2385, the SHAWL Act genealogy documented in CRS IF12719) threaten to erase the Indigenous dimension of Latiné experience — the Mayan roots of Mexican and Central American culture, the Quechua and Aymara foundations of Andean identity, the Guaraní language that is co-official in Paraguay. CRS documents that Latin American Indigenous peoples face the world's largest wage gap (31.2%), 82.6% informal employment, and poverty rates 1.6-3.5x national averages. The Indigenous Latiné experience — migration driven by structural poverty that is specifically Indigenous in character, arrival in a country that classifies them as 'Hispanic' without recognizing Indigenous identity, community formation in diaspora — is a core narrative NMAL must tell. Pre-construction content restrictions that limit how the museum addresses systemic inequality would censor this story before it is ever told. (CRS R46225)

targets.threat_nature: Closure threatened in FY2026 budget; heightened governance vulnerability as Smithsonian-created entity
targets.evidence_description: FY2026 budget request eliminates funding for nation's first federally-funded Black community museum. GOVERNANCE VULNERABILITY: Unlike NMAAHC and NMAI, the Anacostia Community Museum is Smithsonian-established, not congressionally created (CRS IF12718). This means the Board of Regents has more discretion to modify or eliminate it than it does for congressionally authorized museums. Anacostia has less congressional protection than NMAAHC, making it more vulnerable to administrative pressure — the Board could theoretically close it without requiring an act of Congress. This governance distinction is critical: the institution most specifically serving urban African American communities in the DC area has the weakest structural protection of any community-serving Smithsonian museum.

targets.threat_nature: Only federal support program (Native American Library Services) terminated; tribal data infrastructure degraded
targets.evidence_description:
IMLS terminated Native American Library Services Basic Grants — the only federal program supporting tribal libraries. These libraries serve communities where BIA service population data covers ~2 million individuals across ~326 reservations, but systematically undercounts Alaska Natives and many Oklahoma tribes lacking reservation-based service areas. Tribal libraries are often the only community institutions helping tribal members navigate federal data and service systems — including the data infrastructure through which treaty funding obligations are calculated. Their defunding compounds a data crisis: no biennial tribal population report published since 2013; tribal service population data unavailable below the national level; and the Cherokee Nation example illustrates the stakes: ~450,000 enrolled members vs. 1.5M+ self-identified Cherokee — the choice of counting method determines funding levels. (CRS IF12612)

HOUSING-CULTURAL INFRASTRUCTURE LINK: Tribal libraries serve communities facing overcrowding and physically inadequate housing at rates 3-4x the national average. In overcrowded homes, libraries become essential spaces for cultural transmission — language learning, elder storytelling, ceremonial preparation — that housing conditions cannot support. NAHASDA's $1.261B (FY2024) funds housing development and services, but 180 grantees receive less than $200,000 and lack capacity to build adequate community spaces. Tribal libraries fill this gap as the physical infrastructure for cultural continuity when housing fails. Their defunding eliminates both the data navigation support (helping tribes access IHBG formula calculations and HUD technical assistance) and the cultural space that compensates for inadequate housing. The intersection is direct: housing crisis drives cultural programming to libraries; library defunding eliminates the compensating institution. (CRS IF12905)

NATIVE POSTSECONDARY EDUCATION NEXUS: Tribal libraries serve communities where postsecondary enrollment (60%) and degree completion (33%) are the lowest of all racial groups (2022 Census). The 32 tribally controlled colleges — many in the same rural Great Lakes, Plains, Southwest, and Alaska communities served by tribal libraries — depend on the same federal agencies being cut: DOI ($212.757M), ED ($92.050M), and USDA ($34.880M). Tribal libraries are often the only community institutions where prospective students can access information about TCU enrollment, DOI scholarship applications ($54.648M annual program including STEM doctoral funding), and financial aid processes. When BIA workforce cuts delay scholarship distribution and library defunding eliminates the institutional navigation support, the compound effect blocks the education pipeline at the community level. IAIA (Santa Fe, NM) — the only federally chartered institution dedicated to Native American arts and culture — trains the artists and cultural practitioners whose work tribal libraries display and preserve; IAIA's $13.482M DOI funding faces the same DOGE-directed cuts as tribal library programs. (CRS IF10554)

INDIGENOUS MILITARY HISTORY DOCUMENTATION: The Chickaloon Native Village (Alaska Native) received IMLS Native American/Native Hawaiian Museum Services funding (FY2020) for the Nay'dini'aa Na' Kayax Nahwgholnicde project — cataloging the military contributions of Indigenous peoples and the effects of military service on their communities. Indigenous peoples serve in the U.S. military at the highest per-capita rate of any demographic group. The $3.772M IMLS tribal museum program that funded this documentation was terminated by EO 14238, temporarily restored by court injunction (November 2025), and proposed for permanent elimination at $0 in FY2026 — while NARA's 100+ staff reductions simultaneously delay the military service records Indigenous veterans need for VA benefits. The NPS American Battlefield Protection Program (ABPP) — which includes tribal governments as eligible grantees for preservation of sites where U.S. forces fought tribal nations — is now operating with 24% fewer NPS staff. The VA Veterans Cemetery Grant program specifically authorizes grants to federally recognized tribes for establishing tribal veterans cemeteries — an expression of tribal sovereignty over burial practices — but NARA delays in processing military service records obstruct the proof of service required for cemetery eligibility. (CRS R47337)

FY2024 FEDERAL FUNDING BASELINE: IMLS's two dedicated tribal programs — Native American/Native Hawaiian Library Services ($5.763M in FY2024) and Native American/Native Hawaiian Museum Services ($3.772M) — together totaling $9.535M, represent the entire federal investment in tribal libraries and Native American museums. No other federal agency provides library-specific grants to the 574 federally recognized tribes and Hawaiian Native communities. The library program increased from $5.263M (FY2021-2022) to $5.763M (FY2023-2024); the museum program saw a 66% increase from $2.272M to $3.772M in FY2023. Both programs are proposed for elimination at $0 in FY2026. Additionally, tribal libraries receive proportional allocations from the $180M Library Grants to States formula — the largest federal library program — through their State Library Administrative Agencies. For territories with tribal populations, IMLS is often the only federal library funding source. The combined $9.535M represents a bipartisan congressional investment that increased 19.7% between FY2021 and FY2024, reflecting growing congressional recognition of tribal library and museum needs — now reversed entirely. IMLS is funded through the Labor, HHS, Education Appropriations Act, the same bill as NIH, CDC, and SNAP — meaning tribal library funding is subject to the same appropriations subcommittee dynamics as health and nutrition programs. (CRS R48255)

FOOD SECURITY NAVIGATION: Tribal libraries are often the only community institutions where tribal members can access information about food assistance programs — SNAP enrollment, FDPIR availability through their ITO, WIC eligibility (administered through 32 ITOs), OAA Title VI elder nutrition ($40M FY2026 serving 101,000 tribal elders), and Summer EBT. When H.R. 1 SNAP work requirements (effective December 31, 2026) cause mass disenrollment on reservations with 50%+ unemployment, tribal members will need to navigate the FDPIR/SNAP mutual exclusivity rule (households cannot receive both) and other program transitions. Library defunding eliminates the institutional capacity for this navigation at exactly the moment it becomes most critical. The food-housing-library triangle: inadequate housing drives cultural and service functions to libraries; food program complexity requires institutional navigation support; library defunding eliminates the institution connecting these crises. (CRS R42353)

targets.threat_nature: IMLS grants terminated; digitization projects halted; anti-Asian technology scapegoating climate
targets.evidence_description: Grant terminations affected archives preservation and digitization. Additionally, the national security framing of Chinese technology companies (CRS IF12473 documents DOD relisting of Hesai, 58% PRC LiDAR market share, PRC firm infiltration of U.S. research institutions) intensifies the environment of suspicion toward Chinese Americans — the same community whose history this institution preserves. The gap between targeted corporate threats and broad national-origin policy responses creates a scapegoating climate that compounds the institutional harm of grant terminations.

targets.threat_nature: IMLS digitization grants frozen; anti-Asian technology scapegoating climate
targets.evidence_description:
Grant terminations halted digitization of Japanese American incarceration records. The national security framing of Chinese/Asian technology competition (CRS IF12473 documents PRC LiDAR market dominance and DOD military company designations) intensifies suspicion toward Asian Americans broadly — echoing the very history of wartime scapegoating and incarceration that JANM's halted digitization project documents. The museum's mission to preserve the record of what happens when national security rhetoric overrides civil rights protections for Asian Americans is made more urgent precisely as its federal funding is terminated.

TWO CONGRESSIONAL STATUTORY MANDATES: Congress enacted two specific laws protecting Japanese American incarceration site preservation: P.L. 109-441 (2006) authorizes the NPS Japanese American Confinement Sites (JACS) grant program — specifically preserving the 10 War Relocation Authority incarceration camps (Gila River, Granada, Heart Mountain, Jerome, Manzanar, Minidoka, Poston, Rohwer, Topaz, Tule Lake) and other significant sites. P.L. 117-328 (2022) authorizes the Norman Y. Mineta Japanese American Confinement Education (JACE) grants — named for the Japanese American WWII incarceration survivor who became a bipartisan Cabinet secretary — funding educational projects at these sites. Active 2025 JACE grants include the Heart Mountain Wyoming Foundation's Nikkei Community Platform connecting living incarceration survivors with their descendants. Both statutory programs are administered by NPS, which has lost 24% of permanent staff. The compound documentation crisis — NEH canceling Japanese American incarceration oral histories, NPS workforce reductions degrading JACS/JACE administration, IMLS terminating JANM digitization grants — attacks the same historical record through three agencies simultaneously. JANM's mission intersects all three: it preserves the archival record (IMLS), the site history (JACS/JACE via NPS), and the oral history testimonies (NEH) of Japanese American incarceration. (CRS R47337)

targets.threat_nature: Tribal consultation requirements weakened as "equity"; tribal data infrastructure degraded
targets.evidence_description:
EO 14151 characterizes tribal consultation requirements as DEI and targets for elimination. NCAI represents 574 federally recognized tribes whose federal service allocations depend on population data infrastructure now in crisis: no biennial tribal population report published since 2013; BIA service population data (~2 million across ~326 reservations) not publicly available below the national level; tribal enrollment data subject to FOIA vulnerability; and BIA workforce cuts of ~20% eliminating staff who maintain data relationships with tribes. The weakening of tribal consultation as 'equity' removes the very mechanism through which tribes negotiate data methodology with federal agencies — compounding the structural disadvantage where better-resourced tribes receive more accurate counts and therefore more funding. OMB racial category definitions governing how 9.7 million AI/AN people are counted are now subject to DEI-driven rulemaking risk. (CRS IF12612)

NAHASDI REAUTHORIZATION GAP: NCAI's advocacy capacity is critical for NAHASDA reauthorization — now lapsed for 12+ years (authorization expired FY2013). Reauthorization bills were introduced in every Congress from the 113th through the 118th; none enacted. No bill has been introduced in the 119th Congress — the first since 2013 to open without one. NCAI coordinates tribal positions on the IHBG formula that distributes $1.261B (FY2024) across ~600 grantees, advocates for the 180 smallest grantees receiving under $200,000, and pushes for Title VI Loan Guarantee improvements (only 2 loans/$8M guaranteed in FY2023 against $787M in formula grants). The weakening of tribal consultation as DEI directly undermines NCAI's capacity to advocate for reauthorization — the very legislative mechanism that would strengthen the program serving tribes facing housing inadequacy at 3-4x national rates. Without consultation, tribes cannot effectively negotiate formula methodology changes that determine whether Cherokee Nation receives funding based on 450,000 enrolled members or 1.5M+ self-identified Cherokee. (CRS IF12905)

NATIVE POSTSECONDARY EDUCATION ADVOCACY: NCAI coordinates tribal positions on the $339.687M three-agency Native postsecondary education system — DOI ($212.757M), ED ($92.050M), and USDA ($34.880M) — now facing simultaneous workforce reductions across all three agencies. AIAN students have the lowest postsecondary enrollment (60%) and near-lowest degree completion (33%) of all groups (2022 Census). NCAI advocates for: Haskell/SIPI ($30.325M combined, both tuition-free, both facing 25%+ BIE staff cuts); IAIA ($13.482M, president-appointed Board, only federally chartered institution dedicated to Native arts); 32 tribally controlled colleges (20,000+ students); DOI Scholarships ($54.648M, including STEM doctoral funding via Science Post-Graduate Scholarship); and USDA 1994 Land-Grant programs ($34.880M, tribal farmer education and food sovereignty). The weakening of tribal consultation as 'DEI' undermines NCAI's capacity to advocate for these programs — and H.R. 899 (Department of Education Termination Act) threatens the $92.050M ED-administered portion that includes Strengthening TCCUs ($80.097M, HEA Title III — same authority as Strengthening HBCUs and HSIs). The Cobell Education Scholarship Fund (P.L. 111-291, $60M DOI contribution) — created as restitution for the $3.4B trust mismanagement settlement led by Elouise Cobell (Blackfeet Nation) — continues as an endowment while current BIE/BIA cuts perpetuate the trust violation pattern that produced the Cobell litigation. (CRS IF10554)

FOOD SECURITY ADVOCACY: NCAI's advocacy extends to four simultaneous tribal food security crises: (1) FDPIR reauthorization — the $239M tribal food program serving 58,000 households through 107 ITOs is mutually exclusive with SNAP, and H.R. 1 work requirements (effective December 31, 2026) will cause mass SNAP disenrollment on reservations FDPIR cannot absorb; (2) WIC ITO capacity — 32 Indian Tribal Organizations administer WIC but face degraded federal support from HHS workforce cuts; (3) OAA Title VI — $40M in tribal elder nutrition (101,000 participants) with expired authorization (FY2024), paralleling NAHASDA; (4) food insecurity data termination — USDA's 30-year survey ended fall 2025, eliminating the only federal mechanism to measure food insecurity in tribal communities as SNAP cuts take effect. NCAI's consultation capacity is the mechanism through which tribes would advocate for FDPIR expansion, WIC ITO support, OAA reauthorization, and food data restoration — all weakened when consultation is characterized as DEI. (CRS R42353)

targets.entity_name: John F. Kennedy Center for the Performing Arts
targets.entity_type: Performing Arts Center (Congressionally Created Smithsonian Bureau)
targets.threat_nature: Board purged Feb 2025; DEI/Social Impact programming eliminated; renamed Dec 2025; artist boycott threatening 85% of revenue; Beatty v. Trump litigation pending
targets.evidence_description:
The Kennedy Center is a bureau of the Smithsonian Institution — a trust instrumentality organizationally separate from all three branches of government, established by Congress and redesignated in 1964 as a living memorial to President Kennedy (20 U.S.C. § 76i). It is largely autonomous from the Smithsonian Board of Regents with separate budget authority. Trust fund decisions are reviewable only by courts.

GOVERNANCE CRISIS: On February 7, 2025, 36 general trustees were replaced — unprecedented in the Center's history. The statute is silent on removal except for term expiration. CRS flagged that the Smithsonian's nongovernmental status 'may have implications' for trustee changes 'not authorized in statute,' and questioned presidential removal authority over a Smithsonian component when the President lacks it over the broader enterprise (CRS IF12911). Statutory board members include Cabinet secretaries (HHS, State, Education), the Librarian of Congress (vacancy after Hayden firing May 8, 2025), NPS Director (24% staff loss), Smithsonian Secretary Lonnie Bunch, and congressional leaders — creating cascading governance conflicts across multiple tracker entries.

FINANCIAL ARCHITECTURE: Federal appropriations $45.38M (FY2023) fund facility maintenance only — ~15% of revenue. Earned revenue from performances, contributions, and commercial activity generates ~$266M (~85%). The artist boycott (26+ cancelled performances, Nutcracker sales -33%, Hamilton 2026 run lost, major donor withdrawals) threatens the primary financial engine. The administration's financial leverage is limited to ~15%; 85% is beyond executive reach through normal funding channels.

COMMUNITY SCALE: 2 million annual visitors; 1.6 million ticket buyers; 2,200+ performances/exhibits; 400+ free performances annually (daily Millennium Stage). Home of NSO and Washington National Opera. The 400+ free performances are the primary equity access mechanism for low-income communities and DC residents.

RENAMING: December 18, 2025 Board vote renamed facility to include Trump's name. Congress designated the memorial name in 1964 — arguably a congressional prerogative, not a Board decision. Beatty v. Trump challenges the renaming as beyond statutory authority. (CRS IF12911)
targets.current_status: Active — Multiple crises (board purge, programming elimination, renaming litigation, artist boycott)

litigation.case_name: Rhode Island Latino Arts v. NEA
litigation.outcome: NEA gender ideology certification requirement struck down Sept 19, 2025
International student ban enjoined pending trial
Court ruled grant terminations violated APA

litigation.defendants: ["Donald J. Trump", "Kennedy Center Board of Trustees"]
litigation.legal_mechanism: 20 U.S.C. § 76i designates the Kennedy Center as a living memorial to President Kennedy, authorized by Congress in 1964. The Board of Trustees' December 18, 2025 vote to rename the facility to include Trump's name is challenged as beyond the Board's statutory authority — Congress designated the memorial name, not the Board. Rep. Beatty, an ex officio trustee, was muted during the vote and prevented from voicing opposition. The Kennedy Center's own statute grants the Board discretion over performing arts programming and trust fund management, but the name and memorial designation are congressional acts, not Board prerogatives. Congress's appropriation of $45.38M annually specifically maintains Kennedy Center 'facilities as a federal memorial' — a congressional characterization the renaming arguably contradicts (CRS IF12911).
